title: 3D Siamese Voxel-to-BEV Tracker for Sparse Point Clouds

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for object tracking in sparse 3D point clouds. The main contributions are two folds: It introduces a shape-aware feature learning network to enhance the discrimination of targets in the search area; It develops a voxel-to-BEV target localization network to better localize 3D objects. The proposed method achieves the state-of-the-art performance compared with previous arts and ablation studies demonstrate the efficacy of various designs.
reviews.main_review:
The submission is addressing an important problem, namely object tracking in 3D point clouds. The submission is clearly written, making it easy to follow. The content is also well organized with enough technical details provided. Generally speaking, the submission is technical sound. The motivation behind various designs is clear and the experimental results partially support some of the claims.

However, there are also claims not fully justified experimentally. For example, the submission claims it is better at handling sparse point clouds compared with previous methods including SC3D and P2B. If we compare Table 1 and Table 2, when switching from all types of objects to sparse objects, only the proposed method suffers from a performance drop while all the other two baseline methods actually get better performance. Therefore, it is not clear whether the claimed shape aware feature really makes the proposed method more suitable for sparse point clouds. Another example is that there's no ablation studies justifying the necessity of using both global and local features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module.

The method is quite new and its difference from previous works are also clearly discussed. The related work is adequate as far as I can tell. The overall experimental results are quite impressive, which outperforms previous methods by a large margin.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: As I mentioned previously, some of the claims are not fully justified (better at handling sparse points, necessary to use both local and global features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module). Also the ablation study only considers the car category, which is not really representative enough. All four categories should be considered to really making the claimed contributions convincing enough. I hope authors could address this issue during rebuttal. Some claims sound awkward to me, such as line 378-380 (stable does not mean accurate.) The paper should point out that this proposed method is not suitable for indoor scenes where a BEV map might not be sufficient for object proposal.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

Summary:
This paper proposes a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV tracker for object tracking in sparse 3D point clouds. The main contributions are two folds: It introduces a shape-aware feature learning network to enhance the discrimination of targets in the search area; It develops a voxel-to-BEV target localization network to better localize 3D objects. The proposed method achieves the state-of-the-art performance compared with previous arts and ablation studies demonstrate the efficacy of various designs.

Main Review:
The submission is addressing an important problem, namely object tracking in 3D point clouds. The submission is clearly written, making it easy to follow. The content is also well organized with enough technical details provided. Generally speaking, the submission is technical sound. The motivation behind various designs is clear and the experimental results partially support some of the claims.

However, there are also claims not fully justified experimentally. For example, the submission claims it is better at handling sparse point clouds compared with previous methods including SC3D and P2B. If we compare Table 1 and Table 2, when switching from all types of objects to sparse objects, only the proposed method suffers from a performance drop while all the other two baseline methods actually get better performance. Therefore, it is not clear whether the claimed shape aware feature really makes the proposed method more suitable for sparse point clouds. Another example is that there's no ablation studies justifying the necessity of using both global and local features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module.

The method is quite new and its difference from previous works are also clearly discussed. The related work is adequate as far as I can tell. The overall experimental results are quite impressive, which outperforms previous methods by a large margin.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
As I mentioned previously, some of the claims are not fully justified (better at handling sparse points, necessary to use both local and global features in the template feature embedding module and in the shape-aware feature learning module). Also the ablation study only considers the car category, which is not really representative enough. All four categories should be considered to really making the claimed contributions convincing enough. I hope authors could address this issue during rebuttal. Some claims sound awkward to me, such as line 378-380 (stable does not mean accurate.) The paper should point out that this proposed method is not suitable for indoor scenes where a BEV map might not be sufficient for object proposal.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review

## Summary

This paper proposes a novel Siamese voxel-to-BEV (V2B) tracker for 3D single object tracking in sparse point clouds. The work makes two primary technical contributions: (1) a Siamese shape-aware feature learning network that enhances the discrimination of target objects in search areas through dense geometric feature generation, and (2) a voxel-to-BEV target localization network that enables more accurate 3D center localization by leveraging dense 2D bird's eye view representations rather than sparse 3D space. The proposed method achieves state-of-the-art performance on both KITTI and nuScenes datasets, with comprehensive ablation studies demonstrating the efficacy of various design choices.

## Main Review

### Strengths

The submission addresses an important and timely problem in 3D computer vision—object tracking in sparse LiDAR point clouds—which is highly relevant for autonomous driving and robotics applications. The motivation is well-established through statistical analysis (Figure 1), showing that 51% of cars in the KITTI dataset contain fewer than 100 points, thereby clearly justifying the focus on sparse point cloud scenarios.

The paper is clearly written and well-organized, making it accessible to readers. The technical content is presented with sufficient detail, including explicit formulations for the template feature embedding (Equations 1-4), shape-aware feature learning (Equations 5-6), and target localization components (Equations 7-9). The architecture diagrams (Figures 2 and 3) effectively communicate the overall framework and individual module designs.

The technical approach is generally sound. The two-pronged strategy—enhancing shape information through dense point cloud generation while simultaneously avoiding low-quality proposal generation—is intuitive and well-motivated. The use of template feature embedding to build correlations between template and search regions (both globally via max-pooling and locally via cosine similarity) represents a reasonable approach to target discrimination. The voxel-to-BEV projection strategy is a clever way to convert sparse 3D information into a denser 2D representation suitable for regression.

The experimental results are impressive, demonstrating substantial improvements over prior work. On the KITTI car category, the method improves Success from 60.5% (BAT) to 70.5%, representing a 10 percentage point gain. Similar improvements are observed across other object categories (pedestrian, van, cyclist) and on the nuScenes dataset, indicating good generalization capability. The visualization results in Figure 4 provide convincing qualitative evidence of tracking improvements in both sparse and dense scenarios.

The ablation studies provide useful insights into component contributions. Table 3 demonstrates that removing template feature embedding causes a substantial performance drop from 70.5/81.3 to 63.9/73.9, validating its importance. Similarly, the shape-aware feature learning module contributes approximately 2.9 percentage points in Success when removed. The comparison with VoteNet-based detection (Table 4) effectively supports the claim that anchor-free localization is more suitable for sparse point clouds.

### Major Concerns

**Claim-Evidence Mismatch Regarding Sparse Point Cloud Handling**: The paper claims superior performance specifically for sparse point clouds, yet the experimental evidence does not fully support this assertion. When comparing Table 1 (all frames) and Table 2 (sparse frames), an anomalous pattern emerges: while the proposed V2B method shows a performance drop from 70.5/81.3 to 64.7/77.4 on sparse car scenes, the baseline methods SC3D and P2B actually demonstrate *improved* performance when restricted to sparse scenarios. Specifically, P2B achieves 56.2/72.8 on all frames but 56.0/70.6 on sparse frames (essentially unchanged), while SC3D shows 41.3/57.9 on all frames but 37.9/53.0 on sparse frames (a decrease, but less dramatic than the gap between methods). This counterintuitive result raises questions about whether the shape-aware feature learning module truly provides the claimed advantage for sparse point clouds, or whether the method's overall superiority stems from other factors that apply broadly across density conditions. The authors should provide deeper analysis of why performance drops more substantially for their method in sparse scenarios compared to baselines, and clarify whether the claimed sparse-point-specific benefits are actually supported by the data.

**Incomplete Ablation Studies**: While Table 3 provides ablation results for the car category, the scope is insufficient to validate all claimed contributions convincingly. The paper argues for the necessity of both global and local features in two separate modules (template feature embedding and shape-aware feature learning), but the ablation only shows results for the car category. Given that different object categories (pedestrians, vans, cyclists) have distinct geometric properties and sparsity characteristics, the contribution of individual components may vary significantly across categories. The ablation studies should be expanded to include at least pedestrian and van categories to demonstrate that the design choices are robust across diverse object types. For instance, pedestrian tracking might benefit differently from shape completion compared to vehicle tracking, yet this is not explored.

### Minor Concerns

**Limited Justification for Design Choices**: Several design decisions lack thorough experimental justification. For example, while Table 3 shows results with and without shape information, it does not clearly demonstrate why *both* global and local shape features are necessary. The table shows "local geometric" (68.6/79.3) and "global shape" (69.6/80.3) individually, but these results are not directly comparable to the full model (70.5/81.3) because they represent different experimental conditions. A clearer ablation would systematically compare: (a) no shape information, (b) global shape only, (c) local geometric only, and (d) both global and local, all within the same experimental setup. Similarly, for template feature embedding, the individual contributions of global temple embedding (68.8/80.0) versus local temple embedding (68.0/79.2) suggest diminishing returns when combined, but this is not explicitly discussed.

**Limited Scope of Sparse Scenario Definition**: The definition of "sparse scenes" in Table 2 uses fixed point thresholds (≤150 for cars, ≤100 for pedestrians, ≤150 for vans, ≤100 for cyclists). These thresholds appear somewhat arbitrary and are not justified. For instance, why should cars and vans use the same threshold (150 points) despite different physical sizes? A more principled approach would define sparsity relative to object size or surface area, or provide sensitivity analysis showing how results vary with different threshold choices. Additionally, the cyclist category has only 59 frames meeting the sparse criteria, which is a very small sample size for drawing reliable conclusions about method performance.

**Claim Regarding Stability**: The discussion of template generation schemes (Table 5) claims that V2B "can yield stable results on four template generation schemes." However, "stability" (small variance across conditions) does not necessarily imply accuracy. While V2B does show consistent performance across different template schemes, this observation alone does not validate the method's superiority. All methods show some variation across schemes, and the key advantage of V2B is that its *absolute performance* is higher, not necessarily that it is more stable. The terminology should be clarified to distinguish between stability and accuracy.

**Incomplete Analysis of BEV Limitations**: While the method leverages BEV representations effectively for autonomous driving scenarios, the paper does not discuss limitations of this approach. BEV representations inherently lose height information through max-pooling, which could be problematic for objects with significant vertical extent or in scenarios where precise z-axis localization is critical. Although the method includes a separate z-axis head (Equation 9), the interaction between the BEV-based 2D localization and the z-axis regression could be better analyzed. Additionally, the method may not generalize well to indoor environments or non-planar ground surfaces where BEV representations are less natural or informative.

### Technical Soundness

The technical approach is generally sound, though some aspects could be strengthened. The use of Chamfer distance (Equation 6) for shape generation is appropriate and well-established in 3D reconstruction literature. The focal loss formulation (Equation 7) with modified exponents (α=2, β=4) is reasonable, though the choice of these specific values is not justified. The gate mechanism (Equation 5) for feature enhancement is simple but effective, though alternatives (e.g., attention mechanisms) are not compared.

One technical detail that warrants clarification: the feature expansion operation mentioned in Section 3.1.2 is referenced to [79] but not explicitly defined. The paper states that features are expanded "from M × C to 2048 × C," but the mechanism for this upsampling (nearest neighbor, interpolation, learned expansion, etc.) is not clearly specified. This is important for reproducibility.

### Experimental Evaluation

The experimental evaluation is comprehensive in breadth but has some limitations in depth. The comparison with three baselines (SC3D, P2B, BAT) is reasonable

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a 3D object tracker that localizes the target object given its template and a small search space. Their proposed model largely consists of the following two modules: Siamese Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network and Voxel-to-BEV target localization. The feature learning network takes the template point clouds and the search space point clouds as input and generates a feature embedding for the search space, focusing on its correlation to the template point clouds and its ability to generate dense point clouds. reviews.main_review:
Overall, while this paper outperforms the preceding works in terms of performance and computational cost, I have some concerns about the writing quality, novelty, and ablation study.

On the positive side, the proposed method clearly outperforms the preceding state-of-the-art methods. This paper has a proper baseline with improved performance, partial ablation study to support the usefulness of the proposed components, and thorough computational cost analysis for better real-world usefulness. I find it interesting that a point cloud generation task used only during the training phase helps the expressiveness of the point cloud features used for localization.

However, I have a few concerns about this paper:

First, the quality of the writing is poor. There are too many obvious grammatical errors and unpolished sentences. I believe this paper must be thoroughly proofread.

Second, the proposed Template Feature Embedding Networks is a huge complicated module with heavy engineering of features without detailed ablation study in itself. I do see a motivation behind the details of the network design however they are not properly backed by detailed ablation compared to its complexity. Especially considering the venue of the conference, I find it difficult to appreciate heavy engineering without proper theoretical study. For example, ablation of global / local features and different distance functions for correlation and similarity map may be useful.

Third, the Voxel-to-BEV target localization network seems to be heavily inspired by numerous preceding works in 3D object detection and I find it difficult to agree that this is a novel component. This paper must cite papers such as PointPillar and SECOND, which are commonly used 3D object detection encoder that look exactly the same as the proposed module.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: I do not see a thorough analysis of the limitation or societal impact of this work. There is a limited study of failure cases in the supplementary material however as a tracking problem, I believe this approach has numerous limitations such as re-id and tracklet initialization. A couple sentences of a societal impact of this work seems to be expected by the conference as well.

The search space feature is then fed into Voxel-to-BEV target localization networks which flattens the 3D point clouds into BEV 2D image where the target localization happens. The proposed method outperforms the preceding works by a noticeable margin.

Main Review:
Overall, while this paper outperforms the preceding works in terms of performance and computational cost, I have some concerns about the writing quality, novelty, and ablation study.

On the positive side, the proposed method clearly outperforms the preceding state-of-the-art methods. This paper has a proper baseline with improved performance, partial ablation study to support the usefulness of the proposed components, and thorough computational cost analysis for better real-world usefulness. I find it interesting that a point cloud generation task used only during the training phase helps the expressiveness of the point cloud features used for localization.

However, I have a few concerns about this paper:

First, the quality of the writing is poor. There are too many obvious grammatical errors and unpolished sentences. I believe this paper must be thoroughly proofread.

Second, the proposed Template Feature Embedding Networks is a huge complicated module with heavy engineering of features without detailed ablation study in itself. I do see a motivation behind the details of the network design however they are not properly backed by detailed ablation compared to its complexity. Especially considering the venue of the conference, I find it difficult to appreciate heavy engineering without proper theoretical study. For example, ablation of global / local features and different distance functions for correlation and similarity map may be useful.

Third, the Voxel-to-BEV target localization network seems to be heavily inspired by numerous preceding works in 3D object detection and I find it difficult to agree that this is a novel component. This paper must cite papers such as PointPillar and SECOND, which are commonly used 3D object detection encoder that look exactly the same as the proposed module.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I do not see a thorough analysis of the limitation or societal impact of this work. There is a limited study of failure cases in the supplementary material however as a tracking problem, I believe this approach has numerous limitations such as re-id and tracklet initialization. A couple sentences of a societal impact of this work seems to be expected by the conference as well.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review: 3D Siamese Voxel-to-BEV Tracker for Sparse Point Clouds

## Summary

This paper proposes a 3D object tracker designed to localize target objects given their template and a small search space from sparse point clouds. The proposed model consists of two primary modules: a Siamese Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network and a Voxel-to-BEV target localization network. The feature learning network takes template point clouds and search space point clouds as input and generates feature embeddings for the search space, focusing on its correlation to the template point clouds and its ability to generate dense point clouds. The search space feature is then fed into the Voxel-to-BEV target localization networks, which flattens the 3D point clouds into a bird's eye view (BEV) 2D representation where target localization occurs. The proposed method outperforms preceding works by a noticeable margin on both KITTI and nuScenes datasets.

## Main Review

### Strengths

Overall, while this paper outperforms preceding works in terms of both performance and computational cost, there are notable strengths that merit acknowledgment alongside several significant concerns regarding writing quality, novelty, and the depth of ablation studies.

On the positive side, the proposed method clearly demonstrates substantial improvements over state-of-the-art baselines. The paper presents proper experimental baselines with improved performance metrics, includes partial ablation studies to support the usefulness of proposed components, and provides thorough computational cost analysis that enhances real-world applicability. The quantitative results are impressive—for instance, on the KITTI dataset's car category, the method improves Success from 60.5% (BAT) to 70.5% (V2B), representing a meaningful advancement in the field. The evaluation on sparse scenarios (Table 2) is particularly valuable, demonstrating that the method achieves 64.7% / 77.4% Success/Precision for cars in sparse settings compared to 56.9% / 72.0% for VoteNet-based approaches.

I find it particularly interesting that a point cloud generation task, employed only during the training phase, enhances the expressiveness of the point cloud features used for localization. The shape-aware feature learning module that generates dense 3D point clouds (2048 points) from sparse search regions is an intuitive design choice. The observation that this auxiliary task does not increase inference time is also pragmatic and appreciated. Furthermore, the generalization results on nuScenes (Table 1) demonstrate that models trained on KITTI categories can transfer reasonably well to corresponding nuScenes categories, suggesting some degree of robustness across datasets.

The paper's motivation for addressing sparse point clouds is well-grounded in empirical observation. Figure 1 effectively demonstrates that 51% of cars in KITTI have fewer than 100 points, providing clear justification for the focus on sparse scenarios. The visualization results in Figure 4 compellingly show the method's effectiveness in both sparse and dense scenes compared to P2B.

### Major Concerns

However, I have several significant concerns about this paper that must be addressed:

#### 1. Writing Quality and Presentation

The quality of the writing is poor and requires substantial revision. There are numerous obvious grammatical errors and unpolished sentences throughout the manuscript that detract from its technical content. For example, the abstract states "3D object tracking in point clouds is still a challenging problem due to the sparsity of LiDAR points in dynamic environments," which could be more precisely written. The paper contains awkward phrasing such as "our intuition consists of two folds" (Section 1) when "our approach is motivated by two key observations" would be clearer. Additionally, inconsistent terminology and notation make certain sections difficult to follow. The paper must be thoroughly proofread by a native English speaker before resubmission. Beyond grammatical issues, the organization could be improved—for instance, the relationship between different loss terms (Equation 6-9) and their relative importance (λ₁=10⁻⁶, λ₂=1.0, λ₃=2.0) deserves more discussion in the methodology rather than appearing only in the experimental section.

#### 2. Insufficient Ablation Study for Template Feature Embedding

The Template Feature Embedding Network is an extremely complicated module with extensive feature engineering, yet it lacks detailed ablation study commensurate with its complexity. While Table 3 provides some ablation results showing that removing template feature embedding reduces performance from 70.5/81.3 to 63.9/73.9, this binary ablation is insufficient. The module itself contains numerous design choices that are not properly justified:

- **Global vs. Local Feature Embedding**: The paper proposes both template global feature embedding (Equation 2) and template local feature embedding (Equation 4), but provides limited justification for why both are necessary. Table 3 shows results with only local or only global features (68.6/79.3 and 68.8/80.0 respectively), indicating marginal differences from the full approach (70.5/81.3). This raises questions about whether the added complexity is justified.

- **Distance Function Choice**: Equation 1 uses MLP(pᵢ - qⱼ) to compute correlation weights, while Equation 3 uses cosine distance for similarity. Why are two different distance metrics employed? An ablation comparing different correlation functions (e.g., dot product, L2 distance, cosine similarity) would strengthen the design choices.

- **Feature Concatenation Strategy**: The final concatenation of global and local features (fⱼ = MLP[q'ⱼ, q''ⱼ]) is straightforward, but alternative fusion strategies (e.g., addition, gating mechanisms) are not explored.

- **Max Pooling vs. Other Aggregation**: Equation 2 uses max pooling to aggregate template information. How does this compare to mean pooling or attention-based aggregation? This choice significantly impacts the learned representations but is not ablated.

Given the venue (NeurIPS), the paper would benefit from deeper theoretical or empirical analysis of these design choices. The heavy engineering without proper justification makes it difficult to assess whether the improvements come from the core insights or from careful hyperparameter tuning and feature engineering.

#### 3. Limited Novelty of Voxel-to-BEV Target Localization

The Voxel-to-BEV target localization network appears to be heavily inspired by numerous preceding works in 3D object detection, and it is difficult to agree that this component represents a novel contribution. The paper must cite and differentiate from papers such as PointPillars (Lang et al., 2019) and SECOND (Yan et al., 2018), which employ virtually identical architectures for converting 3D voxel representations to BEV feature maps through max-pooling along the z-axis.

The specific architectural details presented in Section 3.2.1—converting point features to volumetric representations through voxelization, averaging features within voxel bins, applying 3D convolutions, and then performing max-pooling along the z-axis to obtain a dense BEV feature map—are standard operations in modern 3D object detection pipelines. PointPillars, for instance, uses precisely this approach to convert point clouds to pillar representations. While the application to tracking is somewhat novel, the core technical contribution is incremental.

Furthermore, the target localization in BEV (Section 3.2.2) with its three heads (2D-center, offset & rotation, z-axis) closely follows existing 3D detection frameworks. The use of Focal loss (Equation 7) and the center parameterization scheme are borrowed directly from existing work [19] (AFDet), as the authors acknowledge. The paper should more clearly delineate what aspects are novel versus what are adaptations of existing techniques. A more thorough comparison with related 3D detection methods would help establish the actual novelty contribution.

The claim that this approach avoids "low-quality proposals" compared to VoteNet-based methods (P2B, BAT) is valid, but the underlying principle of using dense BEV representations for improved detection is not new. The paper would be strengthened by either: (a) providing deeper insights into why this approach works better for tracking specifically, or (b) proposing more novel modifications to the standard BEV detection pipeline.

### Minor Concerns

#### 4. Incomplete Ablation of Shape-Aware Feature Learning

While Table 3 provides ablation results for shape information (removing it reduces performance from 70.5/81.3 to 67.6/78.2), the ablation could be more comprehensive. Specifically:

- The gate mechanism (Equation 5) is introduced to enhance target features and suppress background, but its necessity is not ablated. How much does performance degrade without this gating?
- The choice of EdgeConv for capturing local geometric information is not justified. Why not use other graph-based methods or attention mechanisms?
- The fixed number of generate

Summary:
This paper proposes a 3D object tracker that localizes the target object given its template and a small search space. Their proposed model largely consists of the following two modules: Siamese Shape-Aware Feature Learning Network and Voxel-to-BEV target localization. The feature learning network takes the template point clouds and the search space point clouds as input and generates a feature embedding for the search space, focusing on its correlation to the template point clouds and its ability to generate dense point clouds. The search space feature is then fed into Voxel-to-BEV target localization networks which flattens the 3D point clouds into BEV 2D image where the target localization happens. The proposed method outperforms the preceding works by a noticeable margin.

Main Review:
Overall, while this paper outperforms the preceding works in terms of performance and computational cost, I have some concerns about the writing quality, novelty, and ablation study.

On the positive side, the proposed method clearly outperforms the preceding state-of-the-art methods. This paper has a proper baseline with improved performance, partial ablation study to support the usefulness of the proposed components, and thorough computational cost analysis for better real-world usefulness. I find it interesting that a point cloud generation task used only during the training phase helps the expressiveness of the point cloud features used for localization.

However, I have a few concerns about this paper:

First, the quality of the writing is poor. There are too many obvious grammatical errors and unpolished sentences. I believe this paper must be thoroughly proofread.

Second, the proposed Template Feature Embedding Networks is a huge complicated module with heavy engineering of features without detailed ablation study in itself. I do see a motivation behind the details of the network design however they are not properly backed by detailed ablation compared to its complexity. Especially considering the venue of the conference, I find it difficult to appreciate heavy engineering without proper theoretical study. For example, ablation of global / local features and different distance functions for correlation and similarity map may be useful.

Third, the Voxel-to-BEV target localization network seems to be heavily inspired by numerous preceding works in 3D object detection and I find it difficult to agree that this is a novel component. This paper must cite papers such as PointPillar and SECOND, which are commonly used 3D object detection encoder that look exactly the same as the proposed module.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I do not see a thorough analysis of the limitation or societal impact of this work. There is a limited study of failure cases in the supplementary material however as a tracking problem, I believe this approach has numerous limitations such as re-id and tracklet initialization. A couple sentences of a societal impact of this work seems to be expected by the conference as well.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper introduced a siamese voxel2BEV object tracker for autonomous driving applications. The proposed network architecture is robust to sparse point cloud inputs, and can improve tracking performance significantly. Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks suggest that the proposed approach is highly effective.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
- The paper tackles a challenging task in autonomous driving, and the overall design of the algorithm is a sensible solution for this task.
- Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks are great. The method surpasses existing solutions by a fairly big margin.
- The presentation of the paper is relatively clear, and the visualizations in the paper are helpful for readers to grasp the intuitions of the design. 
- Regardless, I hope the authors release the code as they promised in the supplementary material, especially since this is an application paper, and would be useful for future research projects.

Weakness:
- The worse performance on the cyclist category seems to suggest that there are limitations in the approach. The authors argue that this is due to the lack of training data.  Suppose there are not sufficient data in other categories, would the trend of the result still remain the same?
- Since this is an object tracking paper, it'll be helpful if authors can include videos that demonstrate the tracking outputs. Visualizations in the supplementary material are helpful but I think videos are more intuitive.
- Minor: is the color scheme in Fig. 3 mean anything? When I first saw this diagram, I tried mapping similar colors to certain sections but eventually failed.
- Minor: The paper is only tested on the KITTI dataset, and there are certain places in the whole pipeline that requires parameter tunings (such as coefficients for loss functions). What is the general guidance for parameter tuning when presented with new datasets? How do authors envision the proposed algorithm generalizes to new datasets or real autonomous driving scenarios?

Summary:
This paper introduced a siamese voxel2BEV object tracker for autonomous driving applications. The proposed network architecture is robust to sparse point cloud inputs, and can improve tracking performance significantly. Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks suggest that the proposed approach is highly effective.

Main Review:
Strength:
- The paper tackles a challenging task in autonomous driving, and the overall design of the algorithm is a sensible solution for this task.
- Experimental results on KITTI benchmarks are great. The method surpasses existing solutions by a fairly big margin.
- The presentation of the paper is relatively clear, and the visualizations in the paper are helpful for readers to grasp the intuitions of the design. 
- Regardless, I hope the authors release the code as they promised in the supplementary material, especially since this is an application paper, and would be useful for future research projects.

Weakness:
- The worse performance on the cyclist category seems to suggest that there are limitations in the approach. The authors argue that this is due to the lack of training data.  Suppose there are not sufficient data in other categories, would the trend of the result still remain the same?
- Since this is an object tracking paper, it'll be helpful if authors can include videos that demonstrate the tracking outputs. Visualizations in the supplementary material are helpful but I think videos are more intuitive.
- Minor: is the color scheme in Fig. 3 mean anything? When I first saw this diagram, I tried mapping similar colors to certain sections but eventually failed.
- Minor: The paper is only tested on the KITTI dataset, and there are certain places in the whole pipeline that requires parameter tunings (such as coefficients for loss functions). What is the general guidance for parameter tuning when presented with new datasets? How do authors envision the proposed algorithm generalizes to new datasets or real autonomous driving scenarios?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
I have a broader impact question related to the overall goal of the project: I understand the scope of the project is 3D object tracking on LiDAR inputs. However for autonomous driving applications where safety is very important, why not use additional RGB information if necessary? My guess is that the current algorithm can be extended to deal with RGB+LiDAR inputs, so it'll be interesting if authors can provide more discussions about it.

6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review: 3D Siamese Voxel-to-BEV Tracker for Sparse Point Clouds

## Summary

This paper introduces a Siamese voxel-to-BEV (V2B) tracker designed to address the challenging problem of 3D object tracking in sparse point clouds, particularly relevant for autonomous driving applications. The proposed approach combines a shape-aware feature learning network with a voxel-to-BEV target localization mechanism to improve tracking robustness when dealing with sparse LiDAR inputs. Experimental validation on the KITTI and nuScenes datasets demonstrates substantial performance improvements over existing state-of-the-art methods, with particularly notable gains in sparse scene scenarios.

## Main Review

### Strengths

**Problem Relevance and Technical Soundness.** The paper tackles a genuinely challenging and practically important problem in autonomous driving. The observation that 51% of cars in KITTI contain fewer than 100 points (Figure 1) effectively motivates the work and highlights a real limitation of existing approaches. The overall algorithmic design represents a sensible and well-motivated solution to this challenge. Rather than pursuing incremental improvements on existing proposal-based methods, the authors take a principled approach by directly addressing the sparsity problem through shape-aware feature learning and dense BEV representations.

**Strong Experimental Results.** The experimental validation is comprehensive and convincing. On the KITTI dataset, the method achieves substantial improvements—for example, improving Success from 60.5% (BAT) to 70.5% on the car category, which represents a meaningful 10-percentage-point gain. The results across multiple object categories (cars, pedestrians, vans, cyclists) demonstrate broad applicability. More importantly, the paper provides targeted evaluation on sparse scenarios (Table 2), where the method shows consistent advantages. The performance gains in sparse scenes (64.7% vs 60.7% for BAT on cars) directly validate the paper's core contribution. The generalization experiments on nuScenes (Table 1) further strengthen the claims by showing the method maintains competitive performance when transferred to a different dataset, suggesting the approach captures genuinely useful principles rather than dataset-specific artifacts.

**Clear Presentation and Helpful Visualizations.** The paper is well-written with clear exposition of the main ideas. The architectural diagrams, particularly Figures 2 and 3, effectively communicate the information flow through the network. The visualization results in Figure 4 are particularly valuable, as they provide intuitive side-by-side comparisons of the proposed V2B method against P2B in both sparse and dense scenarios, making the practical benefits immediately apparent to readers. The statistical analysis in Figure 1 provides strong empirical motivation for addressing sparse point clouds.

**Code Availability.** The authors' commitment to releasing code (as mentioned in the abstract with the GitHub link) is commendable and will facilitate reproducibility and future research building on this work. For an application-oriented paper such as this, code release is especially valuable for practitioners in autonomous driving who may wish to adopt or extend the method.

### Weaknesses

**Performance Limitations on Specific Categories.** A notable weakness is the degraded performance on the cyclist category. The paper achieves only 30.4% Success on sparse cyclist frames (Table 2) compared to 64.7% for cars, representing a substantial performance gap. While the authors attribute this to insufficient training data (the cyclist category contains only 308 frames in nuScenes, as shown in Table 1), this explanation raises important questions about the robustness of the approach. Specifically: if other object categories were to have similarly limited training data, would the performance degradation follow a similar pattern? This suggests potential brittleness in the method when data is scarce, which could be problematic for less common object categories in real autonomous driving scenarios. The paper would benefit from either (1) providing analysis of performance degradation as a function of training data size, or (2) proposing strategies to mitigate data scarcity issues for underrepresented categories.

**Limited Temporal Demonstration of Tracking.** While the paper includes static visualizations of tracking results, it lacks video demonstrations showing temporal tracking performance. For an object tracking paper, videos would provide more intuitive understanding of how the method maintains target continuity across frames, handles occlusions, and recovers from tracking failures. The static visualization in Figure 4, while helpful, only shows individual frames and cannot convey the temporal dynamics of tracking. Supplementary videos showing complete tracking sequences would significantly strengthen the paper's presentation and help readers better understand the practical behavior of the method.

**Figure 3 Color Scheme Clarity.** The color scheme used in Figure 3 lacks clear semantic meaning. When examining the diagram, it is not immediately apparent what different colors represent or whether color similarity indicates related functional components. A more principled color scheme—for instance, using colors to distinguish between different types of operations (e.g., one color for similarity computations, another for feature aggregation, etc.)—would improve readability and help readers quickly grasp the architectural structure.

**Limited Analysis of Generalization and Parameter Sensitivity.** While the paper demonstrates generalization to nuScenes, the analysis of how the method generalizes to fundamentally new datasets is limited. The paper mentions parameter tuning requirements (e.g., loss function coefficients λ₁, λ₂, λ₃ are set to 10⁻⁶, 1.0, and 2.0 respectively) but provides minimal guidance on how to adapt these hyperparameters for new datasets. The ablation study on voxel size (0.1m to 0.4m) provides some sensitivity analysis, but questions remain: How sensitive is the method to other hyperparameters? What is the systematic procedure for tuning parameters when adapting to a new dataset with different sensor characteristics or point cloud density distributions? For practitioners deploying this method to real autonomous driving scenarios with different LiDAR configurations, such guidance would be invaluable. The paper would benefit from either providing principled parameter selection strategies or at least empirical guidelines based on dataset characteristics.

### Additional Observations

**Comparison with Proposal-Based Methods.** The paper's decision to move away from proposal-based localization (as used in P2B and BAT) is well-justified through Table 4, which directly compares voxel-to-BEV localization against VoteNet-based proposals in sparse scenarios. The results convincingly demonstrate that the anchor-free approach is more suitable for sparse point clouds. However, the paper could strengthen this comparison by providing computational cost analysis—does the proposal-free approach also offer efficiency benefits compared to methods that must generate and score numerous proposals?

**Shape Completion Training Overhead.** The paper notes that shape-aware feature learning is only used during training and discarded during testing (Section 3.1.2), which is appropriate for avoiding inference overhead. However, the paper could provide more details about the training-time computational cost of generating 2048-point dense completions and computing Chamfer distance losses for each training sample. This information would help practitioners understand the computational requirements for training on new datasets.

**Template Feature Embedding Design Justification.** The ablation study (Table 3) demonstrates that both global and local template feature embedding contribute to performance, but the paper lacks detailed justification for why both are necessary. The global embedding captures overall shape similarity while local embedding captures point-to-point correspondences, but more intuitive explanation of when each type of information is most valuable would strengthen the contribution.

## Broader Impact and Limitations

**Scope and Safety Considerations.** The paper focuses exclusively on LiDAR-based 3D object tracking, which is appropriate for the problem setting. However, a broader discussion of the role of this component within autonomous driving systems would be valuable. In safety-critical autonomous driving applications, why not leverage additional RGB information when available? While this may be outside the paper's stated scope, it is worth noting that modern autonomous vehicles typically employ sensor fusion. The authors could discuss how their method might be extended to handle multi-modal inputs or at least acknowledge the potential benefits of such approaches. Conversely, the paper could also discuss scenarios where LiDAR-only tracking is preferable (e.g., robust performance in adverse weather or varying lighting conditions).

**Dataset Limitations.** The evaluation is restricted to KITTI and nuScenes, both of which are primarily collected in temperate climates with specific LiDAR configurations. The generalization to other geographic regions, weather conditions, or sensor types remains unexplored. This is a common limitation in the field but worth acknowledging.

## Minor Issues

- The notation in equations could be more consistent (e.g., the use of tildes and primes for different feature variants in Section 3.1.1 becomes somewhat dense)
- Some implementation details are relegated to brief mentions (e.g., the "slightly modified PointNet++" in Section 4.1) where more specificity would be helpful
- The paper could benefit from discussing failure cases or scenarios where the method struggles

## Questions for Authors

1. How does performance scale with training data availability across different object categories?
2. What are the practical guidelines for hyperparameter selection when deploying to new datasets?
3. Can you provide video demonstrations of temporal tracking behavior?

reviews.summary: This paper tackles the problem of single-object tracking from point clouds. Specifically, it adopts a tracking by detection framework to find the target in the search area, implemented with a siamese network that extracts both the template and search area features. To improve feature discriminativeness, the template feature is embeded to the search area feature, trained with a regularization shape completion loss. In terms of target localization, in contrast to previous proposal based method, this paper adopts a dense detection approach that directly localizes the target from dense BEV voxel locations. Experiments on KITTI dataset with custom split shows significant improvements over previous state-of-the-art methods.
reviews.main_review:
Following aspects of the submission can be improved.

1. Needs a clear comparison with previous methods.
The method proposed in the submission is largely based on two previous methods, P2B [12] and SC3D [13]. In particular, the overall two-step framework follows P2B, where the first step embeds the template feature into the search area feature, implemented by a Siamese network; and the second step localizes the target given the enhanced search area feature. The main difference lies in the second step, where P2B uses a proposal based method, and this paper adopts a dense detection method, which is shown by experiments to perform much better on objects with sparse points. A suggestion here would be to add qualitative results in addition to the Table 5 in the submission to show examples of these improvements.
Another claimed novelty of shape-awre feature learning also largely follows the scene completion regularization loss proposed in SC3D, with the difference being the specific input feature (Line 187-190). A suggestion here would be to add a specific ablation study for the changes made on top of the regularization loss from SC3D, so as to justify these modifications and thereof the technical novelty.
Overall, I would suggest authors provide a clear comparison with previous methods that are technically related, and clarify what's inherited from them, and what's changed to better solve an existing problem.

2. Needs better presentation of the method section.
It's hard to follow the method section as tons of technical details are thrown to the readers without providing enough context and motivation of why the architecture is designed so. I would strongly suggest the authors to re-write the method section in the revised submission.

3. More results in the experiments.
There can be more results in the experiments to help readers understand how the model is working. For example, the ablation study with SC3D as mentioned in point 1, qualitative comparison with both SC3D and P2B in all four categories, especially the cyclist because the proposed method performs much worse in this category compared with SC3D. Also, in Table 3 the speed comparison, the official speed of P2B is 40FPS on a NVIDIA 1080Ti GPU, however, Table 3 reports 17FPS on a NVIDIA TITAN RTX GPU, any reason why this huge gap?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: I didn't see specific discussions about the limitations and potential negative societal impact in the submission, please add these in the revised submission.

reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review: 3D Siamese Voxel-to-BEV Tracker for Sparse Point Clouds

## Summary

This paper addresses the challenging problem of single-object tracking in sparse point clouds, which is particularly relevant for autonomous driving applications where LiDAR data exhibits significant sparsity. The authors propose a two-stage Siamese-based tracking framework that combines template feature embedding with a novel voxel-to-BEV (V2B) target localization approach. The method first extracts and embeds template features into the search area using both global and local feature matching strategies. Subsequently, rather than relying on proposal generation methods like VoteNet, the approach directly localizes targets from a dense bird's eye view (BEV) representation obtained through voxelization and max-pooling. The paper demonstrates substantial performance improvements over prior work on the KITTI dataset, with particularly notable gains in sparse tracking scenarios where objects contain fewer than 100-150 points.

## Main Review

The following aspects of this submission require significant improvement and clarification:

### 1. Insufficient Clarity on Technical Novelty and Relationship to Prior Work

The method proposed in this submission builds heavily on two previous approaches—P2B [52] and SC3D [21]—yet the paper does not provide sufficiently clear delineation between what is inherited from prior work and what constitutes novel contributions. This distinction is critical for evaluating the technical novelty of the work.

**On the relationship to P2B:** The overall two-step framework closely follows P2B's paradigm, where the first stage embeds template features into search area features through a Siamese network architecture, and the second stage localizes the target using the enhanced features. The authors acknowledge that P2B performs Hough voting-based proposal generation, while their method adopts a dense detection approach instead. However, beyond this architectural swap, the specific design choices and why the dense BEV approach is fundamentally better suited to the sparse point cloud problem require deeper technical justification. While Table 4 provides quantitative comparison between VoteNet-based detection and voxel-to-BEV detection in sparse scenarios, qualitative visualizations showing failure cases of the proposal-based approach and successes of the dense approach would significantly strengthen the motivation.

**On the relationship to SC3D:** The shape-aware feature learning module, while presented as a key contribution, largely parallels SC3D's use of shape completion as a regularization loss. The paper states that SC3D "cannot recover complex geometric structures of the potential target well due to limited templates and large variations" (Section 3.1.2), but this explanation remains somewhat vague. The specific architectural differences are mentioned briefly (lines 187-190 of the method section): SC3D uses a template completion model with PointNet, whereas the proposed method uses a target completion model from search area samples with a gate mechanism and global-local feature branches. However, the paper would benefit greatly from a dedicated ablation study that isolates these specific modifications. For instance, an ablation comparing: (i) SC3D's original shape completion approach, (ii) the proposed shape completion with gate mechanism only, (iii) the proposed approach with global-local branches only, and (iv) the full proposed approach would clarify which design choices drive performance improvements. Without such analysis, it is difficult to assess whether the improvements stem from fundamental algorithmic innovations or from engineering refinements.

**Suggested improvements:** The authors should provide a detailed technical comparison table that explicitly lists the differences between their method and SC3D/P2B across each component (feature extraction, template embedding, shape learning, and localization). Additionally, qualitative comparisons with both SC3D and P2B should be provided across all four object categories, with particular attention to failure cases and edge cases where the proposed method diverges from prior approaches.

### 2. Presentation of the Method Section Requires Significant Improvement

The method section (Section 3) suffers from dense presentation that obscures the intuitions and design rationales behind the architectural choices. Readers are confronted with numerous technical details—including MLP operations, max-pooling functions, gate mechanisms, EdgeConv layers, and various loss functions—without sufficient scaffolding of why these specific choices were made or how they collectively address the sparse point cloud challenge.

**Specific presentation issues:**

- **Lack of clear motivation for template feature embedding:** While Equations (1)-(4) define the global and local template feature embedding operations, the paper does not adequately explain why both global and local embeddings are necessary. What specific complementary information does each provide? How do they interact to improve discrimination between target and background?

- **Unclear purpose of the gate mechanism:** Equation (5) introduces a gate mechanism to "enhance the feature of the potential target and suppress the background," but this description is vague. What does "suppression" mean operationally? How does a learnable weight matrix W accomplish this? A visual illustration or more detailed explanation would help.

- **Dense ground truth processing appears ad-hoc:** The approach of concatenating points from all frames within ground truth bounding boxes to create 2048-point dense clouds (Section 3.1.2) lacks justification. Why is 2048 the appropriate number? How sensitive is performance to this choice? Does this preprocessing introduce any temporal information leakage during training?

- **Insufficient motivation for BEV representation:** While the paper argues that dense 2D BEV space is preferable to sparse 3D space for center regression, the underlying intuition could be better explained. The statement that "low responses in the voxelized feature map can be suppressed" by max-pooling along z-axis needs more rigorous justification. Does max-pooling along z actually suppress background responses, or does it simply compress information?

**Suggested improvements:** The method section should be restructured to follow a clear narrative arc: (1) problem statement and intuition, (2) overall architecture overview, (3) detailed component descriptions with clear motivation for each design choice, (4) loss functions and training procedure. Figures could be simplified and annotated more clearly to show information flow. A conceptual diagram illustrating how each component addresses specific challenges in sparse point cloud tracking would be valuable.

### 3. Experimental Evaluation Lacks Depth and Completeness

While the quantitative results in Tables 1 and 2 demonstrate improvements, the experimental section would benefit from more comprehensive analysis and additional ablations to fully understand the method's behavior.

**Missing ablation studies and comparisons:**

- **SC3D ablation:** As mentioned in point 1, a specific ablation comparing the shape completion approaches of SC3D versus the proposed method is essential. This should isolate the contributions of: (i) using search area samples versus template samples, (ii) the gate mechanism, and (iii) the global-local feature learning architecture.

- **Incomplete category-wise analysis:** The paper reports strong overall improvements but shows mixed results across categories. Notably, the cyclist category shows minimal improvement (30.4/37.2 vs. 25.3/30.5 for BAT in sparse scenarios, Table 2). This is a significant discrepancy that deserves explanation. Are there specific characteristics of cyclist point clouds that make the proposed method less effective? Qualitative results for cyclists should be provided to diagnose this issue.

- **Qualitative comparisons across all categories:** Table 1 provides quantitative results for four object categories, but Figure 4 shows visualization only for cars. Qualitative comparisons with SC3D and P2B for pedestrians, vans, and cyclists would provide valuable insights into where and why the proposed method succeeds or fails.

- **Speed/efficiency analysis:** Table 3 in the original submission (referenced in the review but not visible in the provided manuscript) reportedly shows 17 FPS on NVIDIA TITAN RTX GPU, while P2B achieves 40 FPS on NVIDIA 1080Ti. This substantial difference requires explanation. Is the additional computation from shape-aware feature learning responsible? What is the breakdown of computational cost across components? Does the method run in real-time for autonomous driving applications? These questions are critical for practical deployment.

- **Sensitivity analysis:** The paper studies different voxel sizes (0.1m to 0.4m) but only briefly reports results. More detailed analysis of sensitivity to hyperparameters (e.g., λ₁, λ₂, λ₃ in the loss function, feature expansion target size, radius r in offset regression) would strengthen understanding of the method's robustness.

**Dataset and evaluation concerns:**

- **Custom KITTI split:** The paper uses a custom split of KITTI (scenes 0-16 for training, 17-18 for validation, 19-20 for testing) rather than the standard benchmark split. While this follows prior work [21, 52], it limits direct comparability with other potential methods and makes it difficult for future work to build upon these results. A discussion of why the standard split was not used would be helpful.

- **NuScenes evaluation limitations:** The paper evaluates on nuScenes using models trained on KITTI without fine-tuning. While this demonstrates generalization ability, it also represents a somewhat artificial evaluation

reviews.summary: this paper proposed a Siamese voxel-to BEV tracker, including global and local template matching, shape-completion network and a voxel-to-BEV target localization network.
reviews.main_review:
The results  look promising.

My major concern if novelty is limited.  The proposed method is an ensemble of well-known components: template matching (eg. SiamRPN[1]), shape-completion and bev localization (e.g. LaserNet[2]).

Ablation studies of global and local template features are desired.

[1]Li, Bo, et al. "High performance visual tracking with siamese region proposal network." Proceedings of the IEEE conference on computer vision and pattern recognition. 2018.
[2]Meyer, Gregory P., et al. "Lasernet: An efficient probabilistic 3d object detector for autonomous driving." Proceedings of the IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition. 2019.

# Expanded Review: 3D Siamese Voxel-to-BEV Tracker for Sparse Point Clouds

## Summary

This paper proposes a Siamese voxel-to-BEV (V2B) tracker designed specifically for 3D single object tracking in sparse point clouds. The method comprises three main components: (1) a Siamese shape-aware feature learning network that performs both global and local template feature embedding, (2) a shape completion module that generates dense 3D point clouds of the target, and (3) a voxel-to-BEV target localization network that regresses the 3D center of the target from a dense bird's eye view feature map. The authors evaluate their approach on the KITTI and nuScenes datasets, demonstrating improvements over existing state-of-the-art methods, particularly in sparse scenarios.

## Main Review

### Strengths

The experimental results presented in this work are promising and demonstrate consistent improvements over existing methods. The paper shows a notable improvement on the KITTI dataset for car tracking, increasing Success from 60.5% (BAT) to 70.5% (V2B), which represents a substantial gain. The evaluation on sparse scenarios (Table 2) is particularly valuable, as it directly addresses the paper's stated motivation regarding sparse point clouds. The generalization ability demonstrated on the nuScenes dataset further supports the robustness of the proposed approach. Additionally, the comprehensive ablation studies in Section 4.3 provide useful insights into the contribution of different components.

### Major Concerns

**Limited Novelty**: My primary concern is that the novelty of this work is limited. The proposed method appears to be largely an ensemble of well-established components from prior work. Specifically:

- **Template matching mechanism**: The global and local template feature embedding (Section 3.1.1) follows the standard Siamese network matching paradigm that has been extensively explored in visual tracking. The global feature embedding using max pooling over correlation weights and the local feature embedding using cosine similarity are relatively straightforward adaptations of existing similarity matching approaches, similar to those employed in SiamRPN and related Siamese trackers.

- **Shape completion network**: The shape-aware feature learning module (Section 3.1.2) that generates dense point clouds is conceptually similar to the shape completion approach introduced in SC3D [21], which the authors themselves cite and build upon. While the authors claim their method is "a complex point cloud generation method" with a gate mechanism and global-local branch architecture, the fundamental idea of using shape completion to enhance sparse point clouds has been previously established. The use of EdgeConv for local geometric information and max pooling for global features, while reasonable design choices, are standard architectural components from the point cloud processing literature.

- **BEV-based localization**: The voxel-to-BEV target localization network draws heavily from existing 3D object detection methods, particularly LaserNet, which pioneered the use of BEV representations for 3D detection. The core idea of compressing 3D sparse features into a dense 2D BEV space through max pooling along the z-axis is a well-known technique in autonomous driving perception. The subsequent application of 2D and 3D convolutions for feature aggregation, while effective, represents standard practice in the field.

The combination of these components, while producing good results, does not constitute a significant methodological advance. The paper essentially applies existing techniques (Siamese matching, shape completion, BEV projection) to the 3D tracking problem without introducing fundamentally new concepts or mechanisms.

### Missing Ablation Studies

While the paper includes ablation studies for template feature embedding and shape-aware feature learning, the ablation analysis is incomplete in important respects:

- **Global vs. Local template features**: Table 3 provides results for "local temple" and "global temple" separately, showing that both contribute to performance. However, the ablation study would be more informative if it included more detailed analysis of how these two components interact. For instance, what is the performance when using only local features without global features, or vice versa? The current presentation shows that both are necessary but does not clearly demonstrate the relative importance of each component or explain why both are needed from a conceptual standpoint beyond the empirical results.

- **Individual component contributions**: While Table 3 shows the impact of removing shape information entirely, it would be valuable to see more granular ablations. For example, what is the contribution of the gate mechanism (Equation 5) specifically? How much improvement comes from the global-local branch architecture in the shape generation network versus using a simpler single-branch design?

- **BEV design choices**: The paper includes ablation studies for different voxel sizes (Section 4.3), which is useful. However, there is no ablation comparing the voxel-to-BEV approach against other potential 3D localization strategies beyond the VoteNet comparison in Table 4. For instance, how does the method perform if one were to use direct 3D regression in the original sparse space without BEV projection? This would more directly validate the core motivation for the BEV approach.

- **Loss function weights**: The paper sets hyperparameters λ₁ = 10⁻⁶, λ₂ = 1.0, and λ₃ = 2.0 empirically but provides no ablation study on the sensitivity to these choices or justification for the specific values selected.

### Technical Concerns and Questions

1. **Shape generation during testing**: The paper states that shape information encoding "is only performed during training and will be discarded during testing" (Section 3.1.2). While this avoids inference time overhead, it raises questions about how well the learned discriminative features transfer at test time when the shape generation network is not active. The features learned during training are conditioned on the presence of the shape generation auxiliary task; removing this component at test time could lead to a distribution shift that is not fully explored.

2. **Dense ground truth processing**: The method for generating dense ground truth (Section 3.1.2) involves concatenating all cropped and centered points from multiple frames and then fixing the number to 2048 points. This approach seems to conflate information across different frames and may not accurately represent the true geometric structure of individual target instances. How sensitive is the method to this ground truth generation procedure? Does the method work if each frame is considered independently?

3. **Computational complexity**: While the paper mentions that shape generation does not increase inference time, there is no detailed analysis of the overall computational cost during training or inference. How does the training time compare to baseline methods like P2B and BAT? What is the inference speed in frames per second?

4. **Template update strategy**: The paper briefly mentions the template generation scheme in Section 4.3 and Table 5, but the actual template update mechanism during tracking is not thoroughly discussed. How frequently is the template updated? How does the choice of template update strategy interact with the shape completion network?

### Experimental Observations

The improvements on nuScenes dataset (Table 1) are notably smaller than on KITTI, and in some cases inconsistent. For example, for the Truck category (corresponding to Van in KITTI), the method achieves 40.8/49.7 on KITTI but only 21.7/16.7 on nuScenes, suggesting potential domain adaptation challenges. This raises questions about the generalization claims and whether the improvements are primarily dataset-specific. The paper would benefit from more discussion of these domain differences and potential solutions.

## Minor Issues

1. The paper could better motivate why both global and local template features are necessary beyond the empirical ablation results. A clearer conceptual explanation of what each branch captures would strengthen the presentation.

2. The notation in equations could be clearer. For instance, the use of primes and double primes for different feature representations (q'ⱼ and q''ⱼ) becomes confusing, and a more systematic notation scheme would improve readability.

3. Some implementation details are relegated to brief mentions (e.g., "slightly modified PointNet++") without sufficient detail for reproducibility. The specific modifications should be clearly documented.

4. The paper claims the method can "significantly improve tracking performance in sparse point clouds" as its main contribution, yet the improvement on sparse scenes (Table 2) compared to BAT is only about 2% on average, which is modest relative to the added complexity.

## Limitations and Societal Impact

The authors acknowledge limitations and societal impact considerations as required. The work is focused on autonomous driving applications, which have clear societal implications. The method's reliance on LiDAR data and focus on autonomous vehicles is appropriately contextualized.

## Questions for Authors

1. Can you provide more detailed ablations on the individual contributions of global vs. local template matching?
2. How does performance degrade if shape generation is not used at test time (beyond just removing the auxiliary loss)?
3. What is the computational cost of the method compared to baselines, both at training and inference time?
4. How would you explain the smaller improvements on nuScenes compared to KITTI?

## Overall Assessment

This paper presents a competent engineering solution to 3

this paper proposed a Siamese voxel-to BEV tracker, including global and local template matching, shape-completion network and a voxel-to-BEV target localization network.

Main Review:
The results  look promising.

My major concern if novelty is limited.  The proposed method is an ensemble of well-known components: template matching (eg. SiamRPN[1]), shape-completion and bev localization (e.g. LaserNet[2]).

Ablation studies of global and local template features are desired.
