reviews.summary: This paper proposes a point cloud-based 3D single-object tracking method. To improve tracking performance in sparse point cloud scenarios, the proposed method incorporates the shape completion technique to enhance feature learning. Results are evaluated on the KITTI dataset demonstrating that the proposed method outperforms baselines
reviews.main_review:
Paper Strengths

1. The engineering efforts on designing local and global feature embedding for template feature extraction are intuitive, which also significantly improve performance as shown in Table 4 ablation experiments

2. Qualitative analysis on results for sparse point cloud intuitively demonstrating the usefulness of the proposed method

Paper Weaknesses

1. It is still vague to me if the main contributions of this paper are very useful through current experiments:

a) Regarding shape completion, the main difference claimed in this paper compared to prior work is that the proposed method performs shape completion in the search area while [13] performs shape completion for the template. Although the ablation experiments in Table 4 shows that without shape information by completion, the performance drops, it also needs to compare with shape completion on the template as [13]. As a result, I am not sure if the proposed idea is better or worse than [13]. Also, the absolute performance comparison in Table 1 does not help answer this question because presumably, the code base is different so there are lots of other factors affecting the overall performance. It would be easier to compare two ideas (this paper and [13]) by adding shape completion for the template in the proposed method, and/or change the shape completion to the search area or candidate shapes for [13]’s codebase. Also, intuitively it is unclear to me how performing shape completion for template or search area will make differences. Or maybe we should do both?

b) Regarding the voxel-to-BEV target localization, the proposed method essentially just converts the 3D voxel features into 2D by performing max pool on the z-axis. It is unclear to me, how will the feature max pool on the z-axis help the later network (z-axis head) to better localize the z-axis center? In general, people will think that max-pooling is throwing away information so intuitively it should make the z-axis center estimation harder using the maxpooled BEV features. This contradicts the paper’s claim (line 68) that after feature pool in the z-axis, the regression on both 2D center and z-axis center become more effective and accurate. Besides the above analysis, there is also no detailed analysis on this voxel-to-BEV ablation. For example, in addition to the final performance analysis, can we add an experiment showing that after adding or not adding vowel-to-BEV, will the performance on center estimation (both the 2D center and z-axis center) change significantly? 

2. The network design is not justified. According to the paper (e.g., line 126) PointNet++ is extensively used in the method. Why not use more advanced point cloud processing methods since PointNet++ is 4 years old (NeurIPS 2017). 

3. Also, it seems not reasonable to me that the evaluation (testing) is only performed on two sequences of KITTI? At this moment, even the entire KITTI tracking dataset is too small and cannot be used as a robust testbed for evaluation, let alone only two sequences of it. I understand that this paper is following prior work on this evaluation protocol, but I feel this protocol is not fully justified, in the cases that there are many better testbeds existing. For example, one can use nuScenes for more robust evaluation which has 150 sequences for validation and 150 sequences for testing with more objects. Under the current evaluation protocol, I am not confident any number or analysis we obtain from the only two KITTI sequences is robustly meaningful. Results on Cars might have some implications but results on Van and Cyclist should have a high variation because there are simply too few examples for those objects in two KITTI sequences. I would suggest that authors add one more dataset for evaluation and comparison with other methods, either nuScenes or Waymo for example, unless there is a strong reason that we can only use the two KITTI sequences for evaluation
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Limitation is addressed in the paper and negative societal impact is not discussed which I think is okay
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a point cloud-based 3D single-object tracking method. To improve tracking performance in sparse point cloud scenarios, the proposed method incorporates the shape completion technique to enhance feature learning. Results are evaluated on the KITTI dataset demonstrating that the proposed method outperforms baselines

Main Review:
Paper Strengths

1. The engineering efforts on designing local and global feature embedding for template feature extraction are intuitive, which also significantly improve performance as shown in Table 4 ablation experiments

2. Qualitative analysis on results for sparse point cloud intuitively demonstrating the usefulness of the proposed method

Paper Weaknesses

1. It is still vague to me if the main contributions of this paper are very useful through current experiments:

a) Regarding shape completion, the main difference claimed in this paper compared to prior work is that the proposed method performs shape completion in the search area while [13] performs shape completion for the template. Although the ablation experiments in Table 4 shows that without shape information by completion, the performance drops, it also needs to compare with shape completion on the template as [13]. As a result, I am not sure if the proposed idea is better or worse than [13]. Also, the absolute performance comparison in Table 1 does not help answer this question because presumably, the code base is different so there are lots of other factors affecting the overall performance. It would be easier to compare two ideas (this paper and [13]) by adding shape completion for the template in the proposed method, and/or change the shape completion to the search area or candidate shapes for [13]’s codebase. Also, intuitively it is unclear to me how performing shape completion for template or search area will make differences. Or maybe we should do both?

b) Regarding the voxel-to-BEV target localization, the proposed method essentially just converts the 3D voxel features into 2D by performing max pool on the z-axis. It is unclear to me, how will the feature max pool on the z-axis help the later network (z-axis head) to better localize the z-axis center? In general, people will think that max-pooling is throwing away information so intuitively it should make the z-axis center estimation harder using the maxpooled BEV features. This contradicts the paper’s claim (line 68) that after feature pool in the z-axis, the regression on both 2D center and z-axis center become more effective and accurate. Besides the above analysis, there is also no detailed analysis on this voxel-to-BEV ablation. For example, in addition to the final performance analysis, can we add an experiment showing that after adding or not adding vowel-to-BEV, will the performance on center estimation (both the 2D center and z-axis center) change significantly? 

2. The network design is not justified. According to the paper (e.g., line 126) PointNet++ is extensively used in the method. Why not use more advanced point cloud processing methods since PointNet++ is 4 years old (NeurIPS 2017). 

3. Also, it seems not reasonable to me that the evaluation (testing) is only performed on two sequences of KITTI? At this moment, even the entire KITTI tracking dataset is too small and cannot be used as a robust testbed for evaluation, let alone only two sequences of it. I understand that this paper is following prior work on this evaluation protocol, but I feel this protocol is not fully justified, in the cases that there are many better testbeds existing. For example, one can use nuScenes for more robust evaluation which has 150 sequences for validation and 150 sequences for testing with more objects. Under the current evaluation protocol, I am not confident any number or analysis we obtain from the only two KITTI sequences is robustly meaningful. Results on Cars might have some implications but results on Van and Cyclist should have a high variation because there are simply too few examples for those objects in two KITTI sequences. I would suggest that authors add one more dataset for evaluation and comparison with other methods, either nuScenes or Waymo for example, unless there is a strong reason that we can only use the two KITTI sequences for evaluation

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Limitation is addressed in the paper and negative societal impact is not discussed which I think is okay

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review

## Summary

This paper proposes a Siamese voxel-to-BEV (V2B) tracker for 3D single-object tracking in sparse point clouds. To improve tracking performance in sparse scenarios, the method incorporates shape completion through a shape-aware feature learning module and develops a voxel-to-BEV target localization network that operates on dense 2D bird's eye view representations rather than sparse 3D space. Results are evaluated on the KITTI and nuScenes datasets, demonstrating that the proposed method outperforms prior baselines including SC3D, P2B, and BAT.

## Main Review

### Paper Strengths

1. **Intuitive Feature Embedding Design**: The engineering efforts on designing local and global feature embedding for template feature extraction are intuitive and well-motivated. The local feature embedding using cosine similarity (Equation 3-4) and the global feature embedding using max pooling (Equation 2) provide complementary perspectives on the relationship between template and search area. The ablation experiments in Table 3 convincingly demonstrate the value of these components, showing that using only the local branch (68.0/79.2) or only the global branch (68.8/80.0) underperforms the combined approach (70.5/81.3). This systematic decomposition helps readers understand which components contribute to the overall performance.

2. **Qualitative Analysis on Sparse Point Clouds**: The visualization results in Figure 4 intuitively demonstrate the usefulness of the proposed method for sparse point cloud scenarios. The paper effectively shows side-by-side comparisons between P2B and V2B across both sparse and dense scenes, making it visually clear that V2B maintains tracking accuracy even when point density is severely reduced. The distinction between sparse and dense scenarios is particularly valuable for understanding the method's practical applicability to real-world autonomous driving scenarios where sparsity varies significantly based on object distance and occlusion.

3. **Comprehensive Evaluation on Sparse Scenarios**: The dedicated analysis in Table 2 and the methodology for identifying sparse frames (based on point count thresholds specific to each object category) provides valuable insights. The paper goes beyond simply reporting overall performance and explicitly evaluates the method's effectiveness in the challenging sparse regime, which is the core motivation of the work. This targeted evaluation strengthens the paper's contribution claim.

### Paper Weaknesses

1. **Unclear Contribution and Insufficient Justification of Shape Completion Strategy**:

   a) **Shape Completion Location and Design**: The main difference claimed in this paper compared to SC3D [21] is that the proposed method performs shape completion in the search area while SC3D performs shape completion on the template. Although the ablation experiments in Table 3 show that without shape information (dropping from 70.5/81.3 to 67.6/78.2), the performance decreases, the paper still needs to provide a direct comparison with shape completion performed on the template as SC3D does. The current paper does not convincingly demonstrate whether performing shape completion on the search area is fundamentally better than doing it on the template, or whether both approaches would be complementary and should be combined.
   
   The paper claims (Section 3.1.2) that SC3D's template completion model "cannot recover complex geometric structures of the potential target well due to limited templates and large variations of the potential target in the search area." However, this argument is not empirically validated. A stronger contribution would involve: (1) implementing shape completion on the template within the proposed framework and comparing it to search area completion, (2) analyzing the trade-offs between template completion (which has limited diversity but fixed reference) versus search area completion (which has more diversity but larger variations), or (3) exploring whether performing both simultaneously would yield better results. Without such analysis, it remains unclear whether the choice of completing the search area rather than the template is a principled design decision or simply an arbitrary engineering choice.
   
   Furthermore, the paper's explanation of why search area completion should work better is primarily intuitive rather than empirically grounded. The paper states that the method "learns the target completion model from the samples of search areas with the gate mechanism," but it's unclear whether the gate mechanism (Equation 5) is the key differentiator or whether the location of completion is more important.

   b) **Comparison Methodology**: The absolute performance comparison in Table 1 does not directly address this question because different implementations and codebases can introduce numerous confounding factors. A more rigorous comparison would involve: (1) re-implementing SC3D's template completion approach within the proposed V2B codebase to isolate the effect of completion location, (2) ablating between template completion and search area completion using identical feature extraction and localization networks, or (3) implementing a hybrid approach that performs completion in both locations to understand their complementary effects.

2. **Lack of Detailed Analysis and Justification for Voxel-to-BEV Localization**:

   a) **Counterintuitive Design Claim**: The proposed voxel-to-BEV target localization approach is claimed to improve both 2D center and z-axis center estimation (Section 3.2.1, lines 68-70). However, the design raises a fundamental concern: max-pooling along the z-axis discards information, which intuitively should make z-axis center estimation *harder*, not easier. The paper claims that "the response of the 2D center (i.e., max-pooling feature along the z-axis) in the BEV feature map actually contains the geometric structure information of the potential target," but this explanation is vague and lacks rigorous justification. 
   
   Why would the max-pooled feature, which loses z-axis information, be better for estimating the z-axis center than the full 3D voxel features? The paper's argument that "low responses in the voxelized feature map can be suppressed" (Section 3.2.1) explains why 2D center estimation might improve, but it does not explain why z-axis estimation should improve. In fact, one would expect that using the full 3D feature map would provide more direct information about the z-axis position, whereas the BEV features are inherently 2D and would require the z-axis head to infer depth information indirectly.

   b) **Missing Ablation Analysis**: Table 4 compares voxel-to-BEV with VoteNet-based localization, but this comparison does not isolate the effect of the voxel-to-BEV design itself. The paper would benefit from more detailed ablations such as:
   - Comparing 3D localization (without max-pooling) versus 2D BEV localization (with max-pooling) to directly measure the effect of the pooling operation
   - Analyzing the performance of the 2D-center head, offset & rotation head, and z-axis head separately to understand which components benefit from the BEV representation
   - Providing error analysis showing how 2D center errors and z-axis center errors change with and without the voxel-to-BEV approach
   - Visualizing the learned BEV feature maps and heatmaps to understand what patterns the network learns
   
   Without such detailed analysis, the claim that voxel-to-BEV improves z-axis localization remains unsubstantiated and appears to contradict intuition about information loss through max-pooling.

   c) **Alternative Explanations**: It's possible that the performance improvement comes not from the voxel-to-BEV design itself, but from other factors such as the different network architecture (2D convolutions on BEV versus 3D convolutions on voxels), the different loss functions, or the different training procedures. The paper does not sufficiently disentangle these factors.

3. **Unjustified Network Architecture Choices**:

   The paper states that PointNet++ [51] is used as the backbone "to reduce the inference time of the network" and notes that "it can be replaced with a powerful network such as KPConv [63]." This justification is unsatisfying for several reasons:
   
   a) **Outdated Backbone**: PointNet++ was published in NeurIPS 2017, making it over 4 years old at the time of submission. More recent point cloud processing methods such as KPConv (ICCV 2019) and other contemporary approaches have demonstrated superior performance on various point cloud tasks. The paper does not provide experimental evidence that PointNet++ is the appropriate choice for this task, nor does it compare against using more advanced backbones.
   
   b) **Inference Time Not Analyzed**: The claim about inference time is not substantiated with actual timing measurements. The paper provides no runtime comparisons between PointNet++ and alternative architectures, making it unclear whether the inference time savings are significant enough to justify using a weaker backbone. For a tracking application where real-time performance is important, this analysis should be provided.
   
   c) **Incomplete Justification**: The statement that KPConv "

To improve tracking performance in sparse point cloud scenarios, the proposed method incorporates the shape completion technique to enhance feature learning. Paper Weaknesses

a) Regarding shape completion, the main difference claimed in this paper compared to prior work is that the proposed method performs shape completion in the search area while [13] performs shape completion for the template. Although the ablation experiments in Table 4 shows that without shape information by completion, the performance drops, it also needs to compare with shape completion on the template as [13]. It is unclear to me, how will the feature max pool on the z-axis help the later network (z-axis head) to better localize the z-axis center? reviews.summary: This paper suggests a framework named Siamese voxel-to-Bird Eye View (BEV) tracker, which aims to localise and follow each object of interest in sparse point clouds. The framework first feed the template and search area into the Siamese network to extract point features,  and then by adaptively learning the correlation between the template and search area, it embeds the template’s feature into the potential object in the search area . It also learns to encode shape-aware feature learning to characterize the shape information by generating a dense point cloud from a sparse point cloud. The suggested approach decomposes the 3D center into a 2D center and a z-axis center, and regress them in a dense bird’s eye view (BEV). The framework is claimed to achieve new state-of-the-art results on the KITTI tracking dataset.
reviews.main_review:
1- Clarity: The paper is well written, and the idea is clear. The technical section can be followed easily. However, the application focus of the proposed technique is not very clear to me. The main claim in the paper is that it is a framework for tracking 3D object. is it single object or multiple object tracking? I don't see any component of tracking here such as track initiation, termination and occlusion handling. The framework seems to be a 3D detection method to me rather than any tracking frameworks. Many details clarifying how it is extended for tracking single/multiple objects are not explained.   Considering the dataset used for the evaluation, eg KITTI, I am not convinced that how it is used for tracking single object only and how it is compared with the other methods in the KITTI leaderboards.

2- Many missing related works: The related works and the comparing frameworks seem to be very narrow and cherry picked. There exist many related works in 2D MOT community (e.g. MOTChllenge or KITTI) are missed here. Similarly for 3D MOT, there exist many state-of-the work methods in KITTI, NuScene, Waymo and JRDB datastes and the proposed framework could be compared with. To name just few:  
X. Weng, J. Wang, D. Held and K. Kitani. 3D Multi-Object Tracking: A Baseline and New Evaluation Metrics. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB, NuScene ).
A. Shenoi, M. Patel, J. Gwak, P. Goebel, A. Sadeghian, H. Rezatofighi, R. Martin-Martin and S. Savarese: JRMOT: A Real-Time 3D Multi-Object Tracker and a New Large-Scale Dataset. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB ).
A. Kim, A. Ošep, L. Leal-Taixé. EagerMOT: 3D Multi-Object Tracking via Sensor Fusion. ICRA 2021(KITTI,  NuScene ).


3 -Contribution: the paper does not offer any new theoretical contribution. It is rather a pure application paper using built upon the existing  machine learning tools with some tweaks and minor extensions. While the application papers are also well acknowledged by the NeurIPS research community, but such papers require comprehensive experimental evaluation on different benchmark datasets and comapring with many state-of-the-art frameworks to validate the efficacy of their proposed system. Without this, I would be hesitant to vote for the acceptance of such a paper in this venue. 

4- Experiments and datasets: The experiments are tested on KITTI dataset  only using a new metric (not those used by this benchmark for MOT). Why the approach was not evaluated on KITTI test set and using KITTI tracking metrics such as MOTA or HOTA? I it is not clear to me how the framework can deal with non-rigid object like human (how template is defined) and if it can track human as non-rigid object why it is not tested on 3D human tracking datasets such as JRDB. What about other 3D tracking self-driving datasets such as NuScene or Waymo?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:

Summary:
This paper suggests a framework named Siamese voxel-to-Bird Eye View (BEV) tracker, which aims to localise and follow each object of interest in sparse point clouds. The framework first feed the template and search area into the Siamese network to extract point features,  and then by adaptively learning the correlation between the template and search area, it embeds the template’s feature into the potential object in the search area . It also learns to encode shape-aware feature learning to characterize the shape information by generating a dense point cloud from a sparse point cloud. The suggested approach decomposes the 3D center into a 2D center and a z-axis center, and regress them in a dense bird’s eye view (BEV). The framework is claimed to achieve new state-of-the-art results on the KITTI tracking dataset.

Main Review:
1- Clarity: The paper is well written, and the idea is clear. The technical section can be followed easily. However, the application focus of the proposed technique is not very clear to me. The main claim in the paper is that it is a framework for tracking 3D object. is it single object or multiple object tracking? I don't see any component of tracking here such as track initiation, termination and occlusion handling. The framework seems to be a 3D detection method to me rather than any tracking frameworks. Many details clarifying how it is extended for tracking single/multiple objects are not explained.   Considering the dataset used for the evaluation, eg KITTI, I am not convinced that how it is used for tracking single object only and how it is compared with the other methods in the KITTI leaderboards.

2- Many missing related works: The related works and the comparing frameworks seem to be very narrow and cherry picked. There exist many related works in 2D MOT community (e.g. MOTChllenge or KITTI) are missed here. Similarly for 3D MOT, there exist many state-of-the work methods in KITTI, NuScene, Waymo and JRDB datastes and the proposed framework could be compared with. To name just few:  
X. Weng, J. Wang, D. Held and K. Kitani. 3D Multi-Object Tracking: A Baseline and New Evaluation Metrics. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB, NuScene ).
A. Shenoi, M. Patel, J. Gwak, P. Goebel, A. Sadeghian, H. Rezatofighi, R. Martin-Martin and S. Savarese: JRMOT: A Real-Time 3D Multi-Object Tracker and a New Large-Scale Dataset. IROS 2020 (KITTI, JRDB ).
A. Kim, A. Ošep, L. Leal-Taixé. EagerMOT: 3D Multi-Object Tracking via Sensor Fusion. ICRA 2021(KITTI,  NuScene ).


3 -Contribution: the paper does not offer any new theoretical contribution. It is rather a pure application paper using built upon the existing  machine learning tools with some tweaks and minor extensions. While the application papers are also well acknowledged by the NeurIPS research community, but such papers require comprehensive experimental evaluation on different benchmark datasets and comapring with many state-of-the-art frameworks to validate the efficacy of their proposed system. Without this, I would be hesitant to vote for the acceptance of such a paper in this venue. 

4- Experiments and datasets: The experiments are tested on KITTI dataset  only using a new metric (not those used by this benchmark for MOT). Why the approach was not evaluated on KITTI test set and using KITTI tracking metrics such as MOTA or HOTA? I it is not clear to me how the framework can deal with non-rigid object like human (how template is defined) and if it can track human as non-rigid object why it is not tested on 3D human tracking datasets such as JRDB. What about other 3D tracking self-driving datasets such as NuScene or Waymo?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
No, the authors didn't address limitations and societal impact. 
Tracking 3D objects in point clouds has high potential impact in important applications, such as transportation  and self-driving vehicles. However, tracking technology can be generally deployed in human monitoring and surveillance as well which raise ethical and privacy issues.

reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review: 3D Siamese Voxel-to-BEV Tracker for Sparse Point Clouds

## Summary

This paper proposes a Siamese voxel-to-Bird Eye View (V2B) tracker designed to localize and follow objects of interest in sparse point clouds. The framework operates by feeding a template and search area into a Siamese network to extract point features, then adaptively learning the correlation between these two inputs to embed template features into potential objects within the search area. A shape-aware feature learning module generates dense point clouds from sparse inputs to better characterize target geometry. The method decomposes 3D center localization into separate 2D center and z-axis center regression tasks, performing both in a dense bird's eye view (BEV) representation derived from voxelized features. The authors claim to achieve state-of-the-art results on the KITTI tracking dataset and demonstrate generalization capability on nuScenes.

## Main Review

### 1. Clarity and Conceptual Issues

The paper is generally well-written with clear technical exposition, and the individual components can be followed without significant difficulty. The motivation for addressing sparse point clouds is well-articulated through the statistics presented in Figure 1, which effectively demonstrates that 51% of cars in KITTI have fewer than 100 points. However, there remains a fundamental conceptual ambiguity that significantly undermines the paper's contribution and positioning.

**Tracking vs. Detection Confusion**: The paper claims to address "3D single object tracking," yet the presented framework appears to be fundamentally a 3D detection method rather than a true tracking framework. The authors do not clearly explain whether this is single object tracking (SOT) or multiple object tracking (MOT). More critically, the framework lacks essential tracking components such as track initiation, track termination, and occlusion handling mechanisms. These are fundamental requirements for any legitimate tracking system, as they determine how the system manages object lifecycles across video sequences. The paper provides no discussion of how these critical components are addressed or implemented.

Examining the technical contributions more carefully, the Siamese shape-aware feature learning network (Section 3.1) and the voxel-to-BEV target localization network (Section 3.2) both appear to be detection modules that identify and localize a single object in a given frame. While the authors mention using "the first GTBB and the previous result's point cloud" as templates during testing (Section 4.1), this is merely temporal consistency exploitation, not true tracking with proper state management. The paper does not explain how the method would handle scenarios where an object temporarily leaves the field of view, becomes occluded, or re-enters the scene—all critical challenges in real tracking applications.

**Evaluation Methodology Concerns**: The evaluation protocol raises additional questions about the tracking aspect. The authors use Success and Precision metrics (Section 4.1) based on one-pass evaluation (OPE), which are standard for single object tracking. However, they do not clarify how the search region is defined in subsequent frames or how the method transitions from one frame to the next. Is the search region always centered on the previous frame's detection? How sensitive is the method to tracking failures? These details are crucial for understanding whether this is truly a tracking system or simply a frame-by-frame detection system.

### 2. Incomplete and Narrow Related Work Coverage

The related work section presents a notably narrow selection of comparison methods and misses substantial portions of the relevant literature. While the authors do discuss 2D tracking (Section 2), 3D single object tracking, and briefly mention 3D multi-object tracking, the coverage is insufficient for a comprehensive positioning of the work.

**Missing 3D MOT Literature**: The paper acknowledges 3D multi-object tracking methods but dismisses them without proper justification, claiming that MOT methods follow the same schemes as 2D MOT with 3D detectors. However, several recent state-of-the-art methods in 3D multi-object tracking on autonomous driving datasets would be highly relevant for comparison and contextualization:

- Weng et al. (2020) "3D Multi-Object Tracking: A Baseline and New Evaluation Metrics" (IROS) provides comprehensive benchmarking on KITTI, JRDB, and nuScenes datasets with standardized metrics (MOTA, HOTA)
- Shenoi et al. (2020) "JRMOT: A Real-Time 3D Multi-Object Tracker and a New Large-Scale Dataset" (IROS) presents tracking results on KITTI and JRDB
- Kim et al. (2021) "EagerMOT: 3D Multi-Object Tracking via Sensor Fusion" (ICRA) demonstrates tracking on KITTI and nuScenes

These omissions are particularly problematic because they represent the current state-of-the-art evaluation protocols and benchmarks for 3D tracking on the very datasets used in this paper. The fact that the authors do not compare against or even cite these works suggests either incomplete literature review or an attempt to avoid direct comparison with stronger baselines.

**Limited SOT Baselines**: Even within single object tracking, the paper only compares against SC3D (2019), P2B (2020), and BAT (2021). While these are reasonable choices, the paper would benefit from discussing other potential approaches or explaining why other methods cannot be adapted for this task. The narrow comparison set makes it difficult to assess the true contribution of the proposed method.

### 3. Limited Novelty and Contribution

The paper presents primarily an engineering contribution by combining existing techniques rather than introducing fundamental innovations. While application papers are valuable to the research community, this work falls short of providing the depth of contribution expected for a top-tier venue.

**Incremental Technical Contributions**: The template feature embedding (Section 3.1.1) uses standard techniques—MLPs for correlation learning and max pooling for global feature aggregation, combined with cosine similarity for local feature extraction. These are well-established operations in the deep learning literature. The shape-aware feature learning (Section 3.1.2) employs a gate mechanism (Equation 5) to enhance features, followed by feature expansion and a combination of global pooling with EdgeConv for local geometry capture. While the overall combination is reasonable, individual components lack novelty. The use of Chamfer distance loss for shape generation directly follows prior work (SC3D, as acknowledged by the authors).

The voxel-to-BEV target localization network (Section 3.2) represents the most interesting contribution, proposing to regress 3D centers by decomposing the problem into 2D center detection and z-axis regression in a dense BEV space. However, this is presented as an engineering solution rather than a principled innovation. The motivation—that sparse 3D space is difficult to work with, so projecting to dense 2D space helps—is intuitive but not deeply novel. Similar ideas of working in 2D projections or BEV representations have been explored in 3D object detection literature (e.g., PointPillars, though not cited in this context).

**Lack of Theoretical Insight**: The paper does not provide theoretical analysis or insights into why the proposed approach works. For instance, why does max-pooling along the z-axis specifically preserve the geometric structure information needed for accurate center detection? The authors claim (Section 3.2.1) that "the response of the 2D center in the BEV feature map actually contains the geometric structure information," but this assertion lacks rigorous justification or empirical validation through ablation studies.

### 4. Experimental Evaluation Concerns

While the experimental section presents results on KITTI and nuScenes, there are significant concerns regarding the experimental design, metric selection, and completeness of evaluation.

**Non-Standard Evaluation Metrics**: The paper uses Success and Precision metrics based on OPE (one-pass evaluation), which are standard for single object tracking benchmarks. However, for KITTI, the official tracking benchmark uses MOTA (Multiple Object Tracking Accuracy), MOTP (Multiple Object Tracking Precision), and more recently HOTA (Higher Order Tracking Accuracy). The authors do not explain why they deviate from these standard metrics. Using non-standard metrics makes it difficult to compare results with other methods on the official KITTI leaderboard and raises questions about whether the reported improvements would hold under standard evaluation protocols.

**KITTI Evaluation Protocol Issues**: The authors mention following prior works (SC3D, P2B) and using the training set for both training and testing with a specific split (scenes 0-16 for training, 17-18 for validation, 19-20 for testing). This is not the standard KITTI tracking evaluation protocol. The official KITTI tracking benchmark provides separate training and test sets with official evaluation servers. The paper does not explain why this custom protocol was chosen or how results would translate to the official benchmark. This is a critical limitation, as it prevents proper comparison with other methods evaluated on the official benchmark.

**Limited Dataset Diversity**: The evaluation is primarily on KITTI (with nuScenes used only for generalization testing, not primary evaluation).

title: Generalization of Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning Algorithms: Recurring and Unseen Tasks

reviews.summary: The paper analyzes the generalization error of MAML in 2 scenarios that are different in the new tasks at test time which are (i) the training tasks and (ii) unseen tasks, under the assumptions that the loss function is (strongly) convex and the number of steps of SGD in the adaptation step of MAML is 1. In the former case, this work proposes a new notion of stability that is capable of capturing the dependence on the number of data points per task in the bound. In the latter case, this work formulates a generalization error bound based on the total variation distance between the distribution of the new task and those of the training task.
reviews.main_review:
(originality & significance) I think that the paper presents an improved result over the existing work and other new theoretical results. In particular, it proposes a new notion of stability to construct a generalization error bound that depends on the number of data points per task, which allows a tighter bound compared with an existing work. It also proposes a new approach of using the total variation distance to analyze the generalization bound when the task at test time does not come from the training tasks.

(quality):  The main weaknesses of the analysis is in the assumption of the convex loss function because MAML is often used to learn a deep neural network whose loss is non-convex. Therefore, the implications of such analysis to the real applications of MAML remain unknown. However, I acknowledge that the paper justifies this weakness in Remark 2. Another limitation is about the restriction of the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML. The paper could be improved by mentioning the implications (or challenges) when the number of SGD steps in the adaptation of MAML is larger than 1.

The paper is generally well-structured and well-written. There are only very few minor typos: 
* Assumption 1(ii): “unbounded”.
* Line 196: “from [1]” should be from [24] if I’m not wrong.
* Line 218: “deferentially private” should be “differentially private”.
* Lines 286 and 287: “k” should be “K”.
* Title of section 3.3: “Task” is capitalized.

reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review: Generalization of Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning Algorithms

## Summary

The paper analyzes the generalization error of MAML in two distinct scenarios depending on the nature of new tasks encountered at test time: (i) when the test task is one of the training tasks (recurring tasks), and (ii) when the test task is completely unseen during training. The analysis is conducted under the assumptions that the loss function is strongly convex and the adaptation step of MAML involves a single step of SGD. For the recurring task case, the authors propose a novel notion of (γ, K)-uniform stability that captures the dependence on the number of data points per task n, leading to a generalization bound of O(1/mn). For the unseen task case, the authors formulate a generalization error bound based on the total variation distance between the distribution of the new task and the distributions of training tasks, establishing a principled way to quantify how task distribution mismatch affects generalization.

## Main Review

### Originality and Significance

The paper presents several contributions that advance our theoretical understanding of MAML and meta-learning more broadly. Most notably, the work proposes an improved generalization bound compared to existing analyses, particularly the prior work by Chen et al. [28]. The key innovation lies in the introduction of a new notion of stability—the (γ, K)-uniform stability defined in Definition 3—which is specifically tailored to the meta-learning setting. This definition is fundamentally more refined than previous stability notions used in meta-learning analysis because it explicitly accounts for the structure of the MAML algorithm: it modifies K data points in the inner dataset S_i^in (corresponding to the adaptation batch) and only one point in S_i^out (corresponding to the evaluation set).

The authors' careful justification of why K points need to be changed in Definition 3 is particularly insightful. As they explain, since the empirical loss in equation (3) involves summing over all (n choose K) possible batches of size K, each such batch constitutes a "data unit" in the meta-learning context. This is a conceptually important distinction from standard supervised learning, where individual data points are the atomic units. By respecting this structure, the analysis naturally captures the dependence on both m (number of tasks) and n (number of samples per task), yielding the tighter O(1/mn) bound in Theorem 2 compared to the O(1/m) bound that would result from applying the stability definition from [28].

Furthermore, the application of coupling techniques in Section 3.3 to analyze generalization to unseen tasks represents a novel theoretical contribution. The use of maximal coupling (referenced from optimal transport theory) to eliminate the naive K-dependence in the total variation distance bound is elegant and technically sophisticated. The resulting bound in Theorem 3 that is independent of K is particularly valuable because it suggests that MAML's ability to generalize to new tasks depends primarily on how close the new task distribution is to the training task distributions, rather than on the amount of test-time adaptation data—a finding with important practical implications.

The paper also makes a conceptual contribution by clearly separating and analyzing the training error (Section 3.1) and generalization error (Section 3.2) components of the total test error. This decomposition, illustrated in the error decomposition following equation (4), provides a clearer picture of how different problem parameters affect different sources of error. The distinction between generalization to recurring tasks versus unseen tasks also represents a more nuanced treatment of the meta-learning setting than many prior works.

### Quality of Analysis

#### Strengths

The mathematical analysis is generally rigorous and the proofs appear sound. The authors appropriately leverage existing results from optimization theory (Lemma 1 and related convergence results) while making novel contributions in the stability analysis. The connection between algorithmic stability and generalization, while well-established in classical learning theory, is carefully adapted to the meta-learning setting through the new stability definition. Theorem 1 establishing the connection between (γ, K)-uniform stability and generalization error is a useful theoretical tool that could potentially be applied to other meta-learning algorithms beyond MAML, as the authors note.

The treatment of the training error in Proposition 1 is competent, building on established SGD convergence results while accounting for the specific structure of the MAML objective. The authors appropriately handle the step-size constraints imposed by the generalization analysis, showing that training error can be made arbitrarily small with sufficient iterations T = Õ(mn·L/μ), which is a reasonable dependence on problem parameters.

The analysis of generalization to unseen tasks (Section 3.3) demonstrates technical sophistication. The coupling argument used to bound the difference between F_{m+1}(w) and F(w) is non-trivial and well-executed. The resulting bound in equation (10) clearly decomposes the effect of task distribution mismatch into two terms: one proportional to the average total variation distance from p_{m+1} to each training distribution p_i, and another proportional to the distance from p_{m+1} to the average of the training distributions. This decomposition is intuitive and provides actionable insights about when MAML will generalize well to new tasks.

#### Limitations and Weaknesses

**Strong Convexity Assumption**: The most significant limitation of the analysis is the restriction to strongly convex loss functions. This assumption is quite restrictive in modern machine learning, where MAML is primarily used for training deep neural networks whose loss landscapes are highly non-convex. While the authors acknowledge this limitation in Remark 2 and provide some justification, the practical relevance of the theoretical results remains questionable. The explanation that "the algorithmic stability technique is mainly limited to the convex setting" and that "in the nonconvex case, we need to keep learning rate very small to obtain meaningful generalization results" is honest, but it fundamentally limits the applicability of the theory to practical scenarios where MAML is deployed.

The authors note that strong convexity is necessary to ensure the meta-objective F(w) is convex, and that relaxing this assumption could lead to non-convexity. However, they also mention that convexity (without strong convexity) might suffice for their analysis. More discussion of what could be achieved under weaker convexity assumptions would strengthen the paper. Furthermore, it would be valuable to discuss whether the techniques developed here could potentially be extended to the non-convex setting, even if only for restricted classes of neural networks or with different analytical tools.

**Single-Step Adaptation**: Another important limitation is the restriction to a single step of SGD during the adaptation phase. While few-shot learning often involves limited adaptation steps, many practical applications of MAML use multiple adaptation steps (typically 5-10 steps). The paper briefly mentions in Section 2 that [15] extended convergence results to multiple gradient steps, but the generalization analysis remains limited to K=1 in the main results. The authors state in Section 3 that "we focus on the case that MAML is used to solve this empirical minimization problem," but this case is quite restrictive.

The paper does provide some discussion in Appendix E about extending results to larger K regimes, but this discussion is relegated to the appendix and the main results focus on the "small K regime." For practitioners using MAML with multiple adaptation steps, it is unclear how tight the bounds become or whether the qualitative insights (such as the O(1/mn) dependence) continue to hold. A more thorough treatment of the multi-step case, or at least a clearer characterization of how the bounds degrade as K increases, would significantly enhance the practical relevance of the work.

**Tightness of Bounds**: While the authors argue that their O(1/mn) bound is tight in the small K regime by comparison with classical SGD lower bounds, the dependence on K in the bound γ := O(1)·G²(1+αLK)/(mnμ) suggests the bound may become loose as K increases. The factor (1+αLK) grows linearly with K, which could be problematic in the moderate to large K regime. The authors acknowledge this in their discussion of Theorem 2, noting that "one shortcoming of this result is that it is not tight in the large K regime," and they point to Appendix E for improvements. However, the main results presented do not provide a satisfactory characterization for the practically important case of multiple adaptation steps.

**Limited Empirical Validation**: The paper is purely theoretical with no empirical validation of the bounds or the insights derived from the theory. While empirical validation is not strictly necessary for a theory paper, some experiments demonstrating that the theoretical predictions (e.g., the O(1/mn) scaling, the dependence on total variation distance) align with practice would strengthen confidence in the results. For instance, one could empirically verify whether the generalization error indeed scales as O(1/mn) by varying m and n and measuring generalization performance, or test whether the total variation distance between task distributions correlates with generalization to unseen tasks.

**Restrictive Assumptions on Distributions**: Assumption 2

reviews.summary: This paper analyzes the generalization bound of MAML-type algorithms from two aspects: (1) when the test task is a sample from the training tasks and (2) when the test task is unseen during training stage. A generalization bound on strongly convex case is given in case (1) showing the generalization error is deceasing at a rate of $O(1/mn)$. On the other hand, a bound under the total variation assumption is given in case (2), showing the bound depends on the average total variation between all the training tasks and the unseen task. The paper focuses on theory and thus no experiments are included.
reviews.main_review:
Overall I think this is a good paper, because it provides the generalization bound in terms of data points in the homogeneous case, and a total variation based analysis in the heterogeneous case. I didn't check all the proof line by line, but the key results make sense to me and are, to my best knowledge, new in the literature.

Regarding the technical parts, I have two concerns:

1. The authors state in line 292 Remark 2 that in the analysis of recurring tasks case, strong convexity is assumed because "The algorithmic stability technique is mainly limited to the convex setting." This seems a little off to me, because algorithmic stability has been studied in several previous works. For instance, Section 3.5 of (Hardt et al., 2016; https://arxiv.org/pdf/1509.01240.pdf), (London, 2017; https://arxiv.org/pdf/1709.06617.pdf), (Zhang et al., 2020; https://arxiv.org/pdf/2002.11875.pdf), etc. It seems the divergence comes from a different notion of stability proposed in the paper, which captures the number of data points per task. But I don't see how such definition would prevent the analysis on non-convex problems. Could you elaborate why the non-convexity assumption is challenging based on the stability notion in your case? Maybe an example would help.

2. A minor concern: In many previous works analyzing MAML-type algorithms, a bilevel structure is established (which is closer to how we do meta-learning in reality). For instance (Ji et al., 2020;https://arxiv.org/pdf/2006.09486.pdf) analyzes the convergence, etc. I wonder how the results in this paper would adapt/be applicable to the bilevel case? In bilevel programming, the stability analysis is not new(https://arxiv.org/pdf/2106.04188.pdf), do you see any challenge there?

**Updates**
I've read the author response and I think the authors adequately answered my question regarding difficulty in relaxing the results to the non-convex case. I encourage the authors to include the discussion into the paper so as to make it clear.

reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review

## Summary

This paper analyzes the generalization bounds of Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) algorithms from two complementary perspectives. First, when the test task is drawn uniformly at random from the training tasks, the authors establish a generalization bound for strongly convex loss functions showing that the expected excess population loss decays at a rate of O(1/mn), where m is the number of training tasks and n is the number of samples per task. Second, when the test task is unseen during training, the paper characterizes the generalization error through the lens of total variation distance between the test task's distribution and the distributions of training tasks. The paper is purely theoretical in nature with no experimental validation, focusing instead on developing a rigorous analytical framework for understanding MAML's generalization properties.

## Main Review

Overall, I believe this is a solid theoretical contribution that advances our understanding of MAML's generalization behavior. The paper provides valuable insights by separately analyzing the generalization error for both recurring and unseen tasks, which represents a more nuanced treatment than much of the prior work. The key technical innovation—introducing a novel (γ, K)-uniform stability definition that captures the role of both the number of tasks m and the number of samples per task n—is noteworthy and leads to tighter bounds compared to prior work such as Chen et al. (2020). The resulting generalization bound of O(1/mn) is a meaningful improvement over the O(1/√m) bound in prior stability-based analyses, as acknowledged by the authors. While I did not verify every line of the proofs in detail, the main results are intuitive and, to my knowledge, represent novel contributions to the meta-learning theory literature.

The paper is well-structured and clearly written. The problem formulation in Section 2 is thorough and carefully sets up the distinction between the empirical problem (4) and the population problem (2), making explicit the decomposition of test error into training error and generalization error. The separation of the analysis into training error (Section 3.1), generalization for recurring tasks (Section 3.2), and generalization for unseen tasks (Section 3.3) provides good clarity. The use of coupling techniques in the proof of Theorem 3 to eliminate dependence on K is technically elegant and demonstrates careful analysis.

### Concern 1: Strong Convexity and Non-Convex Extensions

The authors state in Remark 2 (around line 292) that the algorithmic stability technique is "mainly limited to the convex setting" and justify the strong convexity assumption by noting that "in the nonconvex case, we need to keep learning rate very small to obtain meaningful generalization results which makes it impractical." However, this explanation could be more thorough and precise. 

While it is true that algorithmic stability has been studied in non-convex settings in prior work—such as Section 3.5 of Hardt et al. (2016), London (2017), and Zhang et al. (2020)—the authors' concern appears to stem from the specific nature of their proposed (γ, K)-uniform stability definition rather than from algorithmic stability as a general technique. The divergence seems to arise because their stability notion measures sensitivity to perturbations involving K data points (which form the "data unit" in the adaptation step), and this structure may create additional challenges when the meta-objective becomes non-convex.

The authors do provide some discussion in Appendix G regarding why relaxing the strong convexity assumption is difficult, but this material could be more prominently featured in the main text. Specifically, it would be helpful to clarify: (1) How does the (γ, K)-uniform stability definition specifically prevent or complicate non-convex analysis compared to standard stability definitions? (2) What are the concrete technical barriers that arise when attempting to extend Theorem 2 to the non-convex case? (3) Are there partial relaxations (e.g., to convex but not strongly convex objectives) that might be achievable? The authors do mention in Remark 2 that their analysis could potentially extend to the convex (but not necessarily strongly convex) case, and this direction deserves more discussion. A concrete example showing how the analysis breaks down in the non-convex case would significantly strengthen the paper.

### Concern 2: Relationship to Bilevel Optimization and Stability in Bilevel Settings

The paper formulates MAML as a single-level empirical minimization problem (4), which represents a departure from how meta-learning is often studied in recent literature. Many contemporary analyses of MAML explicitly leverage its bilevel optimization structure—for instance, Ji et al. (2020) analyzes MAML through the lens of bilevel optimization and provides convergence guarantees, and subsequent works have extended this perspective. The authors briefly mention bilevel optimization in their related work but do not deeply engage with this perspective.

This raises an important question: How do the results in this paper relate to the bilevel formulation, and would the analysis and bounds change if the problem were explicitly cast as a bilevel optimization? In the bilevel setting, one typically has an outer optimization problem over the meta-parameters and an inner optimization problem over task-specific parameters. The stability analysis in bilevel programming is not new (as evidenced by recent work such as the paper on bilevel stability cited by the reviewer), so it is unclear whether there would be fundamental barriers to applying stability-based generalization analysis in the bilevel setting.

More concretely, the authors could address: (1) How does the empirical problem (4) relate to a bilevel reformulation of MAML? (2) Would the (γ, K)-uniform stability definition need to be modified to account for the bilevel structure? (3) Could the generalization bounds be improved or simplified by explicitly working with the bilevel formulation? (4) Are there any conceptual or technical advantages to the single-level formulation adopted here compared to the bilevel perspective? This discussion would help position the work more clearly within the broader landscape of meta-learning optimization theory.

### Additional Technical Observations

Beyond the two main concerns, several other aspects of the paper merit comment:

**Tightness of bounds in the large K regime:** The authors acknowledge in the discussion following Theorem 2 that their bound is not tight in the large K regime, noting that it depends on O(K/mn) but that the classical lower bound suggests O(1/mn) is achievable. While they mention in Appendix E that improvements are possible for large K, the practical implications of this limitation are not fully explored. In many meta-learning applications, K (the number of labeled samples available at test time) can vary significantly, and understanding the behavior across different regimes would be valuable.

**Assumption 2 (Polish space and non-atomic distributions):** The requirement that distributions be non-atomic is stated to ensure that "with probability one, a set of finite samples generated from a distribution pi are all different." While this is a reasonable technical assumption, it excludes discrete distributions which are common in practice (e.g., classification tasks with categorical labels). A brief discussion of whether the analysis could be extended to discrete settings, or what modifications would be necessary, would enhance the applicability of the results.

**Comparison with Chen et al. (2020):** The paper does an excellent job of highlighting how their proposed stability definition leads to a tighter O(1/mn) bound compared to the O(1/√m) bound in Chen et al. (2020) for the strongly convex case. The intuition—that their definition captures the data unit as a batch of K points rather than the entire task dataset—is clear and well-explained. However, a more detailed side-by-side comparison of the proof techniques would help readers understand the precise technical innovations.

**Theorem 3 and total variation distance:** The result in Theorem 3 characterizing generalization to unseen tasks via total variation distance is interesting and intuitive. The use of maximal coupling to eliminate dependence on K is technically sophisticated. However, the bound involves two terms: one proportional to the average total variation distance to individual training tasks, and another proportional to the total variation distance to the mean distribution. The relative magnitudes of these terms and their practical implications could be discussed more thoroughly. For instance, when would one term dominate the other?

## Limitations and Societal Impact

The paper does not discuss limitations or potential societal impacts. While the work is purely theoretical and unlikely to have direct negative societal implications, a brief discussion of the practical limitations imposed by the strong convexity assumption and the implications for real-world meta-learning applications would be appropriate. Additionally, the paper could acknowledge that the bounds, while theoretically meaningful, may still be loose in practice and that empirical validation would be valuable to assess the tightness of the theoretical predictions.

## Minor Issues

- The notation switches between w* and w*_S; ensuring consistent notation throughout would improve readability.
- Some of the key assumptions (particularly Assumption 1 with its four sub-conditions) are quite strong; a table summarizing all assumptions and their necessity would be helpful.
- The paper could benefit from a simple numerical example illustrating the dependence of the bounds on m, n, and K.

## Recommendation

This paper

reviews.summary: In this paper, the authors provide a generalization and uniform-stability-type analysis for MAML training. In specific, the authors first present a generalization error bound (based on the uniform stability bound) of $O(1/mn)$ for strongly convex objective functions when the new task at the test time belong to one of the training tasks. Then, for a more interesting setting where the new task does not belong to the training tasks, the authors provide a generalization bound consisting of a total variation distance between the data distributions of this unseen task and the training tasks. One toy experiment is provided in the supplementary materials to verify the impact of the sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance.
reviews.main_review:
In this paper, the authors provide a generalization and uniform-stability-type analysis for MAML training. In specific, the authors first present a generalization error bound (based on the uniform stability bound) of $O(1/mn)$ for strongly convex objective functions when the new task at the test time belong to one of the training tasks. Then, for a more interesting setting where the new task does not belong to the training tasks, the authors provide a generalization bound consisting of a total variation distance between the data distributions of this unseen task and the training tasks. One toy experiment is provided in the supplementary materials to verify the impact of the sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance. My detailed comments are given as below.

Pros:
1.	This paper is well written and easy to follow. I find that the notations, definitions, assumptions and related works in this paper are clear and the explanation for the main results are clear and reasonable. 
2.	Studying the generalization for MAML type of algorithms is very important, and this paper takes a good try to provide generalization error and stability bounds for MAML under various settings. 
3.	This paper shows that the sample number for the intra-task training is also important and can be used to further reduce the generalization error. 

Cons:
1.	My first concern is the tightness of the upper bound. It is true that the dependence on $mn$ (i.e., the total number of training sample) is optimal for the strongly convex objective function (in fact, this is a  standard result in statistical learning where the optimal dependence of the excess risk on the sample size M is $O(M)$). However, it is not clear whether other parameters (even some constants) are still optimal for the authors’ upper bounds, e.g., the authors do not prove the optimality of the test sample size $K$. In addition, it seems to me that the results in this paper are not surprising (even though the derivations can be different due to the MAML structure), i.e., the bounds decrease with the total number $mn$ of the training samples (as we always see in standard supervised learning). I will be more excited if the derived upper bounds can involve some special components caused by the composite structure of MAML or the upper bounds capture some interplay between the training and the test procedures of the meta training process of MAML. In Chen et al., 2020 (as pointed out by the authors), they provide interesting findings on how the support/query split used in the meta learning (including MAML) affects the generalization (as well as the theoretical generalization bounds). However, I do not see much new things for MAML in the generalization bounds in this paper. 
2.	My another concern is that the empirical verification is not sufficient. First, the curves in the experiments contain  large variations, and I suggest the authors can run more seeds and do an average to make the results convincing. Second, the authors only verify the impact of sample sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance. Since the sample size $K$ of the test data is also important, I suggest an experiment to test its impact on the generalization. Third, the authors provide a generalization bound for an unseen task, which increases with the total variation distance between tasks’ distributions. Therefore, it would be better if the authors can provide experiments to verify whether such a total variation distance metric really captures the generalization error. If so, this will be a good contribution because it may provide guidelines for how to improve the generalization of meta-learning based on the task selection.  Fourth, since the supervised classification is an important application for meta-learning, it would be better if an experiment can be done for it.
3.	The  training error characterization mainly follows from  exiting works e.g., Fallah et al., 2020, and the stability analysis is somewhat based on Bousquet & Elisseeff, 2020. Can the authors clarify here what is challenging when extending to the MAML training? 

In summary, I think this paper takes a good step to study the generalization of MAML, and it involves some novel characterizations, e.g., eliminating the dependence on $K$ for the total variation distance characterization via the maximum coupling in Hollander, 2012. Although the derivations in this paper may contain new treatments, I do not find the results interesting or exciting and the experiments are not sufficient to verify the generalization of MAML in broader applications. To attract more people form this meta-learning area,  I feel these bounds are not sufficient and more efforts should be taken, e.g., to show that such total variation bound has a tight impact on the generalization of MAML. Therefore, I tend to weakly reject this paper, but I am open to increase my score based on the authors’ feedback and other reviewers’ comments.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes

Then, for a more interesting setting where the new task does not belong to the training tasks, the authors provide a generalization bound consisting of a total variation distance between the data distributions of this unseen task and the training tasks. One toy experiment is provided in the supplementary materials to verify the impact of the sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance.

Main Review:
In this paper, the authors provide a generalization and uniform-stability-type analysis for MAML training. In specific, the authors first present a generalization error bound (based on the uniform stability bound) of $O(1/mn)$ for strongly convex objective functions when the new task at the test time belong to one of the training tasks. Then, for a more interesting setting where the new task does not belong to the training tasks, the authors provide a generalization bound consisting of a total variation distance between the data distributions of this unseen task and the training tasks. One toy experiment is provided in the supplementary materials to verify the impact of the sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance. My detailed comments are given as below.

Pros:
1.	This paper is well written and easy to follow. I find that the notations, definitions, assumptions and related works in this paper are clear and the explanation for the main results are clear and reasonable. 
2.	Studying the generalization for MAML type of algorithms is very important, and this paper takes a good try to provide generalization error and stability bounds for MAML under various settings. 
3.	This paper shows that the sample number for the intra-task training is also important and can be used to further reduce the generalization error. 

Cons:
1.	My first concern is the tightness of the upper bound. It is true that the dependence on $mn$ (i.e., the total number of training sample) is optimal for the strongly convex objective function (in fact, this is a  standard result in statistical learning where the optimal dependence of the excess risk on the sample size M is $O(M)$). However, it is not clear whether other parameters (even some constants) are still optimal for the authors’ upper bounds, e.g., the authors do not prove the optimality of the test sample size $K$. In addition, it seems to me that the results in this paper are not surprising (even though the derivations can be different due to the MAML structure), i.e., the bounds decrease with the total number $mn$ of the training samples (as we always see in standard supervised learning). I will be more excited if the derived upper bounds can involve some special components caused by the composite structure of MAML or the upper bounds capture some interplay between the training and the test procedures of the meta training process of MAML. In Chen et al., 2020 (as pointed out by the authors), they provide interesting findings on how the support/query split used in the meta learning (including MAML) affects the generalization (as well as the theoretical generalization bounds). However, I do not see much new things for MAML in the generalization bounds in this paper. 
2.	My another concern is that the empirical verification is not sufficient. First, the curves in the experiments contain  large variations, and I suggest the authors can run more seeds and do an average to make the results convincing. Second, the authors only verify the impact of sample sizes $m,n$ on the generalization performance. Since the sample size $K$ of the test data is also important, I suggest an experiment to test its impact on the generalization. Third, the authors provide a generalization bound for an unseen task, which increases with the total variation distance between tasks’ distributions. Therefore, it would be better if the authors can provide experiments to verify whether such a total variation distance metric really captures the generalization error. If so, this will be a good contribution because it may provide guidelines for how to improve the generalization of meta-learning based on the task selection.  Fourth, since the supervised classification is an important application for meta-learning, it would be better if an experiment can be done for it.
3.	The  training error characterization mainly follows from  exiting works e.g., Fallah et al., 2020, and the stability analysis is somewhat based on Bousquet & Elisseeff, 2020. Can the authors clarify here what is challenging when extending to the MAML training? 

In summary, I think this paper takes a good step to study the generalization of MAML, and it involves some novel characterizations, e.g., eliminating the dependence on $K$ for the total variation distance characterization via the maximum coupling in Hollander, 2012. Although the derivations in this paper may contain new treatments, I do not find the results interesting or exciting and the experiments are not sufficient to verify the generalization of MAML in broader applications. To attract more people form this meta-learning area,  I feel these bounds are not sufficient and more efforts should be taken, e.g., to show that such total variation bound has a tight impact on the generalization of MAML. Therefore, I tend to weakly reject this paper, but I am open to increase my score based on the authors’ feedback and other reviewers’ comments.
