reviews.text:
# Expanded Review: "On Optimal Interpolation in Linear Regression"

## Summary

This paper investigates the generalization error of a carefully defined class of interpolators in linear regression and identifies conditions for optimality within this class. The authors consider a setting where data is generated from a true linear model whose parameters are drawn from a Gaussian distribution with zero mean and covariance matrix Φ. Within the class of estimators that are functions of the training data, population covariance matrix, prior covariance matrix Φ, and signal-to-noise ratio, they derive a closed-form expression for the optimal interpolator with respect to expected excess risk. A key contribution is demonstrating that there exist specific settings where their proposed optimal estimator achieves substantially lower expected excess risk than the ordinary least squares (minimum-norm) solution. The authors also extend their results to the random features regression model, showing the generality of their approach beyond standard linear regression.

## Main Review

The authors tackle an interesting and timely problem within the active area of research on benign overfitting of linear models. This is a topic that has garnered significant attention in recent years as researchers seek to understand when and why interpolating methods can achieve good generalization performance despite fitting the training data perfectly. While the assumptions made on the problem are quite stringent, the authors provide a clean and well-organized exposition of their results that makes the paper accessible and engaging to read.

### Strengths of the Paper

The paper makes several solid contributions to our understanding of optimal interpolation in linear regression. First, the introduction of the notion of "response-linear achievable" interpolators is conceptually clean and well-motivated. This class captures a natural family of estimators that are linear in the response variable (analogous to the Bayes optimal estimator in ridge regression) while depending on population-level quantities. The closed-form characterization in Proposition 1 is elegant and provides concrete insight into what optimal interpolation looks like in this setting.

Second, the connection to optimization algorithms is valuable. Proposition 2 demonstrates that the optimal response-linear achievable interpolator can be realized as the implicit bias of preconditioned gradient descent with specific initialization, which provides an algorithmic perspective on the theoretical results. This bridges theory and practice in a meaningful way, showing that the optimal interpolator is not merely a theoretical construct but can be obtained through practical optimization procedures.

Third, the paper makes an important observation about the potential magnitude of differences between interpolators. The construction of regimes where the minimum-norm interpolator has arbitrarily worse generalization than the proposed optimal interpolator (Sections 5.3 and 5.4) is particularly valuable. These examples demonstrate that the implicit bias of the optimization algorithm and the initialization scheme can have dramatic effects on generalization performance—a phenomenon that deserves more attention in the literature. The use of the spike covariance model to construct these examples is clever and provides concrete, interpretable settings where the phenomenon occurs.

Fourth, the empirical approximation results in Section 5.1 are encouraging and practically relevant. The demonstration that the optimal response-linear achievable interpolator can be approximated using only training data (via the Graphical Lasso for covariance estimation and cross-validation for signal-to-noise ratio estimation) suggests that the theoretical insights may have practical applicability beyond the idealized setting where population quantities are known.

### Questions and Comments

**1. Relationship to and extension of Amari et al. (2021)**

The authors mention the results by Amari et al. (2021), who studied estimators whose asymptotic bias or variance are individually optimal. The relationship between the techniques and settings in the current paper versus those in Amari et al. deserves more thorough discussion. Specifically, the authors state in the introduction and in Section 4 that their results can recover those of Amari et al. as special cases (by taking δ → 0 or δ → ∞), but a more detailed explanation of how their optimization-based approach differs from the random matrix theory techniques employed by Amari et al. would strengthen the paper.

The key distinction appears to be that the current paper directly minimizes the overall risk (bias plus variance) rather than optimizing bias and variance separately. However, the paper could better explain why this joint optimization is more appropriate than separate optimization, and under what circumstances one might prefer the Amari et al. approach of separately optimizing bias and variance. Additionally, the authors mention in Proposition 3 that preconditioned gradient descent achieves optimal variance among all interpolators, not just response-linear achievable ones. This is an interesting result that deserves more discussion regarding its implications and limitations.

**2. Positioning relative to Bartlett et al. (2020)**

The authors mention on line 91 that an interesting question they study is whether the minimum-norm interpolator is always close to optimal, and they show that the answer is no. However, as the reviewer correctly notes, this question has already received partial attention in prior work. Specifically, Bartlett et al. (2020) have shown that the minimum-norm interpolator is not always consistent—for instance, when the population covariance matrix is isotropic, the minimum-norm interpolator can be inconsistent while ridge regression solutions with appropriate regularization remain consistent.

The current paper would benefit from a more nuanced discussion of how their negative results relate to and extend the observations of Bartlett et al. The paper does reference benign overfitting regimes identified in that work, but could more explicitly discuss the connections and differences. For instance, are the regimes identified in the current paper (spike covariance with extreme eigenvalue ratios) related to or distinct from the regimes studied in Bartlett et al.? How do the magnitudes of the performance gaps compare? This contextualization would help readers understand the novelty and significance of the current results within the broader literature.

**3. Relaxation of Gaussian assumptions**

The paper assumes that both the noise ξ and the true parameter w* are drawn from Gaussian distributions. While the authors note that results are proven in general but presented for Gaussian distributions for exposition, the actual extent to which the Gaussian assumption is critical remains unclear. More specifically:

- Can Proposition 1 be extended to non-Gaussian settings? Are there specific properties of Gaussianity that are essential for the derivation, or would the result hold under more general moment conditions?
- The bias-variance decomposition in equation (3) relies on conditioning on w* and X. How would the results change if we only had partial information about the true parameter or if it were drawn from a non-Gaussian distribution?
- For practical applicability, it would be valuable to understand whether the empirical approximation wOe (which uses only training data) continues to work well under non-Gaussian noise or parameter distributions.

**4. Extension to non-zero-mean parameters**

The paper assumes that w* has mean zero, i.e., E(w*) = 0. This is a natural assumption in many settings, but it would strengthen the paper to discuss whether and how the results extend to the case where E(w*) ≠ 0. This is not merely a technical question but has practical implications: in some applications, one might have prior knowledge that the true parameter has a non-zero mean. Can the definition of response-linear achievable interpolators be extended to incorporate a mean term? Would Proposition 1 still hold with a simple modification?

**5. Empirical approximation experiments: covariance structure and estimation quality**

In the experiments described in Section 5.1 and illustrated in Figures 1 and 2, the paper demonstrates that the empirical approximation wOe performs nearly identically to the optimal response-linear achievable interpolator wO. However, several aspects of these experiments deserve clarification:

- What is the structure of the population covariance matrix Σ in these experiments? The paper mentions the spike covariance model with eigenvalues ρ₁ and ρ₂, but is this the only structure tested? Have the authors tested cases with isotropic covariance or other structures?
- The paper uses the Graphical Lasso with parameter α = 0.25 to estimate Σ. How close is the empirical estimate Σe to the true Σ in these experiments? A visualization or quantitative comparison (e.g., operator norm distance or spectral norm error) would be valuable. This would help readers understand whether the good performance of wOe is robust to covariance estimation error or relies on the Graphical Lasso being very accurate in the specific regimes tested.
- How sensitive are the results to the choice of α in the Graphical Lasso? The authors mention that α can be cross-validated for better performance, but do they observe significant variation in results across different values of α?
- The cross-validation procedure for selecting δe uses a "random tenth" of the data. How stable is this procedure? Does the choice of δe vary significantly across different random splits?

These details would help assess the practical robustness of the empirical approximation approach.

**6. Finite-sample behavior and convergence**

While the paper provides finite-dimensional results (Propositions 1 and 3), much of the comparison between interpolators in Section 5 relies

Main Review:
The authors study an interesting problem on a topic of much recent interest---benign overfitting of linear models. While the assumptions made on the problem are pretty stringent, the authors provide a clean exposition of their results which I appreciated as a reader. 

Here are some questions/comments about the paper:

1. The authors mention the results by Amari et al. (2021), who they mentioned studied estimators whose asymptotic bias or variance is optimal. How do the techniques and setting used in this paper differ from theirs? Can the techniques used there also be used here but instead to balance the bias and the variance optimally? A discussion regarding this would be helpful to the reader. 

2. On ln 91 the authors say an interesting question that they study here is if minimum norm interpolant is always close to optimal, and show that this is not the case. But I think this question has already partially been answered given past work of Bartlett et al. (2020). Given their results, it is easy to construct cases, for example, when population covariance matrix is isotropic, where the minimum norm interpolant is not consistent but the ridge regression solution is. So, I would urge the authors to provide some more nuanced context and discussion here.

3. Regarding the assumptions both the noise xi and the true parameter w* are assumed to be drawn from a Gaussian distribution. Can this be relaxed? Further, can the results of this paper (Proposition 1 in particular) be extended to hold in the case where w* is not mean zero?

4. In the experiments described in Section 4.1, where w_O is approximated by w_{O_e}, what is the structure of the population covariance matrix? Is it isotropic or close to it? Or does it have a heavy tailed structure similar to one that was shown to be necessary by Bartlett et al. for the minimum norm solution to be consistent? Further, empirically, was the estimate Sigma_e close to Sigma? A plot to verify this would be nice.


=================== Post rebuttal comments =================== 

Firstly, I would like to thank the authors for their very carefully response to the questions raised by all of the reviewers. I continue to support acceptance of this paper post the rebuttal period. In my view, I think it would be nice if the authors incorporate the many helpful suggestions raised in all of the reviews in a subsequent version.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The authors do a good job of describing the potential negative impact of their work.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept

**Overall comments and impression**

First, I would like to say that I am not an expert in statistical learning theory, so that I may have missed some insights in the results.

To begin, I would like to say that the paper is perfectly well written: the story is very clear and the mathematical writing is precise and fluid. This made the reading very pleasant and I would like to thank the authors for that.

The aim of the author is clear: provide an (under certain constraints) optimal linear predictor that may perform better than the minimum norm one naturally considered in the literature (naturally coming from GD or SGD on the empirical risk). The authors do so quite effortlessly ad give, to my opinion a nice result for those asking whether minimum norm solutions are always good estimators. **Yet**, I would have liked a lot more comments on the shape of this estimator: what intuition should the reader build when reading the paper ? A clear weakness is that the authors never really take the time of analysing why makes the estimator optimal (beyond technical considerations). In the very same manner, Proposition 2 gives that the optimal linear response is in fact the solution recovered by GD with a certain initialization: could the authors comment a bit on this ? 

The result on the divergence of the minimum norm linear interpolator (whereas the optimal one does not) is nice: however I would really need to understand further *why* and *when* we should expect this to happen. And in general cases *how close* should I expect the minimum norm interpolator to be close to the optimal one ? 


**Minor comments:**

- I guess that the part on the random matrix concepts arrive too early. As far as I understood, there is absolutely no need to put this part at the beginning, and this could be postpone in section 4.2 or even in the Appendix. 

- The part on random features model gives nothing new to the results: they are not commented (at all) and are absolutely expected once the linear model is covered. If these results are not further commented, then I recommend to put it in the Appendix as they do not give any additional insight on the paper. A nice introduction on why linear model are trendy is enough in my opinion (NTK, random features...)

- In Section 4.2, is this possible to derive a close form for the asymptotic risk of the optimal interpolator ? If not, why is this complicated ?

- Give more motivation and justification for the form of the *optimal linear interpolating estimator* you build. Why are you okay with the dependance on \Sigma, \phi, \delta. How could relax these ? I understand that these parameter will be estimated on the fly later: but once again, the comments on this are not deep precise enough to convince me. In particular there are only a few comments on the Graphiical Lasso approximation and why this is a good idea to approximate the population covariance.

- Plots commenting Proposition 2 would rely complement the result! If not, it seems not so important: a comparison with GD without preconditionning would be welcomed.


**Conclusion**

Even if I really enjoyed reading the paper, my opinion is that, under this form, the results lack from commentaries, intuition, insights and a bit of further developments.

reviews.text:
# Expanded Review: On Optimal Interpolation in Linear Regression

## Summary

The paper under review studies the problem of optimal linear interpolation in overparametrized linear regression. More precisely, the authors derive an explicit formula for the *best* linear interpolator that can be written as a linear function of the outputs (response-linear achievable) and as a function of desired quantities including the population covariance matrix, signal-to-noise ratio, and prior covariance. From this closed-form solution, they show how to recover theoretically this estimator as the implicit bias of a preconditioned gradient descent optimization algorithm with specific initialization. Finally, they expose cases where the designed estimator shows superior generalization properties compared to the minimum norm interpolator, which is known to be the implicit bias of standard gradient descent. The extension to random features regression is also provided.

## Main Review

### Overall Comments and Impression

First, I would like to say that I am not an expert in statistical learning theory, so that I may have missed some insights in the results. Nevertheless, the paper presents technically sound contributions to an important question in modern machine learning.

To begin, I would like to say that the paper is perfectly well written: the story is very clear and the mathematical writing is precise and fluid. This made the reading very pleasant and I would like to thank the authors for that. The presentation flows naturally from motivation through definitions to main results and empirical validation, making it accessible to readers from diverse backgrounds.

The aim of the authors is clear and well-motivated: provide an (under certain constraints) optimal linear predictor that may perform better than the minimum norm one naturally considered in the literature (naturally coming from GD or SGD on the empirical risk). The authors do so quite effortlessly and give, to my opinion, a nice result for those asking whether minimum norm solutions are always good estimators. The paper makes a valuable contribution to the interpolation literature by systematically connecting interpolating methods to notions of optimality, which the authors correctly identify as having received only partial attention in prior work.

**Yet**, I would have liked a lot more comments on the shape of this estimator: what intuition should the reader build when reading the paper? Looking at Equation (6), the optimal response-linear achievable interpolator has a somewhat complex form involving both the prior covariance Φ and the pseudoinverse term. A clear weakness is that the authors never really take the time of analyzing why this particular form makes the estimator optimal (beyond technical considerations). What is the role of each component? How do the two terms in the matrix product balance? The paper would benefit significantly from a careful decomposition and interpretation of Equation (6). For instance, is there a way to understand the optimal interpolator as a weighted combination of simpler estimators, or as a modification of some baseline approach?

In the very same manner, Proposition 2 gives that the optimal linear response is in fact the solution recovered by GD with a certain initialization (Equation 8): could the authors comment a bit on this? The initialization involves the same terms as the optimal solution itself, which seems somewhat circular. What is the intuition behind this particular initialization? Why does this specific choice of w₀ lead to optimality? How does this initialization relate to the structure of the problem? Does the initialization encode information about the signal-to-noise ratio and prior covariance in a meaningful way?

The result on the divergence of the minimum norm linear interpolator (whereas the optimal one does not) is nice and represents a concrete contribution: however I would really need to understand further *why* and *when* we should expect this to happen. The paper shows that in the strong/weak features model (Section 5.3 and 5.4), the minimum-norm interpolator can have variance that diverges as √(ρ₁/ρ₂), while the optimal interpolator remains bounded. But is this phenomenon restricted to this particular covariance structure, or should we expect similar behavior more broadly? What properties of the covariance matrix are responsible for this divergence? The authors construct specific regimes (γ = 2, ψ₁ = 1/2) where this happens, but these seem somewhat artificial. How robust is this phenomenon to perturbations of these parameters?

More generally, in typical practical scenarios, how close should I expect the minimum norm interpolator to be to the optimal one? The paper shows one direction (where they can be arbitrarily far apart), but provides limited guidance on typical cases. Is there a characterization of when the two interpolators are close versus far apart? What are the critical eigenvalue ratios or spectral properties that determine the gap between them? These questions would help practitioners understand when the proposed approach is likely to provide significant benefits.

### Minor Comments

**Section Organization and Presentation:**

I guess that the part on the random matrix theory concepts (Section 5.2) arrives too early. As far as I understood, there is absolutely no need to put this part at the beginning of Section 5, and this could be postponed to Section 5.3 where it is actually needed, or even placed in the Appendix. The Definitions 5 and 6 on spectral distributions and Stieltjes transforms are technical tools that are not immediately necessary for understanding the main examples. Moving this material would improve the flow of the paper and allow readers to engage with the concrete examples (Sections 5.3 and 5.4) before encountering the technical machinery needed for the asymptotic analysis.

**Random Features Extension:**

The part on the random features model (Section 6) gives nothing new to the results beyond a straightforward extension: they are not commented upon (at all) and are absolutely expected once the linear model is covered. The propositions for random features (Propositions 4 and 5) follow the same pattern as their linear regression counterparts, and no new phenomena or insights emerge from this extension. The notation becomes heavier (with Σz and Σzx), but the conceptual contribution is limited. If these results are not further commented upon or if they do not provide additional insights into the structure of optimal interpolation, then I recommend moving them to the Appendix. The nice introduction on why linear models are trendy (mentioning NTK, random features, and neural tangent kernels in the introduction) is enough in my opinion to motivate the focus on linear models, without requiring a full treatment of random features in the main text.

**Asymptotic Analysis:**

In Section 5.3 and 5.4, asymptotic risk formulas are derived for specific cases (equations 13-16). However, I would like to ask: is it possible to derive a closed form for the asymptotic risk of the optimal interpolator wO? If not, why is this complicated? The paper shows that wO has better behavior than wℓ2 and wV in specific regimes, but without a general asymptotic formula for wO itself, it is difficult to understand its behavior more broadly. Does the lack of a closed form suggest fundamental limitations of the approach, or is it simply a technical challenge?

**Dependence on Population Quantities:**

The authors should provide more motivation and justification for the form of the *optimal linear interpolating estimator* they build. Why are you okay with the dependence on Σ, Φ, δ? How could we relax these dependencies? I understand that these parameters will be estimated on the fly later (Section 5.1): but once again, the comments on this are not deep or precise enough to convince me. 

In particular, there are only a few comments on the Graphical Lasso approximation and why this is a good idea to approximate the population covariance. The authors state in Section 5.1 that "Under specific assumptions, Ravikumar et al. (2011) provide some convergence guarantees of the Graphical Lasso," but they do not discuss what these assumptions are or whether they hold in the experiments. What happens when the Graphical Lasso fails to accurately estimate Σ? How sensitive is the empirical interpolator wOe to errors in the covariance estimation? The paper provides numerical evidence that wOe performs well (Figures 1-6), but a more thorough analysis of the approximation error would strengthen the claims. Similarly, for the signal-to-noise ratio δ, the authors use cross-validation on a random tenth of the data. How sensitive is this procedure to the choice of validation set size? Would other estimation procedures for δ work equally well?

Furthermore, the assumption that Φ can be estimated or is known seems restrictive. The authors mention in Section 5.1 that "even if no such estimate is available, in Section A.9 we empirically show that, in our examples, using Φ̂ = Id when Φ ≠ Id has a small effect on generalization." This is relegated to the appendix, but it deserves more prominence. If the method is robust to misspecification of Φ, this should be highlighted as a significant practical advantage. Conversely, if there are cases where misspecification of Φ leads to poor performance, these should also be discussed.

**Comparison with Gradient Descent:**

The paper claims that the optimal interpolator is the limit of preconditioned gradient descent with specific initialization

reviews.summary: This paper considers the problem of optimal interpolation in linear regression. It defines the class of response-linear achievable interpolators, and provides a closed-form expression for the optimal interpolator in this class. The paper is about an important topic in machine learning and statistics. However, it is unclear to me whether the "achievable" interpolators are really achievable, and as a result if the investigation is meaningful.
reviews.main_review:
Major comments and questions:
1. The paper claims that the realization of true data-generating parameter and the realization of the noise in the training data are unknown. However, it assumes that the population covariance, the signal-to-noise ratio, and the covariance of the prior for the signal are known. The latter seems to be more unrealistic. For high dimensional data, the covariance matrix contains much more number of parameters than the regression coefficient so it is a much harder problem to estimate the covariance matrix. In a Bayesian setting, the primary goal is to find the distribution of the parameter. If the distribution of the parameter is assumed to be known, then there is no estimation problem.

2. For $\Phi$, do you mean it is the correlation matrix? Otherwise calling $r^2$ the signal is not appropriate. 
   
3. The population risk between lines 125 and 126 is not clear.It is written as the conditional expectation given $X, \xi, w^*$, but this does not make sense. A risk should not be random.

4. I think the $y$ in line 131 should be $y_i$, but what is the $y$ in line 132? The $y$ in line 117 should be $y_i$? How about the $y$ in lines 120 and 124? Each of these may be typos or poor notations, but when there are too many this type of problems and makes it hard to following the paper it becomes a major issue. 
   
5. Line 142, I believe you need $\gamma\ge1$.

6. Line 156 says "rank(X) = n with probability 1" while this becomes a condition in line 160.

7. Definition 4, do you mean there exists a known function? Otherwise, the function $f$ may depends further on other unknowns.

8. As in my first point, the $w_O$ is not realistic. The updating formula in (7) requires the inverse of $\Sigma$ which is almost impossible for high dimensional data. 

9. I don't think Hastie et al. (2019) considered the case of $\Phi=I_d$. It is probably a typo here.
   

Minor issues and typos:

Line 53, "are best" to "are the best".

In (5), use paratheses to make the definition clear.

This paper considers the problem of optimal interpolation in linear regression. It defines the class of response-linear achievable interpolators, and provides a closed-form expression for the optimal interpolator in this class. The paper is about an important topic in machine learning and statistics. However, it is unclear to me whether the "achievable" interpolators are really achievable, and as a result if the investigation is meaningful.

Main Review:
Major comments and questions:
1. The paper claims that the realization of true data-generating parameter and the realization of the noise in the training data are unknown. However, it assumes that the population covariance, the signal-to-noise ratio, and the covariance of the prior for the signal are known. The latter seems to be more unrealistic. For high dimensional data, the covariance matrix contains much more number of parameters than the regression coefficient so it is a much harder problem to estimate the covariance matrix. In a Bayesian setting, the primary goal is to find the distribution of the parameter. If the distribution of the parameter is assumed to be known, then there is no estimation problem.

2. For $\Phi$, do you mean it is the correlation matrix? Otherwise calling $r^2$ the signal is not appropriate. 
   
3. The population risk between lines 125 and 126 is not clear.It is written as the conditional expectation given $X, \xi, w^*$, but this does not make sense. A risk should not be random.

4. I think the $y$ in line 131 should be $y_i$, but what is the $y$ in line 132? The $y$ in line 117 should be $y_i$? How about the $y$ in lines 120 and 124? Each of these may be typos or poor notations, but when there are too many this type of problems and makes it hard to following the paper it becomes a major issue. 
   
5. Line 142, I believe you need $\gamma\ge1$.

6. Line 156 says "rank(X) = n with probability 1" while this becomes a condition in line 160.

7. Definition 4, do you mean there exists a known function? Otherwise, the function $f$ may depends further on other unknowns.

reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review: On Optimal Interpolation in Linear Regression

## Summary

This paper investigates the problem of optimal interpolation in linear regression by introducing the class of response-linear achievable interpolators—those that are linear in the response variable and depend on the data, population covariance, signal-to-noise ratio, and prior covariance. The authors provide a closed-form expression for the optimal interpolator in this class (Proposition 1) and show that it can be derived as the implicit bias of preconditioned gradient descent with specific initialization (Proposition 2). The paper demonstrates through constructed examples that the minimum-norm interpolator can generalize arbitrarily worse than their proposed optimal interpolator, and extends the results to random features regression. While the paper addresses an important topic in machine learning and statistics, there are significant concerns regarding the practical achievability of the proposed interpolators and the realism of the assumptions made. These concerns raise fundamental questions about whether the theoretical investigation provides meaningful insights for realistic machine learning problems.

## Main Review

### Major Comments and Questions

**1. Questionable Practicality of "Achievable" Interpolators**

The paper claims that the realization of the true data-generating parameter and the realization of the noise in the training data are unknown, yet assumes that the population covariance matrix Σ, the signal-to-noise ratio δ, and the covariance of the prior for the signal Φ are known. This assumption appears substantially more unrealistic than the unknowns the authors claim to avoid. In high-dimensional settings, the population covariance matrix contains O(d²) parameters, making it exponentially harder to estimate than the O(d) regression coefficients themselves. For example, when d = 1000, the covariance matrix has roughly one million parameters compared to one thousand regression coefficients. Estimating such a high-dimensional matrix with statistical accuracy is a notoriously difficult problem, even with large sample sizes.

Moreover, the paper's framing obscures this fundamental issue. The authors argue in Section 5.1 that their empirical approximation w^O_e uses only training data X and y without knowledge of Σ or δ, yet this empirical approximation relies on the Graphical Lasso to estimate Σ and cross-validation to estimate δ. This is not the same as not using this information—it merely shifts the burden to estimation. The paper provides no theoretical guarantees on how well the Graphical Lasso recovers the true covariance, particularly for general covariance structures. The authors themselves acknowledge this limitation in the conclusion, stating that "a precise guarantee on the approximation error of the Graphical Lasso for a general covariance matrix Σ" is lacking. This acknowledgment undermines the claim that w^O_e is a practical solution, as the approximation quality depends critically on problem-dependent conditions that may not hold in practice.

Furthermore, in a Bayesian setting where the prior distribution of the parameter is assumed to be known—specifically, that w* ~ N(0, (r²/d)Φ)—the primary goal of inference is typically to learn or refine the posterior distribution of the parameter. If the prior distribution is assumed to be fully known a priori, one must question whether there remains a meaningful estimation problem. The assumption that both the prior covariance Φ and the signal-to-noise ratio δ are known essentially presupposes substantial problem knowledge that would rarely be available in practice.

**2. Ambiguity in the Definition of Φ and Terminology**

The paper uses r² to denote "the signal" and defines the prior covariance as E(w*w*^T) = (r²/d)Φ, where Φ is a positive definite matrix. This terminology and parameterization are confusing and potentially misleading. If Φ is not the identity matrix, then r² does not have a clear interpretation as "the signal" in the usual sense. The term "signal" typically refers to the norm or energy of the true parameter, but here it appears to be only one component of a more complex quantity. The paper would benefit from clarifying whether Φ should be interpreted as a correlation matrix, a normalized covariance matrix, or something else entirely. The current presentation conflates the magnitude of the signal (captured by r²) with its structure (captured by Φ), making it difficult to understand what "signal-to-noise ratio" actually means when Φ ≠ I_d. A more explicit discussion of how these quantities interact and what they represent would significantly improve clarity.

**3. Unclear Definition and Notation for Population Risk**

The definition of population risk in the text around equations (1)-(2) lacks clarity. The risk is written as a conditional expectation given X, ξ, and w*, but this formulation is problematic because a risk should be a deterministic quantity (or at least not random with respect to all sources of randomness). The notation E_{x̃,ξ̃} suggests expectation over test data (x̃, ỹ, ξ̃), but the relationship between this expectation and the conditioning on X, ξ, w* is not clearly explained. The decomposition in equation (2) defines bias and variance in terms of conditional expectations, but it is unclear over what distribution these conditional expectations are taken. More specifically, when the paper writes E_{ξ,w*} r(w), it is ambiguous whether this expectation is over the randomness in the training data, the test data, or both. Clarifying these notational conventions and explicitly stating the order of expectations would significantly improve readability.

**4. Inconsistent and Problematic Notation Throughout**

The paper contains numerous notational inconsistencies and ambiguities that hinder understanding:

- In equation (2) and surrounding text, the use of y is inconsistent. Line 131 appears to use y where y_i might be intended, but line 132 uses y in a different context. The same issue appears in lines 120 and 124. These may be minor typos individually, but their cumulative effect makes it difficult to follow the mathematical development.

- The notation switches between y as a vector in R^n and y_i as individual scalar responses without always making this distinction clear.

- When the paper refers to "the response variable," it sometimes means the entire vector y and sometimes means individual responses y_i, creating ambiguity in expressions like equation (2).

- The use of tildes (x̃, ỹ, ξ̃) to denote test data is introduced informally and could be more clearly defined upfront.

While each individual notational issue might be minor, the accumulation of such problems significantly impedes the reader's ability to verify claims and understand the technical content. In a mathematical paper, precision in notation is essential, and readers should not need to spend effort disambiguating what quantities are being discussed.

**5. Inconsistent Treatment of Assumptions and Conditions**

Line 142 states that "rank(X) = n with probability 1," but this is later treated as a condition (Assumption 1) rather than a consequence. The paper should clarify whether this is derived from the stated assumptions or is an additional requirement. Furthermore, line 142 appears to assume γ ≥ 1 in the context of discussing d/n → γ, but this condition is not explicitly stated at that point. The paper should clearly delineate which statements are assumptions, which are derived consequences, and which are stated as goals or regime descriptions. This distinction is particularly important for readers trying to understand under what conditions the main results hold.

**6. Unrealistic Computational Requirements**

Proposition 2 and equation (7) show that the optimal interpolator is the limit of preconditioned gradient descent with preconditioning by Σ^{-1}. Computing Σ^{-1} for high-dimensional data is computationally challenging and numerically unstable. Even assuming Σ were known (which contradicts the practical motivation), inverting a d × d matrix when d is large requires O(d³) operations and can suffer from severe numerical conditioning issues. The paper does not discuss these computational challenges or propose practical alternatives. While Section 5.1 attempts to address this through the empirical approximation w^O_e using the Graphical Lasso, the theoretical analysis does not account for the approximation error introduced by replacing Σ with an estimate. The absence of convergence guarantees for the Graphical Lasso in general settings means that the theoretical optimality guarantees provided by Proposition 1 may not apply to the empirical version.

**7. Questionable Citation and Assumption About Prior**

The paper states "I don't think Hastie et al. (2019) considered the case of Φ = I_d" in the context of equation (13). This appears to be a transcription error or misstatement that should be clarified. If Hastie et al. did not consider this case, the paper should either justify why their results apply here or provide an independent derivation. The conditions under which equation (13) holds should be explicitly stated in the main text rather than relying
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper considers the problem of optimal interpolation in linear regression. It defines the class of response-linear achievable interpolators, and provides a closed-form expression for the optimal interpolator in this class. The paper is about an important topic in machine learning and statistics. However, it is unclear to me whether the "achievable" interpolators are really achievable, and as a result if the investigation is meaningful.

Main Review:
Major comments and questions:
1. The paper claims that the realization of true data-generating parameter and the realization of the noise in the training data are unknown. However, it assumes that the population covariance, the signal-to-noise ratio, and the covariance of the prior for the signal are known. The latter seems to be more unrealistic. For high dimensional data, the covariance matrix contains much more number of parameters than the regression coefficient so it is a much harder problem to estimate the covariance matrix. In a Bayesian setting, the primary goal is to find the distribution of the parameter. If the distribution of the parameter is assumed to be known, then there is no estimation problem.

2. For $\Phi$, do you mean it is the correlation matrix? Otherwise calling $r^2$ the signal is not appropriate. 
   
3. The population risk between lines 125 and 126 is not clear.It is written as the conditional expectation given $X, \xi, w^*$, but this does not make sense. A risk should not be random.

4. I think the $y$ in line 131 should be $y_i$, but what is the $y$ in line 132? The $y$ in line 117 should be $y_i$? How about the $y$ in lines 120 and 124? Each of these may be typos or poor notations, but when there are too many this type of problems and makes it hard to following the paper it becomes a major issue. 
   
5. Line 142, I believe you need $\gamma\ge1$.

6. Line 156 says "rank(X) = n with probability 1" while this becomes a condition in line 160.

7. Definition 4, do you mean there exists a known function? Otherwise, the function $f$ may depends further on other unknowns.

8. As in my first point, the $w_O$ is not realistic. The updating formula in (7) requires the inverse of $\Sigma$ which is almost impossible for high dimensional data. 

9. I don't think Hastie et al. (2019) considered the case of $\Phi=I_d$. It is probably a typo here.
   

Minor issues and typos:

Line 53, "are best" to "are the best".

In (5), use paratheses to make the definition clear.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Please see the main review.

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.original_rating: 4: Ok but not good enough - rejection

title: Differentiable rendering with perturbed optimizers

reviews.summary:
This paper proposes a new differentiable renderer by introducing perturbed optimizers. The paper re-writes the non-differentiable rasterization and aggregation steps to heaven side functions and relaxes them to be differentiable by adding noise. 
The author shows that previous work, especially softras, can be treated as a special case of the perturbed renderer with specific prior selections. The authors also demonstrated in several experiments they achieve better or comparable results than the state of the art.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths

1) Differentiable reformulation
This paper treats rasterization as a linear programing problem.  It extends the [4] to the rendering pipeline and relaxes the non-differentiable rendering to be differentiable by introducing perturbed optimizers. It provides a new differentiable renderer from a new perspective.

2) Better performance
The authors show AS and VR are very useful in optimization, for both perturbed renderer and softras. In the 6D pose comparison, the results are better than softras.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
There are several weak points of this paper.

1) The paper is not self-contained.  I have a hard time understanding each equation. While the authors largely rely on [4], it makes readers hard to follow without knowing [4]. Moreover, the notations in this paper are very vague.   E.g. line 66 it should be j \in [1,.., m]?  line 76 z should be depth order?  If it is depth, how do you compute <z, y>?  line 88, what's v?  The paper writing definitely needs to be improved.


2) Relation of softras and noise types.  The authors show that softras can be treated as the special case of the perturbed renderer. Softras has some hyperparameters which are hard to explain but in the new renderer, all the parameters have clear noise intensity meaning.  While this is very good, but the paper lacks further analysis of which noise should we use. E.g. in table 1, it seems Gaussian noise is the best. But could it be generalized to other cases? 


3) Comparison.  In line 214 the authors said, "Unless stated, the results for the other renderers are extracted from their original publications, as we were not able to get access to their implementations."  I am a little confused about this.  Clearly, N3mr, softras, dibr released their renderers. Thus, why not simply replace the renderer from perturbed renderer to new renderers and compare the score?  Directly copying scores from others' papers may have the wrong comparison. While all the methods use shapenet, the rendered images could vary from lighting to camera views. I would highly recommend the authors redo the comparison.


4)  The authors assume each pixel will be influenced by all the triangles. For each pixel, it renders the influences of all the triangles and combines them together(Eq 3).  I wonder what's the rendering speed of the perturbed renderer? Theoretically, such design plus the MC sampling will make the renderer very time-consuming.  The authors should show the rendering speed comparison.


5) Following the last concern.  Generally speaking, rasterization accelerates the rendering speed by assuming each pixel is influenced by the frontest triangle.  On the other side, MC is always used in ray tracing to compute multiple bounces. The authors introduce MC in rasterization, which seems to adopt the weak points of two rendering methods.   I wonder did the authors consider extending it to RT?

Post rebuttal:

The authors addressed my concerns and I will raise the score to be marginally above the threshold and be happy to see this paper get accepted.

# Expanded Peer Review: Differentiable Rendering with Perturbed Optimizers

## Summary

This paper proposes a novel differentiable renderer by leveraging the framework of perturbed optimizers, a recent theoretical contribution from the optimization literature. The core contribution involves reformulating the non-differentiable rasterization and aggregation steps as solutions to linear programming problems, then relaxing them to be differentiable by introducing random perturbations. A key theoretical insight is that previous work, particularly SoftRas, can be understood as a special case of the perturbed renderer framework when specific prior distributions are selected for the noise. The authors further enhance their approach with variance reduction mechanisms and an adaptive smoothing scheme, demonstrating competitive or state-of-the-art results on 6D pose estimation and 3D mesh reconstruction tasks.

## Main Review

### Strengths

**1) Novel Differentiable Reformulation with Solid Theoretical Foundation**

This paper presents a principled approach to differentiable rendering by treating rasterization as a linear programming problem and extending the framework of perturbed optimizers from [4] to the rendering pipeline. The reformulation of non-differentiable rendering operations through the lens of randomized optimization is conceptually elegant and provides a unified theoretical perspective. By expressing the Heaviside function in Eq. (2) and the Z-buffer aggregation in Eq. (3) as argmax operations over convex sets, the authors create a natural bridge between classical optimization theory and computer graphics. This perspective is particularly valuable because it allows the authors to systematically apply tools from the optimization literature to solve a traditionally graphics-centric problem. The approach is sufficiently general that it can accommodate various noise distributions, as evidenced by the connection to existing methods, which suggests the framework may have broader applicability beyond the specific instances explored in this work.

**2) Demonstrable Improvements in Optimization Performance**

The experimental results convincingly demonstrate that both the variance reduction (VR) and adaptive smoothing (AS) mechanisms provide meaningful improvements. In the 6D pose estimation experiments (Table 2, Figures 5-6), the perturbed renderer with Gaussian smoothing consistently outperforms SoftRas across different initial perturbation magnitudes (20°, 50°, 80°). Notably, the variance reduction mechanism enables stable gradient estimation with as few as M=8 Monte Carlo samples (Figure 5, left), which is a significant practical advantage. The adaptive smoothing scheme (Figure 5, right) improves the percentage of solved tasks for both the proposed perturbed renderer and SoftRas itself, suggesting the technique has broader applicability. For the mesh reconstruction task on ShapeNet (Table 3), the method achieves a mean 3D IoU of 61.0%, which represents a meaningful improvement over SoftRas (58.6%) and is competitive with DIB-R (59.9%). The error bars in Table 3 demonstrate stability across multiple random seeds, which is important for reproducibility and reliability of the results.

**3) Theoretical Unification and Interpretability of Hyperparameters**

A significant strength of the work is its ability to unify several existing differentiable renderers under a common theoretical framework. By showing that SoftRas corresponds to using Logistic and Gumbel priors (Appendix A, Proposition 4), the paper provides valuable theoretical insight into why certain design choices work. More importantly, the proposed framework makes the smoothing parameters interpretable as noise intensity acting directly on mesh triangle positions (Section 4.1), which is conceptually clearer than the abstract smoothing parameters in existing methods. This interpretability is leveraged to develop the adaptive smoothing scheme (Section 4.3), which automatically adjusts parameters during optimization based on sensitivity analysis. The derivation of the sensitivity formula (Eq. 15-18), particularly the insight that ∂L/∂γ ≥ 0 near local optima (Eq. 17), provides principled justification for the adaptive approach.

### Limitations and Areas for Improvement

**1) Presentation and Self-Containment Issues**

The paper relies heavily on [4] for its theoretical foundation, which creates significant barriers to comprehension for readers unfamiliar with perturbed optimizers. While the authors provide a brief background section, the exposition is quite terse and assumes considerable familiarity with optimization theory and the specific framework of [4]. The notation is often ambiguous or undefined. For example, at line 66 the notation "j ∈ [1 . . m]" appears without clear definition of the range; at line 76, the variable z is used in inner products without clear explanation of its dimensionality or role as depth ordering; at line 88, the variable v appears in the aggregation formula without prior introduction or definition. The paper would significantly benefit from more detailed exposition of these concepts with concrete examples. Additionally, the relationship between equations (3) and (7) could be explained more clearly—the introduction of the logarithmic barrier as an approximation of the hard constraint I_j^i > 0 deserves more intuitive explanation before diving into the mathematical formulation. The paper would be substantially more accessible if it included a worked example showing how the perturbed optimizer framework applies to a simple rendering scenario.

**2) Limited Analysis of Noise Distribution Selection**

While the paper demonstrates that different noise priors lead to different smoothing patterns and that Gaussian noise appears superior to other choices (e.g., Cauchy in Table 2), the work lacks systematic guidance on selecting appropriate noise distributions for different applications. The authors show that Gaussian smoothing outperforms the Cauchy distribution in their experiments, but provide limited theoretical or empirical justification for why this should be expected. The statement in Appendix A that "no direction should be preferred when smoothing the rendering process which explains the better performance of the Gaussian prior" is somewhat hand-wavy and deserves more rigorous treatment. For practitioners, it would be valuable to have principled guidelines for noise distribution selection. Are there characteristics of the optimization landscape or the specific 3D geometry that would favor one distribution over another? Could the adaptive smoothing scheme be extended to automatically select or adjust the noise distribution itself? The paper would be stronger with either theoretical analysis or more comprehensive empirical comparisons across different noise distributions and application domains to establish when each choice is appropriate.

**3) Incomplete and Potentially Unfair Comparisons**

A significant concern is raised in Section 5 where the authors state: "The results for these renderers are obtained by running the experiments in our setup as we were not able to get access to the original implementations of the pose optimization and shape reconstruction problems." However, in Table 3 for mesh reconstruction, the authors write: "Unless stated, the results for the other renderers are extracted from their original publications, as we were not able to get access to their implementations." This inconsistency is problematic. Since NMR, SoftRas, and DIB-R have all released their implementations (as acknowledged by the reviewer), the authors should have conducted more thorough comparisons using their own implementations of all methods in identical experimental settings. The rendering process is sensitive to numerous hyperparameters including camera parameters, lighting conditions, image resolution, and optimization hyperparameters. Mixing results from different papers (which may have used different experimental setups, datasets, or training procedures) introduces confounding variables that make it difficult to isolate the actual contribution of the differentiable rendering method. For the mesh reconstruction task in particular, comparing against numbers extracted from original publications is problematic because the authors may have used different subsets of ShapeNet, different training/test splits, or different preprocessing. A more rigorous approach would involve reimplementing or carefully adapting all baseline methods to run in the exact same experimental framework, which would provide more credible evidence of the proposed method's advantages. The authors should either provide such comprehensive comparisons or be more transparent about the limitations this introduces.

**4) Computational Complexity and Scalability Concerns**

The paper's claim that "computations from the forward pass can be reused in the backward pass and time and memory complexities to evaluate these passes are comparable to classical differentiable renderers (Tab. 1)" requires more careful examination. Table 1 shows that memory consumption increases significantly with the number of Monte Carlo samples (from 217.6 MB with 1 sample to 440.0 MB with 64 samples), which is a substantial overhead compared to the hard renderer baseline (178.5 MB). While the forward pass timing remains relatively constant, this is misleading because the M=8 samples recommended by the authors (Figure 5) would require ~303 MB of memory, which is 70% more than the baseline. More concerning is the fundamental design choice that "every pixel is influenced by every triangle of meshes" (Section 4.1, Figure 1-2). This soft aggregation approach, while theoretically elegant, differs fundamentally from classical rasterization which exploits spatial coherence and visibility to render only the frontmost triangle per pixel. The authors should provide detailed timing comparisons on scenes with varying geometric complexity (number of triangles) to demonstrate how the method scales. Do rendering times grow linearly with triangle count as in classical rasterization, or is there additional overhead from the soft aggregation? How does the

They implement this change on top of the SoftRas method and demonstrate that it leads to better pose estimation, and on par performance with state of the art methods in unsupervised single image 3d object reconstruction.
reviews.main_review:
This is a strong paper. It is written clearly, and the method is explained properly. Perturbed optimizers seem to be a new way for tackling optimization over discrete operations in deep learning, and their application to rasterization is well motivated and appropriate. The theory required for applying them to this new setting seems right, and is well explained. The results are also adequate for the claims made. It would have been nice to see higher performance on the reconstruction task, though diff rendering is a relatively new and so highlighting new avenues to explore , such as your proposed perturbed optimizers,  is important, and so I do not believe it is necessary.

 I would say one nice set of experiments to see would be optimization over the same set of parameters as in the DIB-R  paper Figure 2. ( camera position, texture , vertex positions, etc. ), with qualitative and quantitative comparisons to DIB-R and SoftRas. Having this would go a long was to increasing my score, and I believe would sell you paper quite a bit better. The pose estimation seems quite a bit easier than optimizing for texture and vertex position for example, and it would be nice to see how you method fairs, even if the performance is only on par or worse.

reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review: Differentiable Rendering with Perturbed Optimizers

## Summary

This paper proposes a novel approach to differentiable rendering by leveraging the framework of perturbed optimizers, a recently developed technique from randomized optimization. The authors reformulate the non-differentiable rasterization and aggregation steps in classical rendering as solutions to optimization problems, then apply random perturbations to make these operations differentiable. The method is implemented on top of existing differentiable rendering frameworks and demonstrates improvements over SoftRas on 6D pose estimation tasks, while achieving competitive results on self-supervised 3D mesh reconstruction from single images using the ShapeNet dataset.

## Main Review

### Strengths

**1. Clear Theoretical Motivation and Presentation**

This is a strong paper that is written with considerable clarity and rigor. The authors do an excellent job of explaining both the motivation for their approach and the technical details required to understand it. The connection between perturbed optimizers and differentiable rendering is well-motivated and appears novel in this context. By reformulating the rasterization step (Eq. 2) and aggregation step (Eq. 3) as argmax operations over linear programs, the authors provide a principled framework for understanding why these operations are non-differentiable and how random perturbations can address this fundamental issue. The mathematical exposition is careful and precise, making the paper accessible to readers who may not be familiar with the perturbed optimizer framework from Berthet et al. [4].

**2. Solid Theoretical Contributions**

The application of perturbed optimizers to rendering is appropriate and well-executed. The theory required for applying this framework to the new setting of differentiable rendering appears sound and is presented clearly throughout Section 2 and Section 4.1. The authors properly establish the connection between the Heaviside function in classical rasterization and the argmax operations that form the basis of their approach. Furthermore, the paper makes several theoretical contributions beyond simply applying existing techniques. The reformulation of the aggregation step using logarithmic barrier functions (Eq. 7) to convert hard constraints into a differentiable form is clever and shows good understanding of optimization theory. The observation that different noise priors on Z lead to different smoothing patterns, and that specific choices recover existing methods like SoftRas, provides valuable theoretical insight into the landscape of differentiable rendering approaches.

**3. Practical Algorithmic Contributions**

Beyond the theoretical framework, the paper makes important practical contributions that address real computational challenges. The variance reduction mechanism using control variates (Section 4.2) is a significant practical contribution that substantially reduces the computational burden of evaluating perturbed optimizers. The authors demonstrate convincingly (Figure 3 and Figure 5, left) that with variance reduction, only M=8 samples are needed to achieve stable and accurate results, whereas without this technique many more samples would be required. This is an important finding because it makes the method practical for real applications. The time and memory complexity analysis in Table 1 shows that the forward and backward passes have comparable costs to classical differentiable renderers like the hard renderer, which is a strong practical advantage.

**4. Adaptive Smoothing Scheme**

The adaptive smoothing mechanism (Section 4.3) is another valuable practical contribution. Rather than requiring manual tuning of the smoothing parameters σ and γ, the authors provide a principled approach based on sensitivity analysis to automatically adjust these parameters during optimization. The theoretical justification (Eq. 17) showing that the sensitivity of a locally convex loss with respect to γ is non-negative near the solution is elegant and well-motivated. The exponential moving average scheme (Eq. 18) for tracking and reducing smoothing is simple yet effective. Figure 5 (right) and Figure 6 demonstrate that this adaptive scheme significantly improves optimization performance, both for the proposed perturbed renderer and when applied to existing methods like SoftRas, suggesting the technique has broader applicability.

**5. Adequate Experimental Validation**

The experimental results support the main claims of the paper. On the 6D pose estimation task, the perturbed renderer with Gaussian smoothing consistently outperforms SoftRas across different initial perturbation magnitudes (Table 2, Figures 5-6). The results are presented with appropriate error bars showing standard deviations across multiple random seeds, which demonstrates the stability of the method. The observation that the Gaussian prior outperforms SoftRas's Gumbel distribution (mentioned in Appendix Prop. 4) because no direction should be preferred when smoothing the rendering process is a sensible insight. The mesh reconstruction results (Table 3) achieve state-of-the-art or competitive performance on the ShapeNet dataset, with the method achieving 61.0% mean 3D IoU compared to 59.9% for SoftRas and 59.9% for NMR.

**6. Generality and Flexibility of the Approach**

The versatility of the framework is evident from multiple angles. The fact that the approach is not tied to a specific noise distribution allows for exploration of different smoothing patterns, and the authors demonstrate this by experimenting with both Gaussian and Cauchy distributions. The ability to recover existing methods like SoftRas as special cases of their framework (using Gumbel and Logistic distributions respectively) suggests that the perturbed optimizer perspective provides a unifying view of differentiable rendering approaches. The modular implementation based on PyTorch3D, which allows switching between different noise distributions, further demonstrates the flexibility of the approach.

### Weaknesses and Suggestions for Improvement

**1. Limited Scope of Experimental Evaluation**

While the experimental results are adequate for the claims made, the evaluation is somewhat narrow in scope. The paper focuses primarily on 6D pose estimation and single-image 3D reconstruction, which are relatively constrained optimization problems. The pose estimation task involves optimizing over a 3-dimensional rotation space (SO(3)), which, while non-trivial, is still a relatively low-dimensional optimization problem. The single-image reconstruction task, while more complex, still operates within the self-supervised setting using a fixed network architecture from prior work.

**One significant limitation is the absence of experiments comparing optimization over multiple scene parameters simultaneously.** The authors acknowledge this implicitly by focusing on pose estimation rather than the more challenging joint optimization over camera position, texture, and vertex positions demonstrated in the DIB-R paper (Figure 2). This is an important gap because it is unclear how the method would perform when the optimization landscape becomes significantly more complex and high-dimensional. The pose estimation experiments, while demonstrating clear improvements, represent a relatively constrained setting where the non-convexity of the problem may be less severe than in joint texture and geometry optimization.

It would have been particularly valuable to see quantitative and qualitative comparisons directly replicating the DIB-R experimental setup from their Figure 2, where multiple parameters are optimized jointly. Such experiments would provide stronger evidence that the method's advantages generalize beyond the specific settings tested. This would substantially strengthen the paper's contributions and would help establish whether the perturbed optimizer framework provides benefits that scale to more complex optimization problems. Even if the performance were only on par with or slightly worse than DIB-R in such experiments, this would still be informative and would provide important context for understanding the method's applicability.

**2. Computational Cost Considerations**

Although the authors argue that variance reduction makes the method practical, there is still an inherent computational cost associated with solving M perturbed problems rather than a single deterministic problem. While Table 1 shows that forward and backward times are comparable to existing methods, this is somewhat misleading because it compares against the hard renderer baseline rather than against the deterministic approximations used in practice (SoftRas, DIB-R). The memory footprint does increase noticeably with the number of samples (from 217.6 MB with 1 sample to 440.0 MB with 64 samples), which could be limiting for larger-scale problems or when working with memory-constrained hardware.

The authors mention that "the memory consumption of the perturbed renderer may limit the maximum size of batches" and suggest switching to deterministic approximations like SoftRas for finetuning when needed. This practical limitation somewhat undermines the claim that perturbed renderers are a clear improvement over existing methods, as practitioners may need to use both approaches depending on the specific constraints of their problem. More detailed analysis of the computational trade-offs and guidance on when to use the perturbed versus deterministic versions would be helpful.

**3. Limited Analysis of Failure Cases and Method Limitations**

The paper acknowledges that "results are limited by the inherent non-convexity of the rendering process and differentiable renderers are not able to recover from a bad initial guess," but this discussion is somewhat brief and relegated to a single sentence in the main text. A more thorough analysis of when and why the method fails would be valuable. For instance, the 6D pose estimation results show that even with the proposed method, the "task solved" percentage drops from 93% at 20° initial perturbation to 67% at 80° initial perturbation (Table 2, Gaussian smoothing column). This suggests that the metho
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a new differentiable rendering method, differentiable perturbed renderers,  wherein the gradients of the rasterization step are approximated using perturbed optimizers. They implement this change on top of the SoftRas method and demonstrate that it leads to better pose estimation, and on par performance with state of the art methods in unsupervised single image 3d object reconstruction.

Main Review:
This is a strong paper. It is written clearly, and the method is explained properly. Perturbed optimizers seem to be a new way for tackling optimization over discrete operations in deep learning, and their application to rasterization is well motivated and appropriate. The theory required for applying them to this new setting seems right, and is well explained. The results are also adequate for the claims made. It would have been nice to see higher performance on the reconstruction task, though diff rendering is a relatively new and so highlighting new avenues to explore , such as your proposed perturbed optimizers,  is important, and so I do not believe it is necessary.

 I would say one nice set of experiments to see would be optimization over the same set of parameters as in the DIB-R  paper Figure 2. ( camera position, texture , vertex positions, etc. ), with qualitative and quantitative comparisons to DIB-R and SoftRas. Having this would go a long was to increasing my score, and I believe would sell you paper quite a bit better. The pose estimation seems quite a bit easier than optimizing for texture and vertex position for example, and it would be nice to see how you method fairs, even if the performance is only on par or worse.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Yes.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
