title: Hierarchical Prototype Network for Continual Graph Representation Learning

reviews.summary: This paper introduces continual learning in graph settings, where new graph-based tasks come in over time. To learn effective node classifiers for new tasks without forgetting the old tasks, a three-level prototype network is outlined. Atomic, node, and class prototypes are jointly used on top of Atomic Feature Extractors, where only the relevant features and prototypes are selected and updated in each new task, without disturbing the others. Experiments on eight benchmarks show the effectiveness and lack of forgetting for continual node classification.
reviews.main_review:
[Strengths]

This paper addresses a challenging task in graph learning, namely the scenario where new graph tasks come in over time. The proposed multi-level approach is interesting at a high level as it addresses different levels of granularity.

The paper furthermore excels at outlining current advances in continual learning and specifically in the graph context. This outline helps not only with providing the proper background and starting point of this paper, but also helps to understand what is new here.

The presented results show that the approach not only obtains a high accuracy overall, but at the cost of minimal loss of accuracy with the introduction of new tasks. These results highlight the potential of the proposed approach.

[Weaknesses]

There are a number of limitations to the paper that require addressing.

First, the method is not written well and unnecessarily hard to go through. Section 2 contains many unintuitive steps and unclear notations that make it hard to understand what is going on. For example:
- What is the idea behind subsampling the neighbourhood in lines 125-127?
- According to Equation 3, the node v is represented by a set of features, both from node and structure information. Why are these two sets combined in Equation 3 and separated again in Equation 5? And how do you go from AFE_{node}^{select} and AFE_{struct}^{select} to \mathbb{E}_A^{select}(v)?  There is no explanation and completely unclear.
- What is the point of Algorithm 1? It contains so few steps that there is no information anymore. Steps 4 and 5 are the same too.
- Where does \mathbb{A}(v) magically come from in line 178? What does associated with mean? And how big is the set  for each node? Again not clear from the method section.
- The N-prototypes are obtained from \mathbb{A}(v), and C-prototypes are obtained from N-prototypes. What does Equation 11 mean in that case? How can you throw them on one pile?
- At the end, there is a cross-entropy objective in Equation 12, where the likelihoods are obtained from a fully-connected layers that takes inputs from all levels of prototypes. Don’t the prototypes have different dimensionalities however? What is going on on lines 193 and 195?

Next to the mathematical explanation, the writing also requires improvement. A few examples from Section 2.4 and 2.5 (not a full list):
- Line 192: pass = passed
- Line 203: unlike topology-aware = unlike the topology-aware
- Line 204: HPNs do not require task indicator = HPNs not require a task indicator
- Line 228: is = denotes the / are the

Section 2.5 provides two theorems, but the appendix only provides a proof for Theorem 2. Where is the proof for Theorem 1?

In the experiments, Table 1 shows that the proposed approach outperforms all other approaches. Where do the numbers of the baselines come from? Baseline [39] should btw be updated to:

[a] Zhou, Fan, and Chengtai Cao. "Overcoming Catastrophic Forgetting in Graph Neural Networks with Experience Replay." Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence. Vol. 35. No. 5. 2021.

In [a], they report a best PM of 95.66% and 81.83% on Cora and Citeseer, compared to 93.7% and 79.0% for this paper. Notably, [39] obtains only 71.1% and 65.5%. How come there are such big differences between what is reported in the original paper and what is reported in Table 1? Table 1 suggest that the proposed approach is far better than the baselines, while a direct comparison between papers suggests otherwise.

[Overall]

This paper addresses a challenging task with a multi-level approach. The method is intuitive at a high level and the related work is outlined well. The method section itself can however hardly be followed and requires big changes. Experimentally, there are also open questions that require attention regarding reported numbers.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: The main limitation that is discussed is that only node classification is investigated. The checklist states that there are no negative societal impacts of the work.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review: Hierarchical Prototype Networks for Continual Graph Representation Learning

## Summary

This paper introduces a novel framework for continual learning in graph settings, where new graph-based tasks containing different node categories arrive sequentially over time. To enable effective node classification on new tasks without catastrophic forgetting of previously learned tasks, the authors propose Hierarchical Prototype Networks (HPNs). The approach combines Atomic Feature Extractors (AFEs) with a three-level prototype architecture comprising atomic-level prototypes (A-prototypes), node-level prototypes (N-prototypes), and class-level prototypes (C-prototypes). A key design principle is that only the relevant features and prototypes are selected and updated for each new task, while irrelevant components remain undisturbed. Experiments on eight benchmark datasets demonstrate the method's effectiveness in achieving high accuracy while minimizing performance degradation on previously learned tasks. The authors also provide theoretical analysis on memory consumption bounds and continual learning capability.

## Main Review

### [Strengths]

**Novel Problem Formulation and Motivation**: This paper addresses a genuinely challenging and practical problem in graph neural networks—continual learning with emerging node categories—which has received limited attention despite its relevance to real-world applications. The motivation is well-articulated through concrete examples such as citation networks with emerging research areas and e-commerce networks with new product categories. This problem formulation is distinct from dynamic graph learning, which the authors appropriately differentiate, and represents a meaningful contribution to the continual learning literature.

**Comprehensive Background and Context**: The paper excels in providing a thorough exposition of current advances in continual learning, both in general machine learning and specifically in the graph context. The authors clearly delineate three main categories of continual learning approaches—regularization-based, memory-replay-based, and parametric isolation-based methods—and explain why existing approaches are unsuitable for continual graph representation learning. This contextual foundation helps readers understand the paper's positioning and the specific challenges that HPNs aim to address. The discussion of why existing continual learning methods fail to appropriately handle both feature information and topological structure is particularly insightful.

**Strong Empirical Results**: The experimental results are impressive, demonstrating that HPNs achieve high accuracy while exhibiting minimal catastrophic forgetting across eight diverse datasets. Particularly notable is the performance on large-scale datasets like OGB-Products, which contains over 2 million nodes and 47 categories. The introduction of the Average Retaining Score (ARS) metric in Section 3.5 provides a more nuanced way to evaluate continual learning performance by isolating forgetting effects from task-specific accuracy variations. The consistent positive or near-zero forgetting mean (FM) values across datasets suggest that the approach genuinely mitigates catastrophic forgetting rather than simply achieving good average performance.

**Memory Efficiency**: A significant practical advantage of HPNs is their remarkable parameter efficiency. As demonstrated in Table 4 and Figure 3, HPNs achieve competitive performance on OGB-Products with only thousands of parameters, compared to hundreds of thousands or millions for baseline methods. This dramatic difference in memory footprint, combined with the theoretical upper bound analysis in Theorem 1, positions HPNs as a practical solution for resource-constrained settings and large-scale graph applications.

**Theoretical Contributions**: The paper provides formal theoretical analysis with Theorem 1 (upper bounds on prototype numbers) and Theorem 2 (task distance preserving conditions). These theorems offer insights into the relationship between model configuration parameters (number of AFEs, prototype dimensions, similarity thresholds) and the model's continual learning capacity. The explicit computation and empirical verification of these bounds in Section 3.7 demonstrates a commendable effort to ground theoretical insights in practical settings.

**Comprehensive Experimental Evaluation**: The experimental section is thorough, addressing six key research questions through various analyses. The ablation studies (Tables 2 and 3) systematically evaluate the contribution of different prototype levels and loss terms. The sensitivity analysis (Figure 2) examines the impact of key hyperparameters, and the learning dynamics analysis (Figure 3 left) provides compelling evidence that HPNs maintain performance better than baselines as more tasks arrive. The visualization in Figure 4 offers interpretable evidence that learned representations remain well-separated across tasks.

### [Weaknesses]

**Presentation and Clarity Issues**: The method section is dense and difficult to follow, with numerous notational inconsistencies and unexplained design choices that significantly impede comprehension. The presentation issues are substantial enough to warrant detailed enumeration:

- **Neighborhood Sampling Rationale (Lines 125-127)**: The paper uniformly samples a fixed number of vertices from 1-hop to h-hop neighborhoods to obtain N_sub(v), but provides no justification for this design choice. Why is uniform sampling preferred over importance-based or degree-aware sampling? How sensitive is the method to the sampling size? The paper does not discuss these critical implementation details, leaving readers uncertain about the necessity of this design decision.

- **Inconsistent Feature Combination and Selection (Equations 1-3 and 5)**: The notation creates confusion about the relationship between different sets. Equation 3 combines E^node_A(v) and E^struct_A(v) into a unified set E_A(v), yet Equation 5 appears to operate on these sets separately as E^id_A(v) where id ∈ {node, struct}. The transition from the combined representation in Equation 3 to the separated treatment in Equation 5 is unexplained. Furthermore, the connection between AFE_node and E^node_A(v) is stated as a straightforward application of projection matrices, but the computational process—particularly how multiple AFEs generate multiple embeddings—could be explained more explicitly. The paper states that "each AFE is used to generate an embedding," but it remains unclear whether each AFE generates a single embedding or multiple embeddings, and how dimensionality is managed.

- **Unclear AFE Selection Mechanism**: The process of selecting AFE^select_node and AFE^select_struct from the full sets is described algorithmically but lacks intuitive explanation. Why is similarity to existing A-prototypes the right criterion for AFE selection? Could this create a bias toward selecting AFEs that match existing prototypes rather than those that best represent the current node? The paper does not discuss potential limitations of this greedy selection strategy.

- **Ambiguous Prototype Assignment (Line 178 and beyond)**: The notation A(v) suddenly appears in line 178 with the phrase "each element in E^select_A(v) is assigned a closest A-prototype according to SimE→A(v), and each node is associated with a set of atomic prototypes A(v)." This is the first mention of how A(v) is constructed, yet it appears without formal definition. The phrase "associated with" is vague—does this mean each atomic embedding is matched to exactly one A-prototype, or can multiple embeddings map to the same prototype? How is the cardinality of A(v) determined for each node? These details are essential for understanding the method but are left implicit.

- **Hierarchical Prototype Aggregation (Equation 11)**: The statement that "N(v) and C(v) only contain one N-prototype and one C-prototype" while A(v) contains multiple prototypes creates a notational inconsistency when these are combined in Equation 11 as P_H(v) = A(v) ∪ N(v) ∪ C(v). The use of set union notation is problematic because these are objects of fundamentally different types and multiplicities. If N(v) and C(v) are singleton sets, this should be made explicit. Furthermore, the hierarchical relationship between levels—where N-prototypes are derived from A(v) and C-prototypes from N-prototypes—is unclear when all three are presented as separate components in the final representation. How is the information from different levels combined in practice?

- **Final Classification Layer (Lines 193-195, Equation 12)**: The paper states that "the obtained hierarchical prototypes for each node are first concatenated into a unified vector and then pass through a fully connected layer FC." However, the prototypes at different levels have different dimensionalities (d_a, d_n, d_c), as established earlier in the paper. The concatenation operation implicitly assumes these can be joined into a single vector, but the paper does not explain how dimensionality mismatches are handled or whether padding/alignment is necessary. The notation FC(h_1 ⊕ · · · ⊕ h_{l'_a+l'_r+2}) suggests concatenation of l'_a + l'_r + 2 vectors, but it is unclear whether this counts the single N-prototype and single C-prototype correctly. If N(v) and C(v) each contain one prototype, the total should be l'_a + 1 + 1, not l'_a + l'_r + 2.

- **Algorithm 1 Inadequacy**: Algorithm 1 is too
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper introduces continual learning in graph settings, where new graph-based tasks come in over time. To learn effective node classifiers for new tasks without forgetting the old tasks, a three-level prototype network is outlined. Atomic, node, and class prototypes are jointly used on top of Atomic Feature Extractors, where only the relevant features and prototypes are selected and updated in each new task, without disturbing the others. Experiments on eight benchmarks show the effectiveness and lack of forgetting for continual node classification.

Main Review:
[Strengths]

This paper addresses a challenging task in graph learning, namely the scenario where new graph tasks come in over time. The proposed multi-level approach is interesting at a high level as it addresses different levels of granularity.

The paper furthermore excels at outlining current advances in continual learning and specifically in the graph context. This outline helps not only with providing the proper background and starting point of this paper, but also helps to understand what is new here.

The presented results show that the approach not only obtains a high accuracy overall, but at the cost of minimal loss of accuracy with the introduction of new tasks. These results highlight the potential of the proposed approach.

[Weaknesses]

There are a number of limitations to the paper that require addressing.

First, the method is not written well and unnecessarily hard to go through. Section 2 contains many unintuitive steps and unclear notations that make it hard to understand what is going on. For example:
- What is the idea behind subsampling the neighbourhood in lines 125-127?
- According to Equation 3, the node v is represented by a set of features, both from node and structure information. Why are these two sets combined in Equation 3 and separated again in Equation 5? And how do you go from AFE_{node}^{select} and AFE_{struct}^{select} to \mathbb{E}_A^{select}(v)?  There is no explanation and completely unclear.
- What is the point of Algorithm 1? It contains so few steps that there is no information anymore. Steps 4 and 5 are the same too.
- Where does \mathbb{A}(v) magically come from in line 178? What does associated with mean? And how big is the set  for each node? Again not clear from the method section.
- The N-prototypes are obtained from \mathbb{A}(v), and C-prototypes are obtained from N-prototypes. What does Equation 11 mean in that case? How can you throw them on one pile?
- At the end, there is a cross-entropy objective in Equation 12, where the likelihoods are obtained from a fully-connected layers that takes inputs from all levels of prototypes. Don’t the prototypes have different dimensionalities however? What is going on on lines 193 and 195?

Next to the mathematical explanation, the writing also requires improvement. A few examples from Section 2.4 and 2.5 (not a full list):
- Line 192: pass = passed
- Line 203: unlike topology-aware = unlike the topology-aware
- Line 204: HPNs do not require task indicator = HPNs not require a task indicator
- Line 228: is = denotes the / are the

Section 2.5 provides two theorems, but the appendix only provides a proof for Theorem 2. Where is the proof for Theorem 1?

In the experiments, Table 1 shows that the proposed approach outperforms all other approaches. Where do the numbers of the baselines come from? Baseline [39] should btw be updated to:

[a] Zhou, Fan, and Chengtai Cao. "Overcoming Catastrophic Forgetting in Graph Neural Networks with Experience Replay." Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence. Vol. 35. No. 5. 2021.

In [a], they report a best PM of 95.66% and 81.83% on Cora and Citeseer, compared to 93.7% and 79.0% for this paper. Notably, [39] obtains only 71.1% and 65.5%. How come there are such big differences between what is reported in the original paper and what is reported in Table 1? Table 1 suggest that the proposed approach is far better than the baselines, while a direct comparison between papers suggests otherwise.

[Overall]

This paper addresses a challenging task with a multi-level approach. The method is intuitive at a high level and the related work is outlined well. The method section itself can however hardly be followed and requires big changes. Experimentally, there are also open questions that require attention regarding reported numbers.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The main limitation that is discussed is that only node classification is investigated. The checklist states that there are no negative societal impacts of the work.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper aims to address the catastrophic forgetting issue with continual learning on streaming graphs. To comprehensively incorporate the feature and topological information of new nodes, this paper proposes atomic feature extractors to project raw node features and neighbor nodes into distinct base embeddings, which are then used to refine or compose atomic-, node-, and class-level prototype embeddings in a hierarchical manner. Experiments demonstrate that by doing so, catastrophic forgetting can be avoided and promising accuracy can be achieved on continual graph learning tasks.
reviews.main_review:
The highlights of this paper are as follows:
1. The proposed approach is well motivated, where the compositional node embeddings based on hierarchical prototypes is able to make the approach scalable to larger datasets.
2. Despite some syntax errors, the presentation of this paper is overall clear and easy to follow.
3. The experimental evaluations are thorough, spanning across a wide range of heterogeneous graph datasets, and most technical claims are backed by the results.

At the same time, several shortcomings of this paper are also noticed, which are listed below:
1. The parameter-saving claim only holds when the input node features do not include node IDs, where the AFEs only need to perform transformations on the low-dimensional feature vectors. In some other graph learning settings where node IDs are used as the input feature, AFEs will essentially be multiple node embedding tables where $d_v$ is the total number of nodes, which will dominate the parameter size and become less scalable than other methods that only maintain one node embedding table.
2. Equations 5,6,7,8 need 4 repetitive computations of pairwise cosine similarity between atomic features and the prototype embeddings, which can be very time-consuming given the potential size of set $\mathbb{P}$. Is the approach able to reuse results from previous steps (e.g., Eq 5) to cut the time cost?
3. The atomic features are refined by a hard selection process with pre-defined threshold. While the learning process seems to be end-to-end in Algorithm 1, it remains unclear how the hard selection of atomic features is made differentiable to allow for end-to-end training. 
4. The overall learning objective directly combines three loss functions without any coefficients to balance any part of the sub-losses, which is uncommon. It is expected to provide more details and justifications on doing so.
5. As mentioned above, several syntax errors are found in this paper:
- Incomplete sentences: Figure 1 --> “Given a node v”; Section 2.3 --> “To map … prototype presentations.”; 
- Section 2.2., “they are be uncorrelated” --> are uncorrelated
- Section 2.3, “HPNs then starts to” --> start
- Section 2.3, “a N-prototype” --> an

In summary, the highlights of this paper outweighs its shortcomings, while this paper can still benefit from some clarifications on the issues listed above.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: The limitations of this paper are mainly associated with the lack of detailed explanations w.r.t. some technical claims, and the authors are recommended to provide more corresponding justifications.

reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

This paper aims to address the catastrophic forgetting issue with continual learning on streaming graphs. To comprehensively incorporate the feature and topological information of new nodes, this paper proposes atomic feature extractors to project raw node features and neighbor nodes into distinct base embeddings, which are then used to refine or compose atomic-, node-, and class-level prototype embeddings in a hierarchical manner. Experiments demonstrate that by doing so, catastrophic forgetting can be avoided and promising accuracy can be achieved on continual graph learning tasks.

Main Review:
The highlights of this paper are as follows:
1. The proposed approach is well motivated, where the compositional node embeddings based on hierarchical prototypes is able to make the approach scalable to larger datasets.
2. Despite some syntax errors, the presentation of this paper is overall clear and easy to follow.
3. The experimental evaluations are thorough, spanning across a wide range of heterogeneous graph datasets, and most technical claims are backed by the results.

At the same time, several shortcomings of this paper are also noticed, which are listed below:
1. The parameter-saving claim only holds when the input node features do not include node IDs, where the AFEs only need to perform transformations on the low-dimensional feature vectors. In some other graph learning settings where node IDs are used as the input feature, AFEs will essentially be multiple node embedding tables where $d_v$ is the total number of nodes, which will dominate the parameter size and become less scalable than other methods that only maintain one node embedding table.
2. Equations 5,6,7,8 need 4 repetitive computations of pairwise cosine similarity between atomic features and the prototype embeddings, which can be very time-consuming given the potential size of set $\mathbb{P}$. Is the approach able to reuse results from previous steps (e.g., Eq 5) to cut the time cost?
3. The atomic features are refined by a hard selection process with pre-defined threshold. While the learning process seems to be end-to-end in Algorithm 1, it remains unclear how the hard selection of atomic features is made differentiable to allow for end-to-end training. 
4. The overall learning objective directly combines three loss functions without any coefficients to balance any part of the sub-losses, which is uncommon. It is expected to provide more details and justifications on doing so.
5. As mentioned above, several syntax errors are found in this paper:
- Incomplete sentences: Figure 1 --> “Given a node v”; Section 2.3 --> “To map … prototype presentations.”; 
- Section 2.2., “they are be uncorrelated” --> are uncorrelated
- Section 2.3, “HPNs then starts to” --> start
- Section 2.3, “a N-prototype” --> an

In summary, the highlights of this paper outweighs its shortcomings, while this paper can still benefit from some clarifications on the issues listed above.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The limitations of this paper are mainly associated with the lack of detailed explanations w.r.t. some technical claims, and the authors are recommended to provide more corresponding justifications.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review: Hierarchical Prototype Networks for Continual Graph Representation Learning

## Summary

This paper addresses the important problem of catastrophic forgetting in continual learning on streaming graphs, where new categories of nodes and their associated edges emerge continuously. To comprehensively incorporate both feature and topological information of new nodes, the paper proposes Hierarchical Prototype Networks (HPNs), which employ Atomic Feature Extractors (AFEs) to project raw node features and sampled neighbor nodes into distinct base embeddings. These atomic embeddings are then selectively refined or composed into atomic-, node-, and class-level prototype embeddings organized in a hierarchical manner. The experimental results demonstrate that this approach can effectively avoid catastrophic forgetting while achieving competitive accuracy across multiple continual graph learning tasks, even on large-scale datasets with millions of nodes.

## Main Review

### Strengths

The highlights of this paper are substantial and warrant acceptance:

1. **Well-motivated approach with clear problem formulation**: The paper identifies a genuine gap in the literature by distinguishing continual graph representation learning from both static graph learning and dynamic graph learning. The motivation for why existing continual learning methods are insufficient for graphs is compelling—both feature information and topological structure must be appropriately considered simultaneously. The hierarchical prototype-based approach is well-motivated as it naturally allows for compositional representations at multiple levels of abstraction, analogous to feature hierarchies in convolutional neural networks. This compositional nature is key to achieving scalability on larger datasets while maintaining the ability to learn new node categories.

2. **Clear and accessible presentation overall**: Despite some minor syntax issues (discussed below), the paper is generally well-structured and easy to follow. The framework diagram (Figure 1) effectively illustrates the three-stage pipeline from atomic feature extraction through hierarchical prototype matching. The problem statement in Section 2.1 is clearly articulated with appropriate notation, and the progression from AFEs to hierarchical prototypes flows logically. The writing style is largely accessible to readers unfamiliar with the specific problem domain.

3. **Comprehensive and rigorous experimental evaluation**: The experimental section is notably thorough. The evaluation spans eight diverse datasets including citation networks (Cora, Citeseer, OGB-Arxiv), web page networks (Wisconsin, Cornell, Texas), an actor co-occurrence network, and a large-scale product co-purchasing network (OGB-Products with 2.4M nodes). The paper evaluates performance against both continual learning baselines adapted to graphs (EWC, LwF, GEM, MAS) and graph-specific continual learning methods (ERGNN, TWP), using three different backbone architectures (GCN, GAT, GIN). The introduction of the Average Retaining Score (ARS) metric in Section 3.5 is a thoughtful contribution that better isolates the forgetting phenomenon from task-specific performance variations. The ablation studies systematically validate design choices, and the visualization of learned representations (Figure 4) provides interpretability insights.

4. **Theoretical contributions with practical implications**: The paper provides two theoretical results that inform the design choices. Theorem 1 establishes an upper bound on memory consumption in terms of the number of prototypes, which depends on the spherical code dimension and similarity thresholds. Theorem 2 provides conditions under which task distance is preserved, preventing catastrophic forgetting. While these bounds are acknowledged to be loose, they provide useful guidance on the relationship between the number of AFEs, prototype dimensions, and the model's capacity to handle multiple tasks. The experimental section validates these theoretical insights, particularly in Section 3.6 where the relationship between the number of AFEs and prototype dimensions is empirically verified.

### Major Weaknesses

Several significant technical concerns require clarification:

1. **Parameter efficiency claims require important caveats regarding input representation**: The claim that HPNs are parameter-efficient only holds under specific assumptions about input node features. As noted in the paper, when node features are low-dimensional attribute vectors (gender, nationality, hobby, etc.), the AFEs operate as efficient linear transformations with parameter count O(d_v × d_a × l_a + d_v × d_r × l_r). However, this assumption is problematic in many real-world graph learning scenarios. In settings where node IDs are used as input features—a common practice in many graph neural network applications—d_v becomes the total number of nodes in the graph. For OGB-Products with 2.4 million nodes, each AFE would require a matrix of size 2.4M × 16, making the total parameter count prohibitive. This would fundamentally undermine the scalability advantages claimed in the paper. 

The paper should explicitly acknowledge this limitation and clarify the assumptions under which the parameter efficiency results hold. Specifically, the authors should discuss: (1) whether the method is designed for graphs with semantic node features, (2) how the approach would need to be modified for ID-based features, and (3) whether techniques like embedding tables could be integrated and how that would affect the parameter count. The statement in the contribution that HPNs achieve "around 80% accuracy with only thousands of parameters" on OGB-Products is potentially misleading if it implicitly assumes low-dimensional semantic features that may not be available in all application domains.

2. **Computational complexity of repeated similarity computations**: Equations 5, 6, 7, and 8 involve computing pairwise cosine similarities between atomic embeddings and prototype embeddings multiple times in sequence. Specifically:
   - Equation 5 computes similarities between all atomic embeddings and all A-prototypes to identify relevant AFEs
   - Equation 6 recomputes these similarities for the selected atomic embeddings
   - Equations 7 and 8 further refine these similarities to identify which embeddings match existing prototypes

Given that the prototype set P_A can grow dynamically as new tasks arrive (as per Equation 10), the size of P_A could potentially become large over many sequential tasks. The paper does not discuss the computational complexity of these operations or provide runtime comparisons with baseline methods. This is particularly concerning for the OGB-Products dataset with 2.4 million nodes and 47 tasks. The paper should:
   - Provide computational complexity analysis for the matching process as a function of the number of nodes, AFEs, and accumulated prototypes
   - Discuss whether intermediate results from Equation 5 can be reused in Equations 6-8 to reduce redundant computation
   - Report actual wall-clock training time comparisons with baselines
   - Analyze how runtime scales with the number of sequential tasks as prototypes accumulate

3. **Differentiability of hard selection mechanism in end-to-end training**: While Algorithm 1 presents the learning procedure as end-to-end, the AFE selection process (Equation 5) involves a hard selection step where the top l'_a and l'_r AFEs are chosen based on similarity rankings. This hard selection operation is not differentiable, which raises questions about how gradients flow during backpropagation. Specifically:
   - The selection in Equation 5 uses a sorting operation (sort in descending order) followed by a top-k selection, which are non-differentiable operations
   - The matching process in Equation 7 uses a threshold-based hard assignment (whether similarity ≥ t_A)
   - It is unclear whether the AFE matrices A_i and R_j are updated during training, and if so, how gradients are computed through these hard selection operations

The paper should provide detailed clarification on:
   - Whether gradients are computed with respect to the AFE parameters, and if so, how the hard selection is made differentiable (e.g., through straight-through estimators, soft selection mechanisms, or other techniques)
   - Whether the AFE selection is fixed after initialization or updated during training
   - How the divergence loss (Equation 4) is incorporated into the end-to-end training pipeline given the hard selection constraints

4. **Lack of principled loss weighting and justification**: The overall learning objective in Equation 13 directly sums three loss terms without any weighting coefficients: L = L_dis + L_div + L_cls. This is unusual in multi-objective learning and raises several concerns:
   - The three losses operate on different scales and units: L_cls is a cross-entropy loss, L_dis is a negative cosine similarity, and L_div is a sum of matrix products
   - Without explicit weighting, the relative contribution of each term to the overall optimization is not controlled and may be dominated by whichever term has the largest magnitude
   - Different datasets or tasks may require different balances between preserving old prototypes (L_dis), maintaining AFE diversity (L_div), and classification accuracy (L_cls)

The paper should:
   - Provide justification for why equal weighting is appropriate, or alternatively, introduce learnable or dataset-specific weights
   - Conduct ablation studies showing the sensitivity of performance to different weight configurations
   - Discuss how the relative magnitudes of these three losses are balanced during optimization
   - Provide guidance on how practitioners should set these weights for new applications

### Minor Weaknesses an

reviews.summary: This paper addresses graph representation learning in a particular online/continual/streaming setting in which subgraphs are sequentially recieved which may contain nodes of categories/classes that haven't been seen before, while performance on the previously seen categories/classes must be maintained.  The approach involves extracting "atomic" features based on node features and local network structure, then constructing and maintaining prototype embeddings at various levels of abstraction, in order to produce a final embedding to be used by a node classifier. In each iteration the method decides whether to introduce new prototypes based on matching to previous prototypes. The authors prove a bound on the number of prototypes (hence, memory requirements) and show that the method will avoid catastrophic forgetting. Experimental results versus various baselines are positive.
reviews.main_review:
Originality: While graph representation learning has been heavily studied, including substantial work on temporal/dynamic networks, and continual/online learning has also received significant attention, the particular setting addressed in the paper is an interesting special case of the problem.  The proposed method has not been considered before, but it is not clear that broader insights arise from, or underpin, its development.  

Quality: Although intuitively appealing, the proposed model design is somewhat heuristic in nature.  It is unclear what fundamental principles motivate the use of prototypes or the various algorithmic choices which were made.  At least, the theoretical results in Section 2.5 show that the proposed method avoids certain pitfalls, but they do not otherwise guarantee how close to optimal the predictions are (e.g., in terms of regret).  The experimental results are a strength of the work, including good results in a systematic cross-validation-with-hyperparameter-tuning experiment compared to a number of baselines on multiple datasets, an ablation study, memory consumption, and t-SNE visualizations.

Clarity: The paper can be understood, with some effort.  There are relatively few typos or grammatical issues.  Figure 1, explaining the method, is helpful in understanding the approach.

Significance: The strong experimental results suggest that the proposed method is an improvement over the current state of the art at this particular representation learning problem, which may have practical importance.  However, the heuristic nature of the approach does not suggest that the ideas here will have influence beyond the narrow task, or will stand the test of time.

------

Thanks to the authors for their detailed and well argued rebuttal. The response was helpful for understanding how the authors perceive the points I've raised.   I have no problem with the hierarchical prototype approach proposed here, but I don't really see an overarching objective or motivating principle that spans both the matching prototypes phase and the learning phase, each of which seems to have unrelated objectives.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Graph representation learning can be subject to algorithmic bias/fairness issues.  It would be worth mentioning how this might be addressed in future work.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

However, the heuristic nature of the approach does not suggest that the ideas here will have influence beyond the narrow task, or will stand the test of time.

------

Thanks to the authors for their detailed and well argued rebuttal. The response was helpful for understanding how the authors perceive the points I've raised.   I have no problem with the hierarchical prototype approach proposed here, but I don't really see an overarching objective or motivating principle that spans both the matching prototypes phase and the learning phase, each of which seems to have unrelated objectives.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Graph representation learning can be subject to algorithmic bias/fairness issues.  It would be worth mentioning how this might be addressed in future work.

Rating:
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review: Hierarchical Prototype Networks for Continual Graph Representation Learning

## Summary

This paper addresses graph representation learning in a particular online/continual/streaming setting in which subgraphs are sequentially received which may contain nodes of categories/classes that haven't been seen before, while performance on the previously seen categories/classes must be maintained. The approach involves extracting "atomic" features based on node features and local network structure through Atomic Feature Extractors (AFEs), then constructing and maintaining prototype embeddings at various levels of abstraction (atomic-level, node-level, and class-level), in order to produce a final embedding to be used by a node classifier. In each iteration the method decides whether to introduce new prototypes based on matching to previous prototypes using cosine similarity thresholds. The authors prove a bound on the number of prototypes (hence, memory requirements) and show that the method will avoid catastrophic forgetting under certain conditions. Experimental results versus various baselines are positive, demonstrating competitive or superior performance across eight different datasets while maintaining significantly lower memory footprints.

## Main Review

### Originality

While graph representation learning has been heavily studied, including substantial work on temporal/dynamic networks, and continual/online learning has also received significant attention, the particular setting addressed in the paper is an interesting special case of the problem. The paper makes a clear distinction from dynamic graph learning methods by focusing on class-incremental learning scenarios where new node categories emerge sequentially, rather than on time-dependent changes to edges or node features. The proposed method combining hierarchical prototypes with atomic feature extractors has not been considered before in this specific context, and the application to continual graph representation learning represents a novel contribution to the field.

However, it is not entirely clear that broader insights arise from, or underpin, the development of this approach. While the paper addresses a specific and practically motivated problem, the generalizability of the core ideas to other continual learning scenarios or graph-based tasks remains unclear. The hierarchical prototype framework, while novel in its application to graphs, draws conceptually from existing prototype-based learning literature, and the atomic feature extraction mechanism, though adapted for graphs, represents a relatively straightforward extension of existing feature decomposition techniques. The paper would be strengthened by a more thorough discussion of how the insights from this work might transfer to related problems in continual learning or graph neural networks more broadly.

### Quality

Although intuitively appealing, the proposed model design is somewhat heuristic in nature. The method involves multiple design choices—such as the specific use of cosine similarity for prototype matching (Equations 5-6), the threshold-based filtering mechanism (Equations 7, 9), and the particular three-level hierarchy of prototypes—whose motivation is primarily empirical rather than principled. It is unclear what fundamental principles motivate the use of prototypes specifically, or why this particular hierarchical decomposition (atomic-level, node-level, class-level) is optimal for the continual learning setting. The analogy drawn to "feature maps of convolutional neural networks at different layers" (page 3) provides some intuition but does not constitute a rigorous justification.

Furthermore, the various algorithmic choices warrant deeper investigation. For instance, why is the divergence loss (Equation 4) the appropriate regularizer for ensuring that AFEs produce uncorrelated features? The paper states that AFEs should "be uncorrelated with each other and thus can map features to different subspaces," but alternative divergence measures or orthogonality constraints are not considered or compared. Similarly, the uniform sampling of fixed-size neighborhoods for computing atomic structure embeddings (Section 2.2) is presented without justification or ablation studies comparing this to importance-weighted or adaptive sampling strategies.

At least, the theoretical results in Section 2.5 demonstrate that the proposed method avoids certain pitfalls—specifically, Theorem 1 provides an upper bound on memory consumption, and Theorem 2 establishes conditions under which catastrophic forgetting can be avoided. However, these results are somewhat limited in scope. Theorem 1 only bounds the number of prototypes without providing guarantees on the quality of the learned representations or the expressiveness of the resulting model. Theorem 2 provides sufficient conditions for avoiding catastrophic forgetting (specifically, that the number and dimension of AFEs must be large enough relative to the task distance), but this condition is quite loose and may not be tight in practice, as the authors acknowledge. Importantly, the theoretical results do not otherwise guarantee how close to optimal the predictions are in terms of standard continual learning metrics such as regret, sample complexity, or convergence rates. The paper would benefit from tighter theoretical analysis or empirical validation of how the bounds relate to actual model performance.

The experimental results are a strength of the work. The paper includes systematic comparisons against multiple baseline methods across eight different datasets, with proper error bars and statistical reporting (Table 1). The inclusion of both continual learning-specific baselines (ERGNN, TWP) and adapted classical continual learning methods (EWC, LwF, GEM, MAS) provides a comprehensive evaluation landscape. The ablation studies (Tables 2-3) demonstrate the contribution of different components, showing that each level of prototype (A-prototypes, N-prototypes, C-prototypes) contributes meaningfully to performance, and that all three loss terms (Lcls, Ldiv, Ldis) are beneficial. The memory consumption analysis (Table 4, Figure 3) is particularly compelling, showing that HPNs achieves comparable or superior accuracy to baselines while using orders of magnitude fewer parameters—for example, 4,908 parameters on OGB-Products with 2.4 million nodes versus thousands to millions for baselines. The visualization of learned representations through t-SNE (Figure 4) provides useful intuition about how the method maintains separation between classes as new tasks arrive. However, the paper would be strengthened by additional ablation studies examining alternative design choices, such as different similarity metrics or prototype matching strategies.

### Clarity

The paper can be understood with some effort. The writing is generally clear, with relatively few typos or grammatical issues. The mathematical notation is mostly consistent, though the paper becomes dense in places, particularly in Section 2.3 where multiple levels of prototype matching and selection are described sequentially. Figure 1 is helpful in understanding the overall approach, though it could benefit from more detailed annotations explaining the flow of information and the role of each component. The distinction between AFEnode and AFEstruct is well-motivated conceptually (encoding node attributes versus structural relationships), and the three-level hierarchy of prototypes is clearly explained.

However, some aspects of the presentation could be improved. The problem statement in Section 2.1 could more explicitly define what "performance over existing nodes is uninterrupted" means formally—while the intuition is clear, a precise mathematical definition would strengthen the exposition. The matching process for prototypes (Equations 5-10) involves multiple steps with different thresholds and filtering criteria, and the interplay between these steps could be explained more clearly. Algorithm 1 is quite high-level and does not clearly show how the loss function L is computed from the three components (Ldis, Ldiv, Lcls) or how the prototype matching and refinement steps fit into the overall training loop. Additionally, the paper would benefit from more discussion of computational complexity—while memory consumption is thoroughly analyzed, the time complexity of the prototype matching and selection process is not discussed.

### Significance

The strong experimental results suggest that the proposed method is an improvement over the current state of the art at this particular representation learning problem, which may have practical importance. The method achieves competitive accuracy with dramatically reduced memory footprint, which is valuable for applications involving large graphs and many sequential tasks. The consistent improvements across multiple datasets (Cora, Citeseer, OGB-Arxiv, OGB-Products) and backbone architectures (GCN, GAT, GIN) suggest that the approach is reasonably robust. The introduction of the Average Retaining Score (ARS) metric (Section 3.5) provides a useful new way to measure catastrophic forgetting that isolates the effect of forgetting from task differences, and this contribution may be of independent interest to the continual learning community.

However, the heuristic nature of the approach does not suggest that the ideas here will have influence beyond the narrow task of continual node classification on graph data, or will stand the test of time. The specific combination of design choices—atomic feature extractors, three-level prototype hierarchies, cosine similarity matching with thresholds—appears to be tailored to this particular problem setting. It is unclear whether the core principles would generalize to other continual learning scenarios, other graph learning tasks (e.g., link prediction, graph classification), or to non-Euclidean data more broadly. The paper does not provide sufficient insight into when and why this approach would be preferable to alternatives, or what design principles should guide practitioners in applying similar ideas to new problems. Furthermore, the connection to the broader literature on prototype-based learning, metric learning, and continual learning could be strengthened to position this work within a larger context of research directions.

### Limitations and Societal Impact

The paper briefly addresses limitations in the conclusion and appendix, noting that future work could extend HPNs to tasks beyond node classification (link prediction, multi-label classification

reviews.summary: The paper addresses the continuous representation learning problem for graph data. The main goal is to learn emerging novel node categories, and simultaneously maintain the learned knowledge over existing graphs. In the proposed solution called Hierarchical Prototype Networks (HPNs), several levels of general knowledge are extracted, in the form of atomic, node and class-level prototypes. reviews.main_review:
Strong points

- The paper addresses an important, well motivated problem in graph representation learning with practical significance. 

- The presentation is largely clearly. 

- Experiments are fairly comprehensive, including results on large-scale graphs.

Weak points

- There is some similarity to few-shot learning on graphs [a,b], although they mostly focus on novel class only without considering how the performance of existing classes are maintained. Nevertheless, discussion and comparison with few-shot methods should be included, to highlight the advantage of HPN over few-shot methods.

[a] Meta-GNN: On Few-shot Node Classification in Graph Meta-learning. CIKM19

[b] Relative and Absolute Location Embedding for Few-Shot Node Classification on Graph. AAAI21.

- Hierarchical graph representation learning has also been explored [c,d], which is more flexible/self-adaptive than the 3-level prototypes in this paper.  

[c] Hierarchical graph representation learning with differentiable pooling. In NIPS 2018

[d] Graph Few-shot Learning via Knowledge Transfer. AAAI20.

- The model involves a number of hyperparameters related to the thresholds/number of candidates, including l_a, l_r, l'_a, l'_r, t_A, t_N, t_C. This would make model tuning slow and impractical. It is also possible that, as novel classes emerge, these hyperparameters may not to change as well for optimal performance.

- Message passing GNN is considered SOTA on graph data. The proposed solution derives representations from multi-hop neighbors, lacking the recursively defined message passing mechanism. A discussion on this is needed to demonstrate the design advantages and disadvantages of HPN.

-- update after rebuttal --

Based on the rebuttal, i have increased my rating slightly.

reviews.text:
# Expanded Peer Review

## Summary

The paper addresses the continuous representation learning problem for graph data, focusing on scenarios where novel node categories and their associated edges emerge over time. The main contribution is Hierarchical Prototype Networks (HPNs), a framework designed to learn representations for emerging node categories while maintaining performance on existing categories. The approach extracts knowledge at multiple levels—atomic, node, and class-level prototypes—to enable incremental learning without catastrophic forgetting. The authors provide theoretical analysis on memory consumption bounds and continual learning capability, supported by empirical evaluation on eight public datasets, including large-scale graphs with millions of nodes such as OGB-Products.

## Main Review

### Strong Points

- **Well-motivated and important problem**: The paper tackles a practically significant challenge in graph representation learning. The motivation is compelling: real-world applications such as citation networks (with papers in new research areas) and co-purchasing networks (with new product categories) naturally exhibit the class-incremental scenario studied here. This distinction from static graph learning and dynamic graphs (where nodes/edges change over time but categories remain fixed) is clearly articulated and well-justified.

- **Largely clear presentation**: The paper is generally well-written with a clear problem formulation. The hierarchical prototype framework is intuitive, and Figure 1 effectively illustrates the overall architecture. The progression from Atomic Feature Extractors (AFEs) through three levels of prototypes is logically presented, making the approach accessible to readers.

- **Fairly comprehensive experimental evaluation**: The experiments span eight datasets of varying scales, from small citation networks (Cora, Citeseer) to very large-scale graphs (OGB-Products with 2.4M nodes). The evaluation includes multiple metrics (AM and FM), comparisons with diverse baseline methods across different GNN backbones (GCN, GAT, GIN), and ablation studies examining the contribution of different components. The introduction of the Average Retaining Score (ARS) metric to measure how well previous task performance is maintained is a thoughtful addition to standard continual learning metrics.

- **Theoretical contributions**: The paper provides formal analysis with Theorem 1 establishing upper bounds on the number of prototypes and Theorem 2 providing conditions for avoiding catastrophic forgetting. These theoretical results help explain the empirical behavior and provide guidance for hyperparameter selection, particularly regarding the trade-off between the number of AFEs and prototype dimensions.

- **Memory efficiency**: The demonstrated memory consumption is notably lower than baselines, with HPNs using only thousands of parameters on OGB-Products (Table 4) compared to hundreds of thousands or millions for other methods. This practical advantage is significant for deployment scenarios.

### Weak Points

- **Limited engagement with few-shot learning literature**: The paper addresses a problem with considerable overlap to few-shot learning on graphs, yet provides minimal discussion of or comparison with this related work. References [a] Meta-GNN (CIKM19) and [b] on few-shot node classification both tackle novel class learning on graphs. While the present work explicitly considers maintaining performance on existing classes (which few-shot methods may not prioritize), this distinction should be clearly articulated. A direct empirical comparison or detailed discussion explaining why few-shot methods are unsuitable would strengthen the positioning. The relationship between the class-incremental setting studied here and the few-shot regime deserves explicit treatment, particularly regarding sample complexity and how the two paradigms differ in their assumptions and objectives.

- **Insufficient comparison with hierarchical graph representation learning methods**: The paper proposes a three-level hierarchical prototype scheme but does not compare with existing hierarchical graph learning approaches such as [c] differentiable pooling (NIPS 2018) and [d] graph few-shot learning via knowledge transfer (AAAI20). These methods offer more flexible, self-adaptive hierarchical structures that can potentially adjust hierarchy depth based on data. A discussion of why fixed three-level prototypes are preferred over adaptive hierarchical approaches would be valuable. Additionally, the paper could benefit from analyzing whether the three-level hierarchy is optimal or if the choice is primarily for interpretability and computational efficiency.

- **Hyperparameter complexity and practical tuning challenges**: The model involves a substantial number of hyperparameters: l_a and l_r (number of AFEs), l'_a and l'_r (number of selected AFEs), t_A, t_N, and t_C (similarity thresholds), plus da, dn, dc (prototype dimensions), and h (number of hops). While Section 3.6 provides sensitivity analysis for some parameters (Figure 2 shows stability with respect to threshold variations), the combined effect of tuning multiple hyperparameters simultaneously is not thoroughly explored. The paper uses cross-validation on thresholds from {0.01, 0.05, ..., 0.4}, but the computational cost and practical feasibility of this tuning procedure on large graphs is not discussed. More critically, as new classes emerge in a continual learning setting, it remains unclear whether these hyperparameters require re-tuning or can remain fixed throughout the task sequence. This is a significant practical concern for real-world deployment where retuning after each task may be infeasible.

- **Limited discussion of message-passing GNN design**: State-of-the-art graph neural networks, particularly those based on message-passing frameworks (e.g., GraphSAGE, GAT), employ recursively defined message-passing mechanisms that iteratively aggregate information from neighbors. The proposed HPN approach derives representations from multi-hop neighbors through linear transformations (Equations 1-2) but lacks the recursive refinement characteristic of message-passing GNNs. While this design choice may offer computational advantages and interpretability benefits (explicit prototype-based reasoning), the paper does not discuss the trade-offs. A detailed comparison of the representation expressiveness between the linear atomic feature approach and recursive message-passing would be instructive. For instance, does the lack of non-linear message passing limit HPNs' ability to capture complex structural patterns that message-passing GNNs can learn?

- **Prototype matching mechanism and threshold sensitivity**: The core mechanism for matching atomic embeddings to prototypes (Equations 6-9) relies on cosine similarity thresholds. While Figure 2(c-d) shows reasonable stability across threshold values, the absolute performance differences are non-trivial (e.g., on Cora, AM ranges from ~91% to ~94% as t_A varies). The paper does not provide principled guidance for setting these thresholds a priori, beyond cross-validation. Additionally, the filtering step in Equation 9 uses the same threshold t_A for both matching to existing prototypes and filtering redundancy in new prototypes—whether these should use different thresholds is not explored.

- **Limited novelty in continual learning mechanisms**: While the application to graphs is novel, the core continual learning strategies employed (prototype-based learning, selective parameter activation) are not fundamentally new. The main novelty lies in the specific instantiation for graphs and the hierarchical prototype composition. The paper would benefit from clearer articulation of what aspects are genuinely novel versus adaptations of existing continual learning principles.

### Technical Concerns

- **Divergence loss design**: The divergence loss in Equation 4 encourages uncorrelated AFEs by penalizing Gram matrix products. However, enforcing orthogonality through this loss alone may be insufficient, and the paper does not discuss whether additional regularization or orthogonalization procedures are employed during optimization. The relative weight of L_div compared to L_dis and L_cls in the combined loss (Equation 13) is not specified, raising questions about whether the divergence constraint is adequately enforced.

- **Theoretical bounds tightness**: Theorem 1 provides upper bounds on prototype counts, but the paper acknowledges these are not tight (the bounds depend on spherical codes S(n, N, t) which grow rapidly with dimension). The experimental results in Figure 3(right) show actual memory consumption significantly below the theoretical bound, suggesting the bound may be quite loose. A tighter analysis or empirical characterization of the typical number of prototypes would be more informative.

- **Task distance preserving conditions**: Theorem 2's condition l_a·d_a + l_r·d_r ≥ (l_r + 1)·d_v appears restrictive. The paper does not discuss how often this condition is satisfied in practice or provide empirical verification on the experimental datasets. Additionally, the bound involves λ_min(W^T W) where W is constructed from AFEs, but the paper provides limited guidance on how to ensure this eigenvalue is sufficiently large.

### Missing Experimental Analysis

- **Cross-dataset generalization**: All experiments train and test on the same dataset with class-incremental splits. It would be valuable to evaluate whether prototypes learned on one graph transfer to different graphs (e.g., training on Cora and testing on Citeseer), which would test the generality of learned atomic features.

- **Sensitivity to task ordering**: The class-incremental scheme assumes a fixed task order. The paper does not investigate whether HPNs are sensitive to the order in which new classes are introduced, which is important for understanding robustness.

- **Comparison

title: RotoGrad: Gradient Homogenization in Multi-Task Learning

reviews.summary: The paper proposes a method to reduce the optimization difficulty for multi-task learning. The key idea is to homogenize task gradients in terms of both magnitudes and directions. Specifically, they rescale task gradients to maintain similar convergence rates over tasks. At the same time, they introduce new network parameter R_k for each task k and train it so that the angle between task-specific and average gradients are minimized when task-head parameters are rotated using R_k. The method improves baselines and outperforms existing methods on DIGITS, CIFAR10, NYUv2, and CelebA datasets.
reviews.main_review:
I think the key contribution of this paper is to introduce rotation matrices that reduces gradient conflicts by rotating the feature-space z in a way that matches their directions. In particular, the idea to use rotation matrices is reasonable and good in that they will preserve the relational information (e.g. distance) between features.

They claim that the novelty lies on the joint homogenization of gradients in terms of both magnitude and direction. However, IMTL [18] also jointly addressed loss magnitudes and gradient direction. Rather, I think novelty lies on the way they formulate the problem as a Stackelberg game. In other words, they optimize rotation matrices only to match gradient directions while optimizing the remaining network parameters to minimize the task loss. GradNorm used a similar strategy but they focused on adjusting gradient magnitudes. 

In the experiments, their method improves baselines and performs better than other methods on four datasets and for two different backbone architectures. Additional analysis on training stability, effect of sub-dimension size for rotation, and training time comparison provides more comprehensive views.
However, it seems one needs to carefully adjust the training parameters for the rotation matrix, depending on dataset. when I ran the submitted code for mnist, the median cosine similarity for the task 'density' oscillated largely.


I think L130-131 is a bit misleading, because rotating the feature z is different from rotating its gradient g. Rotating z could result in the change of magnitudes in gradients. For example, consider a function f(x,y) = [relu(x),0]. A vector [x,y]=[1,0] and its rotation [x,y] = [-1,0] have different gradient magnitude.

It would be good to add ablation study to analyze the effect of magnitude and direction separately, i.e., magnitude-only and direction-only. Since each of them was explored in existing work, comparing each to corresponding other approaches will be also informative, e.g., magnitude-only to GradNorm and direction-only to PCGrad and GradDrop.

One way to demonstrate the training behavior will be to visualize the actual reduced gradient conflicts, possibly using angles, compared to the baseline. Also, comparing the method with baselines in the task loss curve will show that the method results in lower task loss.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.
